title: The Professional Project Manager
intro: This module explains what it means to be a "professional" project manager. You will learn that good project management is not one fixed recipe. It is about choosing the right tools for your own project — not too many, not too few. We will look at whether professionalism guarantees success, what happens when project management is done badly, how to weigh costs against benefits, and the famous 80:20 rule. The ideas come from Chapter 1 of "The Professional Project Manager" by Sean Whitaker. By the end, you will understand how to make smart, balanced choices that raise your chances of success.

steps.title: What 'professional' really means
steps.body:
In this book, the word "professional" is chosen on purpose. It describes the way of managing projects that has the best chance of success. But being professional is not only about skill and experience. It is also about matching the way you work to the project in front of you.

Every project is different. The industry, the type, the size, and the complexity all matter. A method that works well for a huge building project may be wrong for a small website. So the professional project manager asks a simple question: what is right for this project, right now?

That is the heart of the whole idea. Professional project management means using the tools, techniques, and processes that are practical and perfect for your project. (A "tool" or "technique" is just a way of doing part of the work, like a plan or a checklist. A "process" is a set of steps you follow.)

So do not think of professionalism as a fixed rulebook. Think of it as making good, thoughtful choices that fit your situation.
steps.example: A NASA space project and a small landscaping business both use project management. But the NASA team needs far more detailed processes. The small business would drown if it copied NASA's approach.
keyPoints: "Professional" means the approach with the best chance of success.
The right approach depends on the industry, type, size, and complexity of the project.
Choose tools that are practical and perfect for your project, right now.

steps.title: Project management is not one-size-fits-all
steps.body:
Many project managers make the same mistake. They take a ready-made method off the shelf, add someone else's tools and processes, and apply the whole mixture to their own project. Then they are surprised when it does not work.

The reason is simple. Project management is not a one-size-fits-all solution. Each project is different. Each organization is different. Each project manager and each team is different too. What works beautifully for one project may fail on another.

So you cannot just copy a method and expect success. You have to look at your own project and pick what fits. It is not only the technical result that decides the best approach. The personalities of the manager, the team, the client, and other stakeholders also matter. ("Stakeholders" are the people who care about or are affected by the project.) The environment matters too — time, risk, money, and quality.

Because of all these factors, two projects that deliver almost the same product can still need very different, yet fully professional, approaches. There is no single correct recipe.
steps.example: Two teams build very similar mobile apps. One team has nervous stakeholders and a tight budget, so it uses more careful controls. The other has a relaxed client and more time, so it works more lightly. Both are professional.
keyPoints: Copying a ready-made method rarely works for your unique project.
People, personalities, and the environment shape the right approach.
Two similar products can need very different professional approaches.

steps.title: The balancing act: too much vs too little
steps.body:
Being professional is a balancing act. You must find the middle point between doing too much and doing too little.

Some managers add as many tools, techniques, and processes as they can, without thinking about whether they help. This is called applying tools "indiscriminately" — meaning without care or judgement. It slows the project down and adds work that brings no value. These managers give project management a bad name.

Other managers go the opposite way. They do not use enough good practices. They prefer an "ad-hoc" style — making it up as they go along. This is risky, and it also gives the profession a bad name.

The author often uses the word "appropriately" to describe the right balance. Too many processes, and you get stuck in extra work that adds no value. Too few, and your project can spiral out of control. The skill is to choose just the right amount for your project.

Think of cooking. You can overcook a meal or undercook it. Neither is good. The goal is to get it just right.
steps.example: A manager on a small two-week task insists on ten formal reports, three sign-off meetings, and a risk register. The extra paperwork takes longer than the task itself. That is "overcooking" the project.
keyPoints: Too many tools slow you down and add no value.
Too few tools let the project spiral out of control.
Professionalism is finding the right, appropriate balance.

steps.title: Quiz: finding the balance
steps.body: Let's check the idea of the balancing act.

quiz.question: According to the chapter, which project manager is being professional?
options: One who applies as many tools and processes as possible, to be safe
One who chooses just the right amount of tools for that project
One who makes everything up as they go, with no set practices
quiz.explanation: Professionalism is a balance — choosing the appropriate amount of tools, not too many and not too few.

steps.title: The most flexible profession
steps.body:
Project management is different from many other professions, and this is important to understand.

Think about an accountant. There are only a few accepted ways to prepare a profit and loss statement or a balance sheet. Clear accounting rules govern the work. Now think about an engineer. There are accepted engineering solutions that must be followed, because lives can be at stake. An engineer may choose between a few options, but must still follow expected standards.

Project management is not like that. Of all the professions, it is the most adaptable, customizable, and flexible in how best practice is applied. ("Best practice" means the proven, sensible ways of doing something.)

This flexibility is both good news and a challenge. The good news is that you can shape your approach to fit your project. The challenge is that no one hands you a fixed list of exactly what to do. You have to use judgement.

This is why so many project management books can feel unclear. They give lots of information but do not tell you which tools to use and which to skip. The professional manager learns to make those choices wisely.
steps.example: An accountant must follow set rules to build a balance sheet. But a project manager can choose to run daily stand-up meetings, or weekly ones, or none — whatever suits the team best.
keyPoints: Accounting and engineering have stricter, fixed rules.
Project management is the most flexible and adaptable profession.
Flexibility is powerful, but it means you must use good judgement.

steps.title: Teach it back: why 'professional'?
steps.body:
Imagine a friend is starting their first project. They ask you: "Why does the author keep using the word professional? Isn't good project management just following a standard method?"

In your own simple words, explain to your friend what "professional project management" means. Try to include these ideas: that it is not one-size-fits-all, that it means choosing the right tools for this project, and that it is a balance between too much and too little. Keep it short and friendly, as if you were talking over coffee.
keyPoints: Explain that each project is different.
Explain the idea of choosing appropriate tools.
Mention the balance between too much and too little.

steps.title: Can you guarantee project success?
steps.body:
Here is an honest and important question. Does using all the right processes, tools, and techniques guarantee that your project will succeed? Does having the best training and credentials mean you cannot fail?

The short answer is no.

Managing projects well is not about promising success. It is about increasing the chances of success and reducing the chances of failure. You can be the most experienced manager in the world, use the perfect method, and check progress all the time — and the project can still fail because of events you cannot control.

The opposite can also happen. Sometimes a person with no sense of best practice, who makes things up as they go, somehow delivers a project against all odds. When this happens, they often boast about it and make it seem normal. But it is not normal.

Why bother being professional, then? Because professional, experienced managers have a far greater chance of success. And unprofessional ones have a far greater chance of failure. You cannot promise a win, but you can strongly tilt the odds in your favour.
steps.example: A skilled manager runs a perfect plan, but a sudden change in law cancels the whole project. That failure was outside their control — it does not mean they were unprofessional.
keyPoints: No method or credential can guarantee success.
Good management raises the chances of success and lowers the chances of failure.
Rare lucky successes by unprofessional managers are not the norm.

steps.title: Tips to increase your chances of success
steps.body:
Since you cannot guarantee success, the author gives practical tips to increase your chances. These are simple and worth remembering.

First, define exactly what "success" means for your project. Is it only time and cost? Or does it also include health and safety, customer satisfaction, environmental impact, and reputation? Agree on the measures — called "metrics" — before you start.

Second, be honest with your stakeholders. Let them know you cannot guarantee success. Do not set unreasonable expectations. But do not paint too dark a picture either. Give a realistic view of the chances.

Third, get the experience you need. This can come from doing the work, and also from a mentor — a more experienced person who guides you.

Fourth, rely on the experience of others, often gathered in a professional body of knowledge, such as the PMBOK Guide.

Fifth, get the training and, if useful, the credentials that suit your type of project. Formal training helps your experience grow faster.

And finally, when you do succeed, make sure everyone knows about it.
steps.example: Before starting, a manager writes down that "success" means finishing within budget AND with zero safety accidents AND happy customers — not just meeting the deadline.
keyPoints: Define your success metrics before you begin.
Be honest with stakeholders — no false promises, no gloom.
Grow through experience, mentoring, shared knowledge, and training.

steps.title: Quiz: the honest truth about success
steps.body: Let's check the key message about guarantees.

quiz.question: What does professional project management actually promise?
options: A guaranteed successful project every time
That you will never make a mistake
A greater chance of success and a lower chance of failure
Success only if you have the highest credentials
quiz.explanation: You cannot guarantee success; professionalism increases the chances of success and reduces the chances of failure.

steps.title: Appropriately applied project management
steps.body:
The author's favourite word is "appropriately." It is one of the keys to success. Let's break down the phrase "appropriately applied professional project management."

"Appropriately applied" means you do not overcook or undercook the project. First, you choose from the wide range of available methods, processes, templates, and tools the ones that fit your organization — in size, dollar value, and complexity. Then, once you have the best ones, you also apply them in the right way at the right time. Use your discretion, keep things flexible, but stay appropriate.

Remember: the level of project management used in a NASA space project is very different from the level used in a small landscaping or software business. Yet both need project management to raise their chances of success.

So "appropriately applied" has two parts. First, pick the right tools for your project. Second, use them at the right moment and in the right amount. It is not enough to own the right tool — you must also use it wisely.
steps.example: A small software team picks a simple task board and short weekly check-ins — appropriate for its size. It does not copy NASA's heavy documentation, which would be far too much.
keyPoints: "Appropriately applied" means not too much and not too little.
First choose tools that fit your organization's size, value, and complexity.
Then apply them at the right time and in the right amount.

steps.title: 'Professional' and the PMBOK Guide
steps.body:
Now let's look at the word "professional" more closely. Technically, being professional means being connected to a recognized profession. But in this book, it means something richer. It means a body of practice that knows what actually works and is committed to always improving it. Around this core knowledge sit institutions, credentials, and ways to tell the good from the bad.

The world's largest professional body for project management is the Project Management Institute, or PMI. The tools and processes in this book are based on their guide, called the PMBOK Guide. (PMBOK stands for the "Project Management Body of Knowledge.") Using proven, supported best practice like this raises your chances of success.

But here is a key point. The PMBOK Guide is not a methodology. It is not a strict, step-by-step way of doing projects. Instead, it is a large collection of best practice. You are encouraged to choose from it the processes, tools, and techniques that suit your own projects.

So the guide is like a big toolbox, not a fixed recipe. You still decide what to pick.
steps.example: The PMBOK Guide lists many possible techniques for planning. A manager reads it and selects only the few that fit their small project, leaving the rest in the "toolbox."
keyPoints: "Professional" means proven practice plus a commitment to improve it.
PMI is the largest body; the PMBOK Guide holds its best practice.
The PMBOK Guide is a toolbox to choose from, not a fixed method.

steps.title: The four mandatory elements
steps.body:
The author makes one small but firm exception to all this flexibility. Some elements are essential and mandatory for any business or project, no matter how small.

These are: scope definition, cost estimating, time estimating, and change control. Let's explain them simply. "Scope definition" means clearly deciding what the project will and will not include. "Cost estimating" means working out how much it will cost. "Time estimating" means working out how long it will take. "Change control" means having a clear way to handle changes when someone wants to add or alter something.

Why are these mandatory? Because, in the author's experience, they make the greatest contribution to project success. And when they are done poorly, they almost always lead to failure.

You do not need to finish all of this work before you start. Often that is impossible. But you will need these things at some point, and the earlier the better. For example, you might define the scope first, then estimate time and costs step by step, in an "iterative" way — meaning you repeat and refine as the project grows.
steps.example: A team defines the scope of a new website up front. Then, as design choices become clearer, they refine their time and cost estimates each week rather than guessing everything on day one.
keyPoints: Four elements are mandatory: scope definition, cost estimating, time estimating, change control.
They contribute most to success and, done badly, cause failure.
You can define scope first and estimate cost and time step by step.

steps.title: Build: your appropriate toolkit
steps.body:
Time to practise choosing appropriately. Imagine you are managing a small, three-week project to plan a company team lunch for 30 people. The budget is small and the client is relaxed.

Your task: write a short plan (5–8 lines) that shows appropriate, professional choices. Include these parts:
1. Scope definition — write one or two sentences saying what the lunch project will include and what it will NOT include.
2. A rough cost estimate and time estimate — even a simple guess is fine.
3. One simple change-control rule — for example, "any new request after week one must be approved by me."
4. One tool or process you decided NOT to use, and one sentence saying why it would be "too much" for a project this small.

The goal is to show that you can cover the four mandatory elements while keeping everything light and appropriate for a tiny project.
keyPoints: Cover the four mandatory elements, even in a tiny project.
Keep the plan light and appropriate to the small size.
Show one tool you dropped, and explain why it would be too much.

steps.title: Consequences of your choices
steps.body:
Being professional means choosing only the tools that are right for your project. But choosing some tools means leaving others behind. This has consequences, and you need to be aware of them.

Most consequences will be good. You keep only the parts of project management that add real value, so your project runs more smoothly. But there can also be consequences from not adopting certain useful practices. This is where experience helps. Sadly, experience only comes with time and often with many mistakes. So stay adaptive, and revisit your decisions regularly — what you chose to use, and what you chose to skip.

There are also serious consequences from the opposite mistake: applying tools indiscriminately, with little thought about whether they fit. These consequences include inefficiencies, confusion about roles and scope, unnecessary bureaucracy (too much paperwork and red tape), and a higher chance of failure.

As you grow, you will learn when to apply a more thorough approach and when it is better to keep things light. You will become skilled at judging the impact of your choices, and ready to change your approach when needed.
steps.example: A manager adds heavy approval forms to a tiny project. The result is confusion about who does what, wasted time, and frustrated team members — all consequences of using tools indiscriminately.
keyPoints: Choosing some tools means leaving others behind, with consequences.
Using tools indiscriminately brings confusion, red tape, and higher failure risk.
Revisit your choices often, and adapt as you gain experience.

steps.title: Cost benefit analysis
steps.body:
So how do you actually choose the right tools? The author does not recommend using gut feeling, and does not recommend simply copying others. Instead, he suggests a form of "cost benefit analysis."

A full cost benefit analysis, done by financial experts, looks at all the costs of an option and all the benefits, usually in money, that it is expected to deliver. That full version is useful for deciding whether to start a whole project. But it is too heavy for our simple purpose here.

What we use here is lighter. It just means comparing the costs of a decision against the benefits it brings. When you wonder whether a tool is worth using, think about the time, cost, and energy of putting it in place. Then think about the benefits — in cost, reputation, and efficiency. Only if the benefits outweigh the costs should you use it.

Here are helpful questions: Will it cost less than the benefits it delivers? Will it make us more efficient? Do we have experience using it? Is it easy to learn? Do our people support it? If you answer "yes" to most, it is probably a good choice.
steps.example: A manager considers a new reporting tool. It would take two days to set up (cost) but would save the team an hour every week and impress the client (benefits). The benefits outweigh the cost, so she adopts it.
keyPoints: Do not rely on gut feeling or blind copying.
Compare the time, cost, and energy of a tool against its benefits.
Use it only if the benefits clearly outweigh the costs.

steps.title: Teach it back: weighing costs and benefits
steps.body:
A teammate is unsure whether to add a new project tool. They say: "I found a fancy tracking tool online. Everyone seems to use it, so let's add it too."

Using the cost benefit idea from the chapter, explain in your own words how they should decide instead. Walk them through the thinking: what "costs" should they consider, what "benefits" should they consider, and what the simple rule is for saying yes. You can also mention one or two of the helpful questions, such as "Is it easy to learn?" or "Do our team members support it?" Keep your explanation short, clear, and encouraging.
keyPoints: Explain that copying others is not a good reason on its own.
List example costs (time, energy) and benefits (efficiency, reputation).
State the rule: adopt only if benefits outweigh costs.

steps.title: The 80:20 rule
steps.body:
Here is a powerful idea that helps you choose. In the mid-20th century, a consultant named Joseph Juran used the term "Pareto principle," now often called the 80:20 rule. It was named after Vilfredo Pareto, an economist who noticed that 80 percent of the land in Italy was owned by just 20 percent of the people.

The 80:20 rule is now used in many fields. It describes any situation where a small part of a group causes most of the results. What does this have to do with project management? Quite a lot.

Using the 80:20 rule, we can say that 20 percent of the tools deliver 80 percent of the project results. The other 80 percent of tools only bring about 20 percent of the results. So which tools are in that powerful 20 percent? They are scope definition, cost estimating, time estimating, and change control — the same four mandatory elements.

This is why those four are required on every project. But remember: this does not mean you ignore the other tools. When used appropriately, the other tools still add value and raise your chances of success.
steps.example: A team spends most of its planning effort on scope, cost, time, and change control — the vital 20 percent. These few areas give them 80 percent of their good results.
keyPoints: The 80:20 rule: a small part of the work drives most of the results.
About 20 percent of tools deliver 80 percent of project results.
That vital 20 percent is the four mandatory elements — but do not ignore the rest.

steps.title: Quiz: the 80:20 rule
steps.body: Let's check your understanding of the Pareto idea.

quiz.question: In project management, what does the 80:20 rule suggest?
options: You should use 80 percent of all available tools on every project
About 20 percent of the tools deliver about 80 percent of the results
Projects always take 20 percent longer than planned
Only 20 percent of projects ever succeed
quiz.explanation: The 80:20 rule here means a small share of tools — the vital 20 percent — produces most of the project results.

title: What Project Management Is
intro: Welcome! This module explains what project management really is. You will learn what a professional project manager does, how organizations grow their project skills, the key words used in the field, and the big documents and roles that shape modern project work. We keep the language simple and give clear examples. By the end, you will feel more confident about this profession and your place in it.

steps.title: What Professional Project Management Is
steps.body:
Project management is very old. Many famous buildings from history were really big projects. The pyramids in Egypt, the Great Wall of China, and Stonehenge all had a start, a middle, and an end. They all had a clear goal. So people have managed projects for thousands of years.

But something changed in modern times. Project management became a real profession. This means it is now a set of clear, repeatable practices. People write down what worked and what did not work. They collect this knowledge in one place. Then every project manager can learn from it. This shared knowledge is called a "body of knowledge."

Professional project management has two more parts. First, a promise to keep improving. Second, a focus on being practical, not just theory.

The opposite is "ad-hoc" management. Ad-hoc means doing things with no plan, in a random way. An ad-hoc manager keeps "reinventing the wheel" (solving the same problem again and again) and never learns from mistakes. This is a shame, because professional practices are easy to find today.

Project management is still a young profession. It is younger than medicine or law. So people often use different words for the same thing. That is normal for a growing field.
steps.example: A builder who follows a written checklist for every house, and updates it after each job, is doing professional project management. A builder who guesses each time and repeats old mistakes is doing ad-hoc management.
keyPoints: Projects have a clear start, middle, end, and goal
Professional PM uses shared, written best practice (a body of knowledge)
It includes a promise to keep improving and stay practical
Ad-hoc management means no plan and no learning from mistakes

steps.title: Key Definitions: Project, Programme, Portfolio
steps.body:
Words matter in project management. If two people use the same word to mean different things, they can make mistakes. So let us learn the most important terms. This book lines up its words with the PMBOK Guide, a leading document we will meet later.

A "project" is any work with a beginning, middle, and end. It delivers a unique product, service, or result. It usually has limits on time, cost, and quality. A project is the opposite of "operational work." Operational work is normal, repeating work that never ends, like answering customer calls every day.

A "programme" is a set of projects that are linked in some way. They may share the same people, or work toward the same big goal.

A "portfolio" is all the projects an organization is doing at one time.

A "stakeholder" is any person, group, or organization who can affect the project or be affected by it. This includes the customer, the team, and even neighbors.

A "baseline" is the original approved plan, plus any changes that were approved later. You compare real progress against the baseline. A project can have baselines for scope, time, cost, and quality.
steps.example: Building one new bridge is a project. All the bridge, road, and tunnel projects that share the same engineers form a programme. Every project the whole city government runs this year is the portfolio.
keyPoints: A project is temporary and delivers something unique
Operational work repeats and never ends; it is the opposite of a project
Programme = linked projects; portfolio = all projects at once
A stakeholder is anyone who affects or is affected by the project

steps.title: What a Project Manager Actually Does
steps.body:
There is an easy trick to understand a project manager. Just change the title to "general manager of a project."

A general manager of a company looks after everything: money, staff, communication, marketing, risks, and strategy. A project manager does the same, but for one project. He or she is the complete manager of that project. This is a big job with wide responsibility.

Here is the most important rule. A true project manager has authority that is as high as his or her responsibility. Responsibility means you are answerable for the results. Authority means you have the power to make decisions and get what you need.

Why does this matter? Imagine you are told to deliver a project on time and on budget. That is full responsibility. But you cannot choose your team, control the money, or make key decisions. That is low authority. This is a trap. The author calls it "heading to the guillotine with no way to stop the blade." You will get the blame, but you cannot fix the problem.

So if you have the title "project manager," you should have equal high responsibility and authority. If you do not, the honest choice is to use a smaller job title that matches your real power.
steps.example: A project manager who must deliver a website on time should also have the power to hire the developers, control the budget, and approve changes. Responsibility without that power is a setup for failure.
keyPoints: Think of a project manager as the 'general manager of a project'
They manage every part: money, people, risks, communication, strategy
Authority must be as high as responsibility
Responsibility without authority sets you up for stress and blame

steps.title: The Other Project Roles
steps.body:
"Project manager" is not the only job title in this field. Many roles exist, each with a different level of responsibility and authority. Knowing them helps you see where you fit today.

A "contract manager" often comes from an engineering background. They are strong on technical skills. They lead a project when a contract fully defines the work. But, generally, they are not strong on leadership, communication, or managing stakeholders. Their focus is delivering the product or service in the contract.

A "project coordinator" reports progress to senior managers. They do mid-level project tasks and have some limited authority. Often they are a technical expert doing project work only part time.

A "project administrator" gives administrative support. They can own small parts of a project but have very little authority. This is often an entry-level job for people who want to become project managers.

A "project expeditor" has no authority at all. They often have low-level duties, mostly around reporting.

Where you sit in this list matters less than your commitment to your own growth. Beginners can take courses or the CAPM credential and find a mentor. Mid-level people can gain the PMP credential and start teaching others. Even experts must keep learning, or their skills go stale.
steps.example: If a contract fully describes the work and no strong leadership is needed, a contract manager fits well. For a project needing great communication and leadership, a full project manager is the better choice.
keyPoints: Contract manager: strong technical skills, weaker on leadership
Coordinator and administrator have limited authority; expeditor has none
These are often steps on the path to becoming a project manager
Your commitment to learning matters more than your title

steps.title: Teach Back: Project Manager vs Contract Manager
steps.body:
Time to explain in your own words. Imagine a friend says: "A project manager and a contract manager sound the same to me." How would you explain the difference?

In your answer, try to cover: what a project manager is responsible for (think of the 'general manager' trick), and what a contract manager focuses on instead. Then say when each one is the right choice for a project.

Keep it simple, as if teaching a beginner. Two or three short sentences are enough.
steps.example: You might start: 'A project manager runs every part of a project, like a general manager. A contract manager mostly makes sure a contract is followed...'

steps.title: Organizational Project Management Maturity
steps.body:
People can be beginner, intermediate, or advanced. Organizations are similar. We describe an organization's project skill using "organizational project management maturity," or OPMM. An organization can have a low, medium, or high level.

To measure OPMM, you look at several things. Does the organization ask staff to grow as project managers? Does it use a clear method, with processes, tools, and templates? Is it committed to always improving? There are many tools to measure this, some simple and some complex.

Here is a key idea. Not every organization needs the highest level. The right level depends on the projects. It depends on their size, cost, length, complexity, and industry.

An organization doing very large, long, complex, and expensive projects should aim high. The risk is big, so strong control is worth it. But a small business doing short, cheap, simple projects may be fine at a lower level. Too much process would just slow them down.

So maturity is not about being the best on paper. It is about matching your project methods to the kind of projects you actually do. Once you know your current level, you can decide the level you really need, and plan the gap between them.
steps.example: A hospital building a huge new wing over five years should aim for high maturity. A small print shop doing two-week jobs may be perfectly fine with lower maturity and less paperwork.
keyPoints: OPMM measures an organization's project skill: low, medium, or high
It looks at training, methods, tools, and commitment to improve
The right level depends on project size, cost, length, and complexity
High maturity is not always the goal; match methods to your projects

steps.title: Quiz: Maturity Level
steps.body: Let's check your understanding of maturity.

quiz.question: A small company does short, cheap, simple projects. What level of project management maturity is likely right for them?
options: The highest possible level, because higher is always better
A lower level that matches their small, simple projects
No project management at all
The same high level a huge construction firm needs
quiz.explanation: The right maturity level depends on project size and complexity, so simple projects may suit a lower level.

steps.title: Framework and Methodology: Two Big Words
steps.body:
Two words often confuse people: framework and methodology. Let us make them clear.

A "framework" is a collection of best practice that is not prescriptive. "Not prescriptive" means it does not tell you exactly what to do. Instead, it offers many processes, tools, and techniques. You choose the ones that fit your project's size, industry, and complexity. The PMBOK Guide is the world's leading project management framework. Think of a framework as a big menu of good options.

A "methodology" is different. It is a specific and particular way of doing projects. It comes complete with defined processes, templates, and tools. It tells you the exact steps to follow. Most organizations build their own methodology from a framework like the PMBOK Guide. Others buy a ready-made one. Examples include PRINCE2, Agile, Scrum, Method123, and TenStep.

So the simple difference is this. A framework gives you choices and flexibility. A methodology gives you fixed steps to follow. You start with a framework, then shape a methodology from it for your real work.

Using the same words across your organization helps a lot. When everyone means the same thing by "baseline" or "stakeholder," you make fewer mistakes.
steps.example: A framework is like a cookbook full of many recipes and techniques. A methodology is one exact recipe with fixed steps you follow every time you cook that dish.
keyPoints: Framework = a flexible menu of best practice; you choose what fits
The PMBOK Guide is the leading framework
Methodology = one fixed way with defined steps, templates, and tools
Organizations often build a methodology from a framework

steps.title: Teach Back: Framework vs Methodology
steps.body:
Now explain this pair in your own words. A new team member asks: "Why do we have both a framework and a methodology? Are they not the same?"

In your answer, show the main difference: one is flexible and offers choices, the other gives fixed steps. Try to use one everyday comparison of your own, like a menu, a cookbook, or a map. Then name one real example of each if you can.

Keep it short and friendly, like helping a colleague.
steps.example: You could say: 'A framework is like a menu you pick from. A methodology is the set recipe you follow. PMBOK is a framework; PRINCE2 is a methodology...'

steps.title: Standards, Frameworks, and Methodologies
steps.body:
There are many project management documents. It helps to see them in three layers, from broad to specific.

At the top sits a "standard." The main one is ISO 21500: Guidance on Project Management. It is the international standard. It took seven years to build and involved project organizations around the world. So it gives a very broad, unifying view. When first released it was a "guiding" standard, not a strict "normative" one you must obey.

In the middle are "frameworks" and their credentials. A framework is a body of knowledge. It captures good practice across the whole profession. The largest is the PMBOK Guide from the Project Management Institute (PMI). The Association for Project Management (APM) has its own body of knowledge too. Frameworks hold more detail than standards, but they still do not give one exact way to run a project. PMI offers the PMP and CAPM credentials, which are respected, senior-level qualifications.

At the bottom are "methodologies." These are specific ways to run projects, built from frameworks. Each suits different projects. Scrum fits fast, changing IT work. PRINCE2 suits lower-complexity IT projects. Method123 suits defined, complex projects across industries. Methodology certificates usually need no prerequisites, so they do not prove seniority.
steps.example: Think of a pyramid. ISO 21500 (a standard) is the wide top guide. PMBOK (a framework) sits in the middle with more detail. Scrum or PRINCE2 (methodologies) sit at the bottom as exact ways to work.
keyPoints: Standard (ISO 21500): broad international guidance at the top
Framework (PMBOK, APM BoK): detailed best practice, but not one fixed way
Methodology (Scrum, PRINCE2, Agile): specific steps for specific projects
PMP and CAPM are senior framework credentials worth planning for

steps.title: Quiz: Which Layer?
steps.body: Let's test the three layers.

quiz.question: PRINCE2 and Scrum are best described as which of the following?
options: International standards at the top of the hierarchy
Frameworks like the PMBOK Guide
Methodologies: specific ways to run certain projects
Job titles for project managers
quiz.explanation: PRINCE2 and Scrum are methodologies, the specific ways to run projects at the bottom of the hierarchy.

steps.title: Quiz: Framework Meaning
steps.body: One more on frameworks.

quiz.question: What best describes a framework like the PMBOK Guide?
options: A fixed set of exact steps you must follow on every project
A flexible collection of best practice you choose from to fit your project
A legal contract that defines the scope of work
A single software tool for scheduling tasks
quiz.explanation: A framework is non-prescriptive: it offers best practice options you select to match your project.

steps.title: The PMO: Center of Excellence
steps.body:
PMO stands for Project Management Office. Sometimes it means Program Management Office. Either way, it is the "center of excellence" for project management in an organization. Research shows that organizations with a PMO deliver more successful projects, more often, than those without one.

A PMO is unique to each organization. It reflects the organization's current and future maturity. In a small organization doing rare projects, the PMO might just be a ring binder full of templates that people use.

In a large organization running complex, strategic projects, the PMO does much more. It can be the place where all project staff sit. It can recruit and train project managers. It can develop, apply, and audit the project methodology. It can report on all current projects, spot conflicts between projects, and run the process for selecting which projects to do.

One more important point. A PMO is not fixed forever. Research suggests it often changes its shape about every two years, to match changes in the organization. Whatever form it takes, its purpose stays the same: to support project people and improve the chances of project success.
steps.example: In a tiny firm, the PMO may be one folder of templates. In a big bank, the PMO is a full department that hires project managers, owns the method, and reports on every project.
keyPoints: PMO = the center of excellence for project management
Organizations with a PMO deliver more successful projects
It can be as small as a folder of templates or a full department
The PMO changes shape over time to match the organization

steps.title: Three Types of PMO
steps.body:
A PMO can take different forms. The book describes three broad types. Each type matches a different level of maturity.

The first is a "supportive" PMO. It suits organizations with low maturity. It provides templates, best-practice guides, and similar documents. People running projects use these if they wish. The PMO manager here works only part time. This type helps, but it does not control.

The second is a "directing" PMO. It suits medium maturity. It does everything the supportive PMO does, and more. It also provides common reporting, a standardized methodology, and project governance. Now the method is expected, not just offered. The PMO manager may be part time or full time, depending on the size of the organization.

The third is a "controlling" PMO. It suits high maturity in large organizations with complex projects. It does all the earlier tasks and takes full responsibility for everything to do with project management. The PMO manager works full time.

So the pattern is simple. As maturity rises, the PMO does more and has more control. It moves from offering help, to setting standards, to taking full charge.
steps.example: A supportive PMO says 'here are templates if you want them.' A directing PMO says 'use this method and report this way.' A controlling PMO owns all project management across the whole organization.
keyPoints: Supportive PMO: offers templates and guides; low maturity; part-time manager
Directing PMO: adds common reporting, standard method, governance; medium maturity
Controlling PMO: takes full charge; high maturity; full-time manager
More maturity means the PMO has more control

steps.title: Build: Design a Stealth PMO
steps.body:
Time to apply your learning. Imagine you work in an organization that has no PMO. Senior managers do not yet see its value. The book suggests a "stealth" or "virtual" PMO: a small, low-cost, volunteer version that proves its worth first.

Your task: write a short, simple plan (about 5 to 8 sentences) to start a stealth PMO. Include these three moves from the chapter:

1. Start a project managers' users group. Say when it will meet and one topic it could discuss (for example, standardizing templates or improving change control).
2. Ask the project managers to help write the shared methodology and to review (audit) each other's projects.
3. When projects succeed, give credit to the group so leaders see its value.

Finish by naming one benefit you would show managers to win their support for a bigger PMO later.
steps.example: You might begin: 'We will hold a project managers' breakfast on the fourth Friday each month. Our first topic is standardizing our templates...'

steps.title: Project Sponsorship and Governance
steps.body:
Every project needs two more things: a sponsor and some form of governance. A mature PMO provides both. But even with no PMO, each project still needs them. They increase the chance of success.

The "project sponsor" is a senior person who authorizes the project to go ahead. The sponsor provides financial support. The sponsor also acts as a political champion, helping the project get the backing it needs. Importantly, the sponsor does not manage the project day to day. That is the project manager's job. A bored or weak sponsor can hurt a project, so sponsors should get training.

"Governance" comes from a project steering committee, board, or group. It is made of senior managers and stakeholders. They look at the project from a high level. They check it is meeting its goals. They ask the project manager for regular updates. They assess big changes and make large decisions. But, like the sponsor, they do not run daily operations.

So there are two levels. "Governance" is high-level direction, decisions, and oversight. "Operations" is the daily work of running the project, done by the project manager and team. Everyone should understand this difference. Clear role descriptions and organization charts help keep it clear.
steps.example: The sponsor approves the budget and champions the project to top managers. The steering committee reviews progress and approves major changes. The project manager runs the daily work.
keyPoints: The sponsor authorizes the project, funds it, and champions it politically
The sponsor does not manage daily work; the project manager does
Governance = a steering committee giving high-level oversight and big decisions
Governance is direction; operations is the daily running of the project

steps.title: Quiz: The Sponsor's Role
steps.body: Final check on sponsorship.

quiz.question: Which statement best describes a project sponsor?
options: They manage the daily work of the project team
They authorize and fund the project and champion it, but do not manage it day to day
They have no authority and only handle reporting
They write the code and build the product themselves
quiz.explanation: The sponsor authorizes, funds, and champions the project, while daily management stays with the project manager.

title: Initiating, Planning and Closing
intro: Every project moves through a natural set of stages, from the first idea to the final sign-off. In this module you will learn that sequence: how to choose the right projects, how to justify and approve them, how to plan the work, and how to close a project the right way. We keep the language simple and use clear examples from real projects. By the end, you will understand the project management plan, the project charter, and why writing down every change is one of the most important habits a project manager can build.

steps.title: The natural sequence of a project
steps.body:
Every project follows a natural order of work. There are five main parts: initiating, planning, executing, monitoring and controlling, and closing. Each part has its own focus and happens at a different point in the project's life.

Initiating is about choosing the right projects. You look at all the projects you could do, keep the good ones, drop the weak ones, and get the chosen ones approved and resourced. Planning is where you decide how you will do the work: the scope, cost and time estimates, and your approach to risk, quality, and communication. On small projects these two often overlap and feel like one step.

Executing is where the planned work gets done, and not only building the product; it covers all the other work you promised too. Monitoring and controlling is your checking step: you compare the plan against what is really happening and handle change requests.

Here is a surprise for many people. Closing does not start at the end. You plan for closure right at the beginning, setting the conditions that mean the project is finished. Many people picture a straight line from start to finish, but real projects loop back and repeat parts of the cycle until the work is done.
steps.example: A fast IT team may go through the whole cycle every day. A large construction project may plan and execute in monthly steps. Same five parts, different speed.
keyPoints: The five parts: initiating, planning, executing, monitoring and controlling, closing
You plan how to close a project at the very beginning
The work is a revolving cycle, not a straight line

steps.title: Quick check: the sequence
steps.body: Let us check one key idea about the cycle.

quiz.question: When do you plan how the project will be closed?
options: At the very end, once all the work is done
At the beginning, during planning
Only after the client pays the final invoice
You never need to plan closure
quiz.explanation: The chapter says planning for closure happens at the beginning, where you set the conditions for finishing the project.

steps.title: The project management plan
steps.body:
The project management plan is the main document that guides how you run your project. Like everything in professional project management, it must fit the organization. Small teams doing small projects have small plans. Large teams doing complex projects have large, detailed plans.

The plan is really a collection of smaller plans and documents. It can include a scope management plan, a schedule management plan, a cost management plan, a quality plan, a risk register, a communications plan, a milestone list, reporting templates, and more. One item surprises people: a project closure checklist. Remember, you must plan how to close the project too, not just how to start it.

You can build the plan with any tools. You might use a word processor, a spreadsheet, special project software, or even handwritten templates. The tool does not matter as much as this: the plan must be easy to use and must help your organization succeed.

Here is a useful tip. If people avoid using part of your plan, that part may not be helpful. A truly professional plan is one that people actually use.
steps.example: A small marketing team keeps one folder with blank templates: a scope sheet, a simple budget, a risk list, and a closure checklist. That folder is their project management plan.
keyPoints: The plan is a set of smaller plans and templates
It must fit the size of the organization and project
Include a closure checklist, and keep the plan easy to use

steps.title: Teach it back: the plan
steps.body: Imagine a new colleague asks you: "What is a project management plan, and why does a small project need a smaller one than a big project?" Explain it in your own simple words. Mention at least three things the plan can contain, and explain why it is a bad sign if people avoid using part of the plan.

steps.title: Portfolios, programs, and projects
steps.body:
Organizations usually have many possible projects. How do you pick the right ones? This is the job of portfolio management, and it is very important. The more time you spend choosing the right project at the start, the greater the chance of success at the end.

Let us learn three words that sound similar but mean different things. A portfolio is all the approved projects an organization is doing or planning to do. A program is a group of projects that are linked in some way. They might deliver different parts of one big goal, or they might share the same resources, like the same engineers. A project is a single piece of work.

Portfolio management has two main jobs. The first is choosing the right projects. The second is prioritising them and giving out resources, so the most important projects get the time and people they need.

A program manager has a special job: to manage conflicts and links between the projects in the program, so they all work well together.
steps.example: An organization has eight approved projects in its portfolio. Three of those eight share the same engineering team, so they are grouped together as one program.
keyPoints: Portfolio = all approved projects
Program = a group of linked projects
Portfolio management chooses the right projects and shares resources

steps.title: The selection process and the business case
steps.body:
Before a project joins the approved portfolio, it must pass a careful selection process. This process should be rigorous, repeatable, and fair, so good projects get through and weak ones are rejected. There are a few exceptions, like emergency work or work the law requires. Watch out for "pet projects" that only get approved because someone powerful pushes them. A high number of these end in failure.

The selection process is usually captured in a business case. A business case is a document that helps the organization judge each project in a standard and open way. If a company has no business case, it is probably allowing risky, politically driven projects.

A good business case describes the problem or opportunity, why the project is needed, and how it will solve the issue. It lists all the possible options, including the "do nothing" option. It names the recommended solution, the outputs, the expected outcomes, the timing, the known risks, and the financial or non-financial returns.

If you are handed a project to manage, ask one question: has it been through the business case process? If not, think twice before accepting it.
steps.example: Before building a new app, the team writes a business case: the problem, three possible options (including doing nothing), the recommended option, the risks, and the money it should earn back.
keyPoints: Every project should pass a fair, repeatable selection process
The business case judges projects in a standard, open way
Always include the 'do nothing' option and known risks

steps.title: Quick check: the business case
steps.body: Let us test your understanding of the business case.

quiz.question: What is the main purpose of a business case?
options: To design the final product in detail
To assess the merits of each project in a standard and transparent way
To pay the project's invoices
To close the project at the end
quiz.explanation: The business case gives a robust, standardized, and transparent way to judge the merits of each project before it is approved.

steps.title: Checking strategic alignment
steps.body:
The very first filter in the selection process is strategic alignment. This is a simple but powerful question: does this project help the company reach its strategic goals? Strategic goals are the big, long-term aims of the organization, like growing in a certain market or becoming the best in a service.

If a project does not support these goals, the company should not do it. Two reasons. First, it will not move the organization forward. Second, the company probably does not have the core skills, called core competencies, to do the work well.

What if your organization has no clear written strategy? You can still use this filter in another way. Ask whether you truly have the skills to complete the work, or whether you are only tempted by the chance to make quick money. The chapter is clear: resist that temptation. If you take work outside your skills just for fast cash, you will not have the ability or the motivation to do it well, and the risk of failure goes up.
steps.example: A software company is offered a road-building contract. It pays well, but the company has no road-building skills and it does not match their strategy. The right choice is to say no.
keyPoints: Strategic alignment is the first filter for any project
If it does not support the company's goals, drop it
Do not take work outside your skills just for quick money

steps.title: Financial justification: the simple tools
steps.body:
After strategic alignment, the next filter is money. Financial justification checks whether a project gives a good enough return. This matters for two reasons. First, your organization needs a satisfactory return on the money it invests. Second, remember that the client does not fund the project. Your company pays wages, materials, and other costs until the client pays the first invoices. So you must know how the project affects your own cash.

Here are three simple tools that are easy to set up.

Payback period asks: how long does it take to earn back the money you invested? You pick a time limit. If the project earns the money back inside that limit, it looks good. If it takes longer, it does not.

Profit margin is the profit the company requires on each project. If a project will not reach that margin, the company simply does not do it. Margins differ by industry, so check with your accountant.

Opportunity cost asks: if you do this project, which other projects can you no longer do? If those other projects are worth more, you may want to rethink your choice.
steps.example: You invest 20,000 dollars and set a payback limit of two years. If the project earns 20,000 back within two years, it passes the payback test.
keyPoints: Payback period = how long to earn the money back
Profit margin = the profit level the company requires
Opportunity cost = the value of projects you give up

steps.title: Financial justification: the deeper tools
steps.body:
Three deeper tools give a more thorough view. They usually need a spreadsheet.

Present value (PV) is the value of future money in today's dollars. Money later is worth less than money now. Would you rather have 10,000 dollars today or 12,000 in two years? To decide, you calculate what that future 12,000 is worth today. Using a 10 percent discount rate, 12,000 in two years is worth about 9,917 today, so you would take the 10,000 now.

Net present value (NPV) is the value in today's dollars of all future cash flows over a set period, minus what you spend now. If the NPV is positive, your investment earns at least the return you require, so the project looks worthwhile. A negative NPV means the future money is worth less than what you spend, so the project may not be worth doing. Between two projects, the higher NPV is usually the better one.

Return on investment (ROI) is the financial return shown as a percentage of the money invested. Internal rate of return (IRR) is harder to calculate but very accurate; a good IRR should beat what you would earn by leaving your money in the bank.
steps.example: A project spends 100,000 now and earns 30,000, 35,000, 37,000, and 39,000 over the next four years. At a 10 percent discount rate, the NPV is about 10,634 dollars, which is positive, so it looks worthwhile.
keyPoints: Present value = future money expressed in today's dollars
Positive NPV means the return meets your requirement
A good IRR beats the return from a bank

steps.title: Quick check: reading NPV
steps.body: Test your understanding of net present value.

quiz.question: A project has a positive NPV. What does this tell you?
options: The project has no risk at all
The future cash flows earn at least the required return, so the investment looks worthwhile
The client will pay all invoices early
The project will finish ahead of schedule
quiz.explanation: A positive NPV means the money invested today generates future cash flows that earn the required level of return.

steps.title: Non-financial justification
steps.body:
Not every good reason to do a project is about money. Many strong projects are justified by non-financial criteria. These are benefits that do not show up directly as profit but still matter a great deal to the organization.

Typical non-financial reasons include gaining market share, building barriers that make it harder for competitors to enter your market, reducing your reliance on a single supplier, and supporting the local community. A project might earn little money yet still be worth doing because it protects your position or builds goodwill.

Many kinds of organizations lean heavily on non-financial criteria. Health services, social services, not-for-profit groups, wildlife conservation projects, and even the Olympic Games all use non-financial reasons to decide which projects to take on.

In practice you usually mix financial and non-financial criteria. The exact mix is your decision. The chapter gives one clear rule: the more professional your criteria are, the greater the chance of project success. So choose your criteria carefully and write them down, instead of deciding by feeling.
steps.example: A phone company runs a rural coverage project that earns almost no profit. They still approve it, because it builds their reputation and reduces the chance a competitor enters that region.
keyPoints: Non-financial reasons include market share and community support
Health, charity, and conservation groups rely on them heavily
Most organizations mix financial and non-financial criteria

steps.title: Prioritising your projects
steps.body:
Sometimes several projects pass the selection process with flying colors. But you may not have enough people or money to do them all. So you need to rank them. This is prioritising: deciding which projects matter most.

First, choose the metrics that matter to you. Most organizations give the biggest weight to financial return. But there are other useful metrics too, such as reputation, difficulty, future growth, repeat business, and market share. You can give each metric a different weighting, so some count more than others in the final score.

A weighted scoring approach means each project gets a score for each metric, then you multiply by the weighting and add it all up. The interesting result is that the project with the highest total is not always the one with the biggest financial return. A project can win overall because it scores well on reputation and growth.

At the end, you want a written, ranked list that everyone can see. When a new project appears, you place it in its correct position. Doing this well helps you send time, energy, and resources to the most important projects.
steps.example: Project B has the highest financial return, but after adding weighted scores for reputation and future growth, Project C wins the top priority spot.
keyPoints: Prioritising ranks projects when you cannot do them all
Use weighted metrics, not just financial return
Keep a written, ranked list everyone can see

steps.title: Teach it back: prioritising
steps.body: A teammate says: "Surely we should just do the project that makes the most money first, right?" Explain, in simple words, why that is not always true. Describe how weighted scoring works, and give at least two non-financial metrics that could push a lower-earning project to the top of the list.

steps.title: The project charter
steps.body:
Once a project passes selection, you must record its formal approval. You do this with a project charter. The chapter calls the charter the founding document or the birth certificate of a project. It proves the project has support and approval. Every project, no matter how small, should have one.

The size of the charter fits the size of the project. For a big project, the charter may be a large document, even a full business case. For a small project, it can be as simple as a signed work order. It does not have all the detail of later documents like the full scope statement or budget, but it holds enough to commit real time and money.

The charter also has a symbolic value. It shows a commitment to professional project management. Importantly, the project manager should help prepare the charter. If someone else writes it and simply hands it over, you often get missed information, poor early estimates, and weak commitment from the start.

A charter can answer questions like: does the project match our strategy? Does it meet our financial needs? Who is the client? What is the known scope, budget, and time constraint? Who will manage it?
steps.example: For a small office move, the charter is a one-page signed work order naming the client, a rough budget, the deadline, and the project manager. Small, but it still exists.
keyPoints: The charter is the project's 'birth certificate' of approval
Every project needs one, sized to fit the project
The project manager should help write it

steps.title: Build a simple project charter
steps.body: Practice by writing a short project charter for a small, real or imagined project, for example organising a team training day. Keep it to about one page. Answer these charter questions in your own words: Does the project match the organization's goals? Does it meet the financial needs? Who is the client? What is the known scope at this stage? What is the rough budget? What are the time constraints? Who will be the project manager? Write it plainly, and imagine getting the client to sign it.

steps.title: Closing a project the right way
steps.body:
Every project must be closed formally and professionally. The charter proved the project began; the closure process proves it did what it should, that bills are paid, and that resources are freed. Sadly, most projects are never closed properly. Two reasons: there is no documented closure process, and the team is pulled onto new projects before finishing the old one. Many people wrongly see closure as a luxury.

Plan these closure elements at the start, not at the last minute. Deliverable acceptance means getting sign-off from the client that the product meets the agreed specifications. Financial closure means all invoices are raised and all bills are paid. Contractual closure means every contract condition is met and both sides agree the contract is finished. Reassigning resources means the people who worked on the project have new work to go to.

Lessons learned may be the most valuable part. You look back at what went well and what went wrong, write down the root causes, and store them for future project managers. A post-implementation review, done some time later, checks whether the project really delivered its intended outcomes, not just its outputs. The easiest tool for all this is a written close-out checklist.
steps.example: Six months after launch, a team runs a post-implementation review and finds the new system did cut costs as hoped. That is benefits realisation: checking the outcome, not just the output.
keyPoints: Formal closure proves the project delivered and bills are paid
Key steps: acceptance, financial and contractual closure, reassigning people, lessons learned
A written close-out checklist makes closure much easier

steps.title: Managing project changes
steps.body:
During any project, people will ask for changes. Some are small, some are large. They come from team members, clients, and other stakeholders. The chapter gives its strongest warning here: no matter the size or the source, ALL changes MUST be assessed and documented. This is the only place in the whole book the author emphasises two words like this, because undocumented change is perhaps the single greatest cause of project failure.

Why do people skip it? A change seems small, we do not want to cause a fuss, so we just do the extra work. Often that is fine. But just as often it leads to arguments, unpaid invoices, unhappy staff, lost customers, and even legal action.

The solution is a change control process. Small changes may just need a saved email that records a verbal agreement. Larger changes need proper forms, and very large ones may need a new business case. Use staged levels: quick decisions for small changes, and escalation to senior staff or a change control board for big ones. Keep a change register that logs every request and its outcome. Remember the golden rule: always deliver the original scope PLUS all approved changes.
steps.example: A client asks for one extra report. Instead of just doing it, the manager logs it in the change register, gets email approval, and adds it to the agreed scope.
keyPoints: ALL changes must be assessed and documented, without exception
Undocumented change is the greatest single cause of failure
Use staged change control and always log every request

title: Managing Scope
intro: Scope is the heart of every project. It says clearly what work you will do, and what work you will not do. When scope is clear, people agree. When scope is unclear, people make guesses, and guesses turn into arguments. In this module you will learn what project scope means, how product scope is different from project scope, the words we use to describe project work, how to define the scope with your team and client, and how to build a Work Breakdown Structure (WBS). Take your time, and enjoy learning step by step.

steps.title: What is project scope?
steps.body:
Project scope is the description of all the work you will do in a project. It lives in a document called the scope statement. The author of the chapter says the scope statement is the most important part of a project. He believes every project must have one.

But the scope statement does more than one job. It describes the work you will do. Just as important, it also describes the work you will not do. This second part sets the boundary of the project. A boundary is like a line on a map. It shows where the work stops.

Why does this matter so much? Because most fights on projects do not come from the work you wrote down. They come from the work you did not write down. When something is not clear, people make assumptions. An assumption is a guess about what is true. These guesses often turn into arguments later.

So take your time and get the scope right. Once you have a clear scope statement, do not do any work that is not written in it. Doing extra, undocumented work is a fast way to make a project fail.
steps.example: A team builds a company website. The scope statement says they will design 5 pages and write the text. It also says clearly: they will NOT provide photos or translate the site. This stops a later argument about who buys the photos.
keyPoints: Scope describes both the work you will do and the work you will not do.
The document that holds this is the scope statement.
Unclear scope leads to assumptions, and assumptions lead to arguments.
Do not do any work that is not written in the scope statement.

steps.title: Building scope is a team effort
steps.body:
Defining scope is often an iterative process. Iterative means you do it step by step, in small rounds, not all at once. Sometimes you can only describe the work for the near future. This happens because the full scope depends on the work you are doing right now.

When scope grows step by step, you must keep checking with two groups. First, your project team members, the people doing the work. Second, your client, the person or company who wants the project. You ask them: is the scope correct and complete? This checking keeps everyone on the same page.

You can start writing the scope statement yourself. But it is usually essential to get input from the team and other stakeholders. A stakeholder is anyone who cares about or is affected by the project. Their knowledge fills the gaps you cannot see alone.

Another smart move is to look back. Think about past mistakes and misunderstandings on earlier projects. These lessons show you what a good scope statement looks like for the kind of work you do.
steps.example: A software team defines the scope for the login feature first. They cannot fully define the payment feature yet, because it depends on the login work. So they plan the login now and check the payment scope with the client in a later round.
keyPoints: Iterative means you define scope in small rounds, not all at once.
Keep checking the scope with your team and your client.
Get input from stakeholders, not just yourself.
Learn from past mistakes to improve future scope statements.

steps.title: What goes inside a scope statement
steps.body:
A scope statement can hold many kinds of information. You do not have to include every item on every project. You choose the parts that fit your work.

Here are the common parts. Project outcomes and objectives: the results you want and the goals you aim for. Deliverables: the things you will hand over, like a report or a finished product. Milestones: important points in time that show progress, like "design finished" or "testing done". A description of the technical requirements of the product: what the product must be able to do. Exclusions: the work you are not doing. And any other detail that needs to be written down.

If you build your own scope statement template, make sure it has the most useful of these parts. A template is a ready-made form you fill in for each project. It saves time and helps you not forget important things.

Notice that exclusions are on this list. Writing what is out of scope is just as valuable as writing what is in scope. Both together give a full, clear picture.
steps.example: A scope statement for a mobile app might list: objective (help users track water intake), deliverables (iPhone and Android app), a milestone (beta version by March), and an exclusion (no smartwatch version in this project).
keyPoints: A scope statement can include objectives, deliverables, milestones, and technical requirements.
It should also include exclusions, the work you are not doing.
You pick the parts that fit your project.
A template is a ready-made form that saves time and prevents forgetting.

steps.title: Teach it back: why write the exclusions?
steps.body:
Time to teach this idea in your own words. Imagine a new project manager asks you: "Why should I bother writing down the work we are NOT doing? Isn't it enough to describe the work we ARE doing?"

Explain to them, in simple language, why exclusions matter. Use the idea of assumptions and arguments from the chapter. Give one short example from your own life or work to make it clear.

steps.title: Product scope vs project scope
steps.body:
If you ask most people what a project does, they will say: it makes a product. That is only partly true. A lot of work happens in a project, and only some of it is about making the product.

Project scope is the description of all the work in the project. It is more than the product. It also includes planning, executing, monitoring and controlling, and closing work. There is other work too: risk management, communications, estimating, and quality management. All of this must be described in some way in the project scope.

Product scope is smaller. It is the description of the features, functions, and specifications of the product or service you will deliver. Features are what the product has. Functions are what it does. Specifications are the exact details, like size or speed.

Here is the key relationship: product scope is a subset of project scope. Subset means it is one part inside the bigger whole. So the product scope sits inside the project scope, along with all the other project work.
steps.example: Building a new house: the product scope is the house itself, its rooms, wiring, and plumbing. The project scope is bigger, it also includes planning the schedule, managing risks, talking to the owner, and checking quality.
keyPoints: Project scope covers ALL the work: product plus planning, risk, communication, quality, and more.
Product scope covers only the features, functions, and specifications of the product.
Product scope is a subset (a smaller part) of project scope.
Making the product is only one part of the total project work.

steps.title: Quick check: product vs project scope
steps.body: Let's check your understanding of how the two kinds of scope fit together.

quiz.question: Which statement is correct about product scope and project scope?
options: Project scope is a subset of product scope.
Product scope is a subset of project scope.
They are two names for exactly the same thing.
Product scope includes risk and communication work.
quiz.explanation: Product scope (the product's features and functions) is one part inside the larger project scope, which also covers planning, risk, communication, and quality work.

steps.title: Words for project work: from low to high detail
steps.body:
The chapter gives a set of terms for describing project work. These words move from a low level of detail to a high level of detail. They are not steps you must always follow in order. They are simply names for how well the work is defined at different stages.

Let's start with the first, lower-detail terms. The statement of work (SOW) is a high-level narrative description of the work. Narrative means it is written in plain sentences, like a short story. The SOW is usually made right at the start. It helps describe the work in a business case or a project charter. But it is not detailed enough to finish the project on its own.

The project charter contains enough scope description to formally authorize the project. Authorize means to give official permission. For some projects, the charter just gives permission to spend money on defining the scope more. For other projects, the charter may already hold a full scope description.

So far the detail is still low. These early documents get the project started, but more definition is needed next.
steps.example: A city wants a new park. The SOW says in a few sentences: "Build a public park with grass, trees, paths, and benches." The project charter then officially approves the project and allows money to be spent planning it in detail.
keyPoints: The terms move from low detail to high detail, and are not a strict order.
The statement of work (SOW) is a short, high-level description made at the start.
The project charter gives official permission to run the project.
Both are early documents and usually need more detail added later.

steps.title: Words for project work: reaching full detail
steps.body:
Now let's continue to the higher-detail terms. After the early documents, you add more definition.

Requirements are gathered from clients and users. A requirement is a need or a thing the product or project must do. Collecting requirements adds detail to low-detail documents like the SOW and the project charter.

The preliminary scope statement is the next stage. Preliminary means early or not final. It is one more round in the iterative process of building a full scope.

The scope statement is the full and complete description of the work to be done, and not to be done. It can cover the next stage of work or even the whole project. Remember, development is often iterative, based on the next stage, time frame, or budget.

Finally, the work breakdown structure (WBS) takes the work described in the scope statement and breaks it into its component parts. Component parts are the smaller pieces that make up the whole. We will explore the WBS soon.
steps.example: For the park project: requirements are collected (the paths must fit wheelchairs, the benches must be wooden). These build up into a full scope statement, which is then broken into smaller parts in a WBS.
keyPoints: Requirements are the needs gathered from clients and users.
A preliminary scope statement is an early, not-yet-final version.
The scope statement is the full and complete description of the work, in and out.
The WBS breaks the scope statement into smaller component parts.

steps.title: Quick check: naming the documents
steps.body: You just learned several terms for describing project work. Let's test one.

quiz.question: Which document is the full and complete description of the work to be done, and not to be done?
options: The statement of work (SOW)
The project charter
The scope statement
The business case
quiz.explanation: The scope statement is the full and complete description of the work to be done and not to be done; the SOW and charter hold less detail.

steps.title: How to define the scope
steps.body:
There are many ways to collect the parts of a scope statement. Your choice of tools depends on your own type of project. Let's look at the main methods.

The easiest place to start is your own knowledge, especially if you have done this kind of project before. If you have not, go and ask other people who have expertise, meaning deep skill or knowledge in the area. Remember, scope is more than the product. It includes all the project work and the agreed requirements of all stakeholders, not just the client.

You can collect requirements in many ways: in person, by email, by survey, by phone, or in a facilitated brainstorming workshop. Facilitated means someone guides the meeting.
