Whatever way you choose, always feed back your understanding to the people you talked to. This means you repeat what you heard, so you can be sure you got it right.

This work produces your requirements documentation, which clearly sets out each stakeholder's needs. You may also build a requirements traceability matrix. This is a table that maps each requirement back to a basic objective of the project, so you can see why each requirement exists.
steps.example: To define the scope for an online shop, the manager uses her own experience, emails three past clients for advice, and runs a brainstorming workshop with the team. She then repeats back what she heard to make sure it is correct.
keyPoints: Start with your own knowledge, then ask people with expertise.
Collect requirements in person, by email, survey, phone, or workshop.
Always feed back your understanding to confirm you got it right.
A requirements traceability matrix maps each requirement to a project objective.

steps.title: Agree inclusions and exclusions with the client
steps.body:
You should always talk with the client about their expectations of the scope. An expectation is what a person believes will happen. Make sure you discuss and write down all inclusions and all exclusions with them. Inclusions are the work you will do. Exclusions are the work you will not do.

Here is an important warning from the chapter. Do not assume that because you are a technical expert, your client sees things the same way you do. You may know what is normally in and out of a scope of work. But your client is often a non-technical person. They may make their own assumptions about what work is included.

These hidden assumptions are exactly what cause misunderstandings and arguments later. So it is your job to make everything clear and written down, not left in someone's head.

You can also use your project team members to help. They may have done this kind of work before and will know what is normally required. And one of the author's favorite methods is to look back at similar finished projects. You study their scope statements and their lessons learned, meaning notes on what they did well and what they did badly.
steps.example: A builder assumes painting the walls is not included, because it usually is not. The client assumes it IS included. Because they discussed and wrote it down early, they caught the difference before it became a costly argument.
keyPoints: Discuss and document all inclusions and exclusions with the client.
Do not assume the client sees the scope the way a technical expert does.
Hidden assumptions cause misunderstandings and arguments.
Use your team's experience and the lessons learned from past projects.

steps.title: Teach it back: the client conversation
steps.body:
Imagine you are coaching a new project manager before their first client meeting. They say: "I'm the technical expert here, so I already know what's in and out of scope. Why waste time asking the client?"

In your own simple words, explain why this thinking is risky. Use the ideas of assumptions, inclusions, and exclusions. Then give one piece of practical advice for how they should run that client conversation.

steps.title: The Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)
steps.body:
Once your scope statement is written, you can use it to start estimating. You estimate the time the project will take, the cost of the project, and any risks. Estimating means making a careful guess based on knowledge.

The best way to estimate well is to break the scope into smaller parts. These small parts are often called work packages. When the work is this small, you can accurately assign time, cost, and risk to each work package. This breaking-down process is called decomposition. Decomposition means splitting a big thing into smaller pieces.

You can show decomposition as a picture called a Work Breakdown Structure, or WBS. The WBS is often called the backbone of a project. Why? Because without it, you will struggle to fully define the scope, make accurate time and cost estimates, work out the resources you need, manage risks, and check progress. It is generally accepted that all projects should have a WBS.

You can do decomposition quickly with experienced people and past lessons. You can do it formally with questionnaires or brainstorming sessions, or more informally through a series of emails and conversations.
steps.example: For a house, the big job "build the house" is decomposed into smaller work packages like foundation, walls, roof, and plumbing. Each small package can then get its own time, cost, and risk estimate.
keyPoints: Break the scope into small parts called work packages.
This breaking-down process is called decomposition.
The picture of it is the Work Breakdown Structure (WBS).
The WBS is the backbone of a project; all projects should have one.

steps.title: How far should you break the work down?
steps.body:
A common question is: how small should the pieces be? How far do you decompose each part of the WBS? The chapter says: be professional about it.

Your target level of detail is the point where you can reliably estimate the time and cost for each work package. Reliably means you can trust the guess. Once you reach that point, you can stop. Breaking the work down further usually gives little extra benefit for the extra time and effort it takes.

Also, remember an important rule: a work package is still a deliverable, not a set of instructions on how to do the work. A deliverable is a result you hand over. So a work package describes what will be produced, not the tiny steps of how to produce it.

When the WBS is fully decomposed, it should capture all the work in the current scope. But note one thing: the WBS does not put the work into any reliable order or sequence. Putting activities in the right order comes later, during project scheduling. The WBS answers "what work?", not "in what order?".
steps.example: "Install plumbing" is a good work package because you can estimate its cost and time, and it is a deliverable. You would NOT break it down into tiny steps like "pick up wrench", which gives no useful estimating benefit.
keyPoints: Stop decomposing when you can reliably estimate time and cost.
Breaking down too far wastes effort for little benefit.
A work package is a deliverable (a result), not how-to instructions.
The WBS shows what work exists, but not the order; sequencing comes later.

steps.title: Quick check: what the WBS does not do
steps.body: The WBS is powerful, but it has clear limits. Let's check one of them.

quiz.question: Which task is NOT done by the Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)?
options: Breaking the scope into smaller work packages
Capturing all the work in the current scope
Putting the activities into their correct time sequence
Helping you estimate time and cost for each work package
quiz.explanation: The WBS shows what work exists and breaks it into packages, but it does not sequence the activities; ordering happens later during project scheduling.

steps.title: WBS nodes, numbers, and the WBS dictionary
steps.body:
Each box in a WBS is called a node. Inside each node there are numbers, and these numbers are important. They give every piece of work its own code, so you can identify it easily.

These numbers do more than label the work. You can use them to estimate the cost of each element. If you charge costs to your job, you can use the numbers as cost account numbers. This means you record the money spent against each work package's number. The result is much greater transparency, a clear view, of exactly where the money has gone.

A graphical WBS node usually shows only a little information: the work package name, the WBS code, and in short form, the duration (how long), the cost, and the resources assigned. Resources are the people or tools used.

Because the node shows only limited information, a helpful partner tool is the WBS dictionary. This is a separate document with much more detail about each work package. The node gives the quick summary; the dictionary gives the full story for anyone who wants to look deeper.
steps.example: A WBS node reads: "1.3.2.1 Plumbing, $13,500, 6 days, 2 plumbers." The number 1.3.2.1 is used as a cost account, so all plumbing spending is tracked in one clear place.
keyPoints: Each box in a WBS is a node, and its numbers form a unique code.
The codes can be used as cost account numbers to track spending clearly.
A node shows only a short summary: name, code, duration, cost, resources.
The WBS dictionary is a partner document with fuller detail on each work package.

steps.title: Build a mini WBS and one dictionary entry
steps.body:
Now it is your turn to practice. Pick a small, simple project you understand well, for example: making breakfast, planning a birthday party, or building a small website.

Do three things:

1. Write the top goal (the whole project) at the top.

2. Decompose it into 3 to 5 work packages. Remember, each work package should be a deliverable (a result), and small enough that you could estimate its time and cost. Give each one a simple number code (like 1.1, 1.2, 1.3).

3. Choose ONE work package and write a short WBS dictionary entry for it. Include a brief description of what is included, one thing that is excluded, a rough cost, and a rough duration.

Keep it simple. The goal is to feel how decomposition and the WBS dictionary work in real life.

steps.title: Quick check: why scope matters most
steps.body: Let's finish with the big idea that runs through the whole chapter.

quiz.question: According to the chapter, what causes MOST disagreements on projects?
options: The work that was clearly described in the scope statement
Assumptions about work that was NOT described in the scope statement
The numbers written inside the WBS nodes
Having too many stakeholders in the workshop
quiz.explanation: Few arguments come from what is written down; most come from assumptions people make about work that was left out of the scope statement.

title: Estimating Cost and Time
intro: Welcome! In this module you will learn how to guess the cost and time of a project in a smart way. We call this "estimating." An estimate is your best guess about the future, using the information you have today. You will see how the Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) helps, what makes an estimate good or weak, simple rules to follow, and three useful tools: analogous, parametric, and three-point estimating. Take your time, and enjoy the learning!

steps.title: Why estimating matters
steps.body:
Every project needs two big answers early on. People want to know: How long will this take? How much will it cost? This module helps you answer both.

An estimate is not magic. It is your best guess about the future, based on what you know today. It is not a fixed price. It is not a promise. It is a careful forecast.

Cost and time use very similar methods, so we study them together. Your cost estimates become the base of your project budget. Your time estimates become the base of your project schedule. So this work is important. Good estimates lead to good plans.

There is one key idea to remember from the start. An estimate is only as good as the information you have. When you know a lot, and your information is detailed and correct, your estimate will be strong. When you know little, your estimate will be weak and unclear. This simple rule guides everything in this module.
steps.example: A client asks, "How much for the new website?" You do not pick a random number. Instead, you make a careful forecast using the tools in this module.
keyPoints: An estimate is a best guess about the future, not a fixed price
Cost and time use similar methods, so we learn them together
Cost estimates feed the budget; time estimates feed the schedule
An estimate is only as good as the information you have

steps.title: How the WBS supports estimating
steps.body:
First, a quick reminder. The scope statement describes all the work of the project. The Work Breakdown Structure, or WBS, breaks that scope into smaller and smaller parts. The smallest parts are called work packages.

Why does this help estimating? Because it is very hard to guess the cost of a huge, unclear job. But it is much easier to guess the cost of one small, clear task. When you break the big project into small work packages, you can estimate each small piece well. Then you add the pieces together.

So the WBS is the most detailed form of your scope. Using the WBS is the most accurate way to estimate cost and time.

There is also a timing rule. Early in a project, the scope is not fully defined, so your estimates are less accurate. Later, when the scope is broken down with a WBS, your estimates get better. In the same way, you can estimate near work quite well, but far-off work is harder to guess. If parts of your scope are still unclear or undefined, the estimates for those parts will be unclear too.
steps.example: Instead of guessing the cost of "build a house," you break it into work packages like "pour foundation," "frame walls," and "install roof," then estimate each one.
keyPoints: The WBS breaks scope into small work packages
Small, clear tasks are much easier to estimate than one big job
The WBS is the most accurate base for estimating cost and time
Undefined parts of the scope give undefined estimates

steps.title: Quick check: scope and accuracy
steps.body: Let's check the main idea about the WBS and scope.

quiz.question: Why does a detailed WBS give more accurate estimates?
options: It removes all risk from the project
It breaks the scope into small, clear work packages that are easier to estimate
It lets you skip the scope statement
It makes the project shorter
quiz.explanation: The WBS splits the scope into small work packages, and small clear tasks are much easier to estimate accurately than one large, vague job.

steps.title: What makes an estimate good or weak
steps.body:
We already know that clear, detailed scope makes better estimates. Now let's look at other things that change the quality of an estimate.

The first is the length of the project. Longer projects are harder to estimate. Over a long time, the scope may be hard to define, and outside factors bring more uncertainty. For example, prices may rise over time. This price rise is called inflation. The longer the project, the harder these things are to predict.

The second is the human factor. Most estimates are made by people, and people are not perfect. Each person brings their own bias. Bias means a personal leaning that colors your view. Some people are naturally optimistic, so they guess low. Others are pessimistic, so they guess high. Also, a person with more experience usually gives a better estimate than a beginner.

So when you read an estimate, remember who made it and how long the project is. These both shape how much you can trust the number.
steps.example: A five-year building project must guess prices years ahead. Inflation could push material costs much higher, so the far-future part of the estimate is less certain.
keyPoints: Longer projects are harder to estimate because of more uncertainty
Inflation means prices rising over time
Bias is a personal leaning that colors a person's estimate
Experienced people usually give better estimates than beginners

steps.title: The problem with padding
steps.body:
Here is a very human habit. When people estimate a task, they often add extra time or extra money, just to feel safe. This extra amount is called padding, or a buffer. They think, "I will add a few days, just in case."

This sounds smart, but it creates a problem. Many managers know that people pad their numbers. So the manager cuts the estimate down. Then the worker feels the cut is unfair, so next time they pad even more. The manager cuts again. This becomes a game with no winner. Trust goes down, and the numbers become less honest.

What is the better way? Try to build a culture where people do not feel they must pad. Make it safe to give an honest estimate. Ask people to back up their numbers with some data or a reason. When accurate estimates are valued and respected, people feel free to be honest. That is much better than the padding game.

Remember, some uncertainty is real and should be shown openly. That is different from secretly padding a number to feel safe.
steps.example: A developer thinks a task takes 3 days but writes 5 "to be safe." The manager suspects this and cuts it to 3. Next time, the developer writes 7. The game gets worse.
keyPoints: Padding is extra time or money added to feel safe
When managers cut padded numbers, people pad even more
Build a culture that values honest estimates backed by data
Show real uncertainty openly instead of hiding it in padding

steps.title: Teach it back: the padding game
steps.body: Now it is your turn to teach. Imagine a new team member says, "I always add a few extra days to every estimate, just in case. It keeps me safe." In your own simple words, explain why this padding habit can cause a problem between workers and managers. Then say what a team could do instead to get honest estimates. Try to use the words "padding" and "buffer."

steps.title: Good estimates: ask the right people
steps.body:
There are some simple guidelines that make estimates better. Let's start with the most important one: get the people who will do the work to estimate the work.

Why is this so powerful? There are two reasons. First, the people doing the work know the work best. They know the real steps, the real time, and the real cost of materials. So their numbers are more realistic. Second, it creates buy-in. Buy-in means a person feels the plan is partly theirs, so they care about it. If you hand someone an estimate made by others, they feel less bound to meet it. But if they made the estimate themselves, they feel responsible for it.

A second guideline: use several people or sources. When more than one person estimates, and they can see and question each other's numbers, the final estimate becomes more realistic. The act of asking, "Why is your number so different?" leads to better estimates for everyone.

So do not estimate alone in a quiet room. Bring in the doers, and let different views meet.
steps.example: Before promising a delivery date, the project manager asks the two engineers who will build the feature to give their own estimates, then compares them.
keyPoints: Let the people who do the work make the estimate
They know the real steps, time, and costs
Buy-in means people feel responsible for their own estimate
Using several people and comparing numbers improves accuracy

steps.title: Good estimates: units, risk, and normal conditions
steps.body:
Let's continue with three more simple guidelines for better estimates.

First, standardize your units. This means everyone must use the same measure. For cost in one currency, this is easy: you use dollars and cents. It is harder with several currencies. For time, you must choose one unit. Will estimates be in hours, days, weeks, or months? Pick one and tell everyone. If one person says "3" meaning days and another means weeks, you have a big mess.

Second, show the risk clearly. Every estimate has some uncertainty. Say how sure you are. Then people can make a good choice: go ahead, ask for more detail, or find another way to do the work.

Third, assume normal working conditions. Do not build your estimate around a perfect day or a disaster day. Assume a normal day, unless you already know about a special situation that you must include.

These guidelines are small, but together they remove confusion and make your estimates clear and easy to trust.
steps.example: A manager writes, "All time estimates in this project are in days, not hours," so no one confuses a 6-hour task with a 6-day task.
keyPoints: Standardize units: choose hours, days, weeks, or months, and tell everyone
Show how much risk or uncertainty is in each estimate
Assume normal working conditions unless you know of a special case
Clear units and clear risk make estimates easy to trust

steps.title: Quick check: estimating guidelines
steps.body: Let's test the guidelines for good estimates.

quiz.question: According to the guidelines, who is usually best placed to estimate a task?
options: The most senior manager, alone in an office
The person who will actually do the work
Anyone, as long as they guess quickly
The client who is paying for the project
quiz.explanation: The people doing the work know the real effort and cost, and estimating their own work creates buy-in, so they feel responsible for the numbers.

steps.title: The toolbox: effort versus accuracy
steps.body:
Now we reach the fun part: the tools you can use to estimate cost and time. There are several proven methods. Sadly, some people still just pick a random number, or choose the highest number the client will pay. Both of these lead to unhappy clients and weak businesses. You can do much better.

Here is the key trade-off. Each method has a different level of accuracy, and a different level of effort and cost. In general, the more accurate a method is, the longer it takes and the more it costs. A quick guess is cheap but weak. A careful method is strong but takes time.

So you must be wise. Do not use an expensive method when a simple one is enough. The chapter gives a smart warning: never use a technique where the extra cost of the technique is greater than the extra accuracy it gives you.

Most projects use more than one method. Also, always write down which techniques you used and what assumptions you made. A template or checklist helps you stay consistent every time.
steps.example: For a small internal task, a quick estimate is fine. For a million-dollar contract, you spend more time and use a careful method, because the accuracy is worth the cost.
keyPoints: More accurate methods usually cost more effort and time
Match the method to how important the estimate is
Never spend more on a technique than the accuracy it gives back
Write down your techniques and assumptions every time

steps.title: Analogous estimating
steps.body:
Our first named tool is analogous estimating. The word "analogous" means "similar." So this method uses a similar past project or task to guess the new one.

Here is how it works. You find a work package from the past that is like your new work. You know how long it took or how much it cost. Then you compare the sizes and adjust. If the new work is bigger, your estimate goes up. If it is smaller, your estimate goes down.

Let's see an example. Suppose a similar task in the past took 10 hours. Your new task is twice as big. So your estimate is 20 hours. You took the old number and stretched it to fit the new size.

Analogous estimating has medium effort and medium accuracy. It is faster than breaking down every detail, and it is quite useful when you have good records of past work. But it is only as good as how similar the past work truly is. If the old task was very different, the estimate will be off. So choose your comparison carefully.
steps.example: Last year, painting one similar office took 10 hours. The new office is twice the size, so you estimate 20 hours to paint it.
keyPoints: Analogous means similar; you use a similar past job
You take a known past result and adjust it for the new size
Effort is medium and accuracy is medium
It only works well if the past work is truly similar

steps.title: Parametric estimating
steps.body:
Our second named tool is parametric estimating. This is one of the most popular and most accurate methods. It sounds hard, but the idea is simple.

You take a known amount of work, and you multiply it by a known rate. A rate is a cost or time "per unit." So the formula is: amount of work multiplied by rate. That is it.

Here is the classic example. Suppose you must build 10 kilometers of road. You know that each kilometer costs 1,000 dollars. You multiply 10 by 1,000, and you get an estimate of 10,000 dollars. Clean and simple.

Parametric estimating has high effort and high accuracy. It is very reliable when you truly know the rate. So it works best for repeatable work where the cost or time per unit is well known, like roads, tiles, or lines of pipe.

How is this different from analogous? Analogous stretches one whole past job to fit the new one. Parametric uses a rate per unit and multiplies. Parametric is usually more accurate, because a solid, known rate is stronger than a rough comparison of two whole jobs.
steps.example: You need 10 km of road. Each kilometer costs 1,000 dollars. Estimate = 10 x 1,000 = 10,000 dollars.
keyPoints: Parametric = amount of work multiplied by a known rate
A rate is the cost or time for one unit
Effort is high and accuracy is high
It needs a solid, known rate to work well

steps.title: Quick check: analogous vs parametric
steps.body: Let's tell these two tools apart.

quiz.question: You know each square meter of tiling costs 25 dollars, and you must tile 40 square meters. Which method are you using when you calculate 40 x 25 = 1,000 dollars?
options: Analogous estimating
Three-point estimating
Parametric estimating
Ballpark figure
quiz.explanation: Multiplying a known amount of work (40 square meters) by a known rate (25 dollars each) is parametric estimating.

steps.title: Three-point estimating
steps.body:
Our third named tool is three-point estimating. It comes from an older method called PERT. It is very useful when you are unsure, because it uses three guesses instead of one.

The three points are:
- Optimistic (O): the best case, if everything goes well.
- Realistic (R): the most likely case, a normal outcome.
- Pessimistic (P): the worst case, if things go badly.

You could just add the three and divide by three to get a simple average. But three-point estimating is smarter. It gives extra weight to the realistic number, because that outcome is most likely. In fact, it counts the realistic number four times. The formula is:

(O + (4 x R) + P) divided by 6

Let's try it. Optimistic is 10 days. Realistic is 15 days. Pessimistic is 25 days. So: (10 + (4 x 15) + 25) divided by 6 = (10 + 60 + 25) divided by 6 = 95 divided by 6 = about 15.83 days.

Three-point estimating has medium effort and high accuracy. It is a great choice when the future feels uncertain and you want to balance hope and worry.
steps.example: O = 10 days, R = 15 days, P = 25 days. Three-point estimate = (10 + 60 + 25) / 6 = 95 / 6 = about 15.83 days.
keyPoints: Three points: optimistic, realistic (most likely), pessimistic
Formula: (O + 4R + P) / 6
The realistic number gets four times the weight
Medium effort, high accuracy; great when the future is uncertain

steps.title: Build it: your own three-point estimate
steps.body:
Time to practice. Pick a real task you know, like painting a room, writing a report, or fixing a bug. Then do these steps:

1. Write your optimistic estimate (O) - the best case if all goes well.
2. Write your realistic estimate (R) - the most likely time.
3. Write your pessimistic estimate (P) - the worst case.
4. Use the formula: (O + (4 x R) + P) / 6.
5. Do the math and write your final three-point estimate.

As a bonus, find the range of certainty. The chapter gives a simple heuristic (a rule of thumb) for the standard deviation: (P - O) / 6. Two of these on each side of your estimate gives about 95% certainty. So write your answer as "about X days, plus or minus Y days." Show your numbers and your final result.

steps.title: Teach it back: pick the right tool
steps.body: Show what you have learned. Imagine a coworker says, "Analogous, parametric, three-point... they all sound the same to me. When do I use each one?" In your own simple words, explain each of the three tools in one or two sentences, and give a quick example of when it fits best. For extra credit, mention the level of effort and accuracy for each. Keep it friendly and clear, as if teaching a new team member.

steps.title: Clarity, accuracy, and wrapping up
steps.body:
You now know the main estimating tools. The chapter also lists a few others you may meet: the ballpark figure (a fast, low-accuracy guess), top-down and bottom-up estimating, lessons learned, expert judgment, published data, and the quote. A quote is special: it gives an agreed price and delivery time, so it has very high accuracy. The important skill is to know each tool's accuracy and the right time to use it.

The chapter ends with a wise idea: the myth of certainty. An estimate is never a fixed promise. So aim for two things: clarity and accuracy.

Clarity means being open and transparent. Show your sources. Show your assumptions. Say how sure you are. This lets people know how to treat your estimate.

Accuracy means a small margin of error. This comes from well-defined scope. Better scope means fewer surprises.

When you finish estimating, you have two valuable things: the total estimated cost and the total estimated time. These become the foundation of your budget and your schedule. Well done - you now have a real toolkit for estimating with honesty and skill.
steps.example: A strong estimate says: "About 20,000 dollars, plus or minus 15%, based on last year's similar project and today's material rates." It is both clear about its sources and honest about its range.
keyPoints: Many tools exist; know each one's accuracy and best use
An estimate is never a fixed promise - certainty is a myth
Clarity means showing sources and assumptions openly
Accuracy comes from well-defined scope and feeds your budget and schedule

title: Creating a Schedule
intro: Welcome! In this module you will learn how to build a project schedule step by step. A schedule shows what work must be done, in what order, and how long it will take. You will meet simple tools like sequencing, network diagrams, the critical path, and ways to finish faster. We keep the language easy, with clear examples from the book "The Professional Project Manager" by Sean Whitaker. Let's begin.

steps.title: What is a project schedule?
steps.body:
A project schedule is a picture of all the work in your project shown in the right order. It lists the tasks and activities you must do. It shows which task comes first, which comes next, and how tasks connect to each other. From this, you can see how long a stage, a milestone, or the whole project will take.

Think of the schedule as a map of time. It tells you when to expect each part of the work to be finished. It helps you forecast, which means to predict, when milestones and the project will be done.

The first schedule you make is the best (optimal) plan. After you improve it for time and resources, you save it as a baseline. A baseline is your saved plan. Later, you compare real progress against this baseline to see if the project is on track and to decide what to fix.
steps.example: You are painting a house. Your schedule shows: buy paint, then prepare walls, then paint. It also shows each task takes 1 day, so the job takes 3 days in total.
keyPoints: A schedule shows all tasks in the correct order.
It tells you how long milestones and the project will take.
The first plan is the optimal plan; once saved it becomes the baseline.
You compare real progress against the baseline.

steps.title: Good input means a good schedule
steps.body:
A schedule is only as good as the information you put into it. If your scope of work is clear and your Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) is well made, your schedule will be strong. The WBS is the list of all the work, broken into smaller parts. If the input is messy, the schedule will be weak too.

You can build your schedule in one pass or in several rounds. One pass works best when the whole project is fully known and every time estimate is complete. But often you learn more as the project goes on. So you may plan in rounds, adding detail each time.

The book describes the steps one after another, but you do not have to work in a strict line. Unless one task truly needs another to finish first, you can do many steps at the same time. Break the work into clear steps only if it is new to you and this makes planning easier.
steps.example: A builder who does similar houses every month keeps a template. The template already lists the usual tasks, their order, and their times, so she does not start from zero each time.
keyPoints: Good scope and WBS give a good schedule.
You can plan in one pass or in several rounds.
Steps do not have to be done in a strict line.
Templates save time for repeated work.

steps.title: Defining the activities
steps.body:
The first step in a good schedule is to take your scope of work and break it into small activities. Start from the work packages in your WBS, then break these into even smaller pieces. For a very small project, you can go straight to the small pieces without a middle step.

Why break it down so far? Because small pieces are easier to estimate. You want each activity to reach a level where you can guess its time and cost in a reliable and accurate way. The more you break down the work, the more accurate your schedule will be.

Sometimes you do not yet know all the work, or some work depends on other work first. That is normal. Break down what you can right now. Come back and add more detail later as the work becomes clearer. Mark the parts you are sure about, and mark the parts that are still uncertain. When you can, ask the people who will do the work to help estimate. They know it best.
steps.example: A work package called "kitchen" breaks into smaller activities: fit cabinets, connect water, install the oven, and paint the walls. Each small activity is easy to time.
keyPoints: Break work packages into small activities.
Smaller pieces are easier to estimate accurately.
Break down what you can now; add detail later.
Ask the people doing the work to help estimate.

steps.title: Quick check: breaking down work
steps.body: Let's check one key idea about activities.

quiz.question: Why do we break the work down into small activities?
options: So the project has more paperwork
So the time and cost of each activity can be estimated more reliably and accurately
So we can skip the WBS completely
So we never have to update the plan again
quiz.explanation: Smaller activities are easier to estimate, which makes the whole schedule more accurate.

steps.title: Putting activities in sequence
steps.body:
Once your activities are clear, you put them in order. This is called sequencing. Here you decide which activity comes before another, and which comes after. You also decide the type of link between them.

An activity that comes right before another is called its predecessor. An activity that comes right after another is called its successor. So the walls are the predecessor of the roof, and the roof is the successor of the walls.

You do not need fancy software to start. You can use a spreadsheet, or even sticky notes on a white board. Write each activity on one note, then arrange the notes in the order they happen. Draw arrows between them to show the links. When you finish, you have a network diagram: a picture of all activities and how they depend on each other. If you use software, the arrows appear automatically once you set predecessors and successors. Only draw a network diagram if it truly helps your project.
steps.example: On sticky notes you write "build walls" and "put on roof." You place walls first, roof second, and draw an arrow from walls to roof. Walls are the predecessor; the roof is the successor.
keyPoints: Sequencing puts activities in the right order.
A predecessor comes before; a successor comes after.
Sticky notes or software both work.
The result is a network diagram.

steps.title: Burst, merge, and relationship types
steps.body:
Two words help you read a network diagram. A burst activity has more than one direct successor: two or more tasks depend on it. A merge activity has more than one direct predecessor: two or more things must happen before it can start. An activity can be both. Burst activities can cause bottlenecks, which means points where work gets stuck.

Activities link in four relationship types. Each type sets the start or finish of one task next to another.

Finish-to-Start (FS): the successor cannot start until the predecessor finishes. You cannot start the roof until the walls are built. This covers about 95% of all links. Finish-to-Finish (FF): the successor cannot finish until the predecessor finishes. An editor can start proofreading while a chapter is still being written, but cannot finish until the writing is finished. Start-to-Start (SS): the successor cannot start until the predecessor starts. You cannot document a test until the test has started. Start-to-Finish (SF) is the rarest: the successor cannot finish until the predecessor starts.
steps.example: Finish-to-Start: you cannot put the roof on the house (successor) until the walls are built (predecessor). This is the most common link, used about 95% of the time.
keyPoints: Burst = more than one successor; merge = more than one predecessor.
Finish-to-Start is by far the most common link (about 95%).
Finish-to-Finish, Start-to-Start, and Start-to-Finish are the other three.
Start-to-Finish is the rarest.

steps.title: Quick check: relationship types
steps.body: Let's test the most common link type.

quiz.question: In a Finish-to-Start (FS) relationship, when can the successor start?
options: As soon as the predecessor starts
Only after the predecessor is finished
At the same moment as the predecessor
Only after the successor itself finishes
quiz.explanation: Finish-to-Start means the successor cannot start until the predecessor is finished, like waiting for walls before the roof.

steps.title: Dependencies, leads, and lags
steps.body:
A relationship type shows the start-finish link. A dependency shows how fixed that link is.

A mandatory dependency means the successor must always follow the predecessor. You cannot build the second floor until the first floor is built. A discretionary dependency means the successor should follow the predecessor, but sometimes they can happen at the same time; then you check the extra risk. It is best to paint before laying carpet, but not required.

An external dependency waits on something outside your project that you cannot control, like a permit from local government. An internal dependency is inside your team's control, like assembling software code before testing it.

Two more useful words: a lead is time a successor can start before its predecessor finishes. A lead of three days means the successor may begin three days early. A lag is time a successor must wait after the predecessor ends, for example, waiting for paint to dry or concrete to cure before the next step.
steps.example: Lag: after you pour concrete, you must wait two days for it to cure (harden) before you build on top. That waiting time is a lag of two days.
keyPoints: Mandatory = must follow; discretionary = should follow, can overlap.
External = outside your control; internal = inside your team's control.
Lead = start early, before the predecessor finishes.
Lag = wait time after the predecessor finishes.

steps.title: Teach it back: lead vs lag
steps.body: Now teach this in your own words. Imagine a friend who has never heard these words. In two or three simple sentences, explain the difference between a lead and a lag. Give one everyday example of each (for instance, starting a task early, or waiting for something to dry). Keep it short and clear.
keyPoints: A lead lets the successor start early.
A lag adds a wait after the predecessor.
Use a real example for each.

steps.title: Estimating activity resources
steps.body:
After you order your activities, you estimate the resources each one needs. Resources can be people or machines. Look at each activity and think how long each resource must be assigned to finish it. When you can, ask the people who will do the work; they give the best estimate.

We usually estimate resources before time. Why? Because time depends on resources. Two people often finish a task in half the time of one person. So you must know your resources before you can guess the time well.

Sometimes a project is resource constrained. This means you only have a fixed number of people or machines. Then you do not estimate resources, because they are already set; instead, the time must adjust to fit the resources you have.

By laying resources over your schedule, you see resource loading: when each resource is needed. This gives early warning of times when you may not have enough people. It also shows when people will sit idle. If you plan too much work at once, you can move non-critical work to another time. This is called resource leveling.
steps.example: Your schedule needs five designers in week two but you only have three. Resource leveling means moving some non-urgent design work to a later week so you never need more than three at once.
keyPoints: Resources are people or machines.
Estimate resources first, because time depends on resources.
Resource constrained = fixed resources, so time must adjust.
Resource leveling moves work to smooth out demand.

steps.title: Estimating activity duration
steps.body:
Now you know your activities, their order, and the resources you have. With all this, you can estimate the duration, which means how long each activity takes. You can also estimate the time for groups of activities and for the whole project.

Use the estimating methods that fit your project and the quality of information you have. Do not force a method that does not suit your case.

Some activities are time constrained. This means they must be finished within a fixed period. In that case, you must supply enough resources to meet the deadline. Remember, time depends on resources, so more work in less time usually needs more people or machines.

Watch out for a hard case: an activity that is both time constrained and resource constrained. If you do not have enough resources to finish inside the fixed time, something has to change. You must either extend the time or add more resources. You cannot keep both limits fixed and still succeed.
steps.example: A report is due Friday (time constrained), but you also have only one writer (resource constrained). If one writer cannot finish by Friday, you must move the deadline or add a second writer.
keyPoints: Duration is how long an activity takes.
Time constrained means a fixed deadline.
Meeting a tight deadline usually needs more resources.
If time and resources are both fixed and too tight, one must change.

steps.title: The network schedule, Gantt chart, and slack
steps.body:
The final step joins everything: all activities, their order, the resources assigned, and each duration. This becomes your project schedule baseline, the saved plan you measure real progress against. The baseline includes the first plan plus all approved changes.

You can draw network diagrams by hand, but it is slow. The easy way is software and the popular Gantt chart, created by Henry Gantt. A Gantt chart uses bars to show how long each activity lasts and arrows to show the links. It shows the time for single activities, for sections, and for the whole project. It is also a great way to communicate: people can quickly see the plan and how the project is going.

Once your diagram becomes a schedule, you can see the slack (also called float; here two words mean the same thing). Free slack is how long an activity can be delayed before it affects the next activity. Total slack is how long an activity can be delayed before it affects the whole project's finish date.
steps.example: A Gantt chart shows a bar for "paint walls" that is 3 days long, with an arrow to "hang pictures." If painting has 2 days of free slack, it can start 2 days late without delaying the pictures.
keyPoints: The finished schedule becomes the baseline.
A Gantt chart uses bars for duration and arrows for links.
Slack and float mean the same thing.
Free slack affects the next task; total slack affects the whole project.

steps.title: The critical path
steps.body:
The critical path is the path through your schedule that has no slack (zero float). You can have more than one critical path, but each one has zero slack.

Why does it matter? Because it is the path of greatest risk. With no slack, a delay in any activity on this path delays the whole project. So we find the critical path to know where to pay close attention.

A project can have many paths from start to finish. To find the critical path, you do a schedule network analysis. First you list every path. Then you do a forward pass, working left to right, to find the early start and early finish of each task, and the total project duration. Then you do a backward pass, working right to left, to find the late start and late finish. When you subtract late start from late finish, you get the total slack for each task.

The critical path is made of the activities with zero total float. In the book's example, the project lasted 25 days and the critical path was A-C-E-F-H.
steps.example: In the book's diagram, after the forward and backward passes, the tasks with zero float were A, C, E, F, and H. So the critical path is A-C-E-F-H, and any delay there delays the whole project.
keyPoints: The critical path has zero slack.
A delay on it delays the whole project.
Forward pass finds early dates and total duration.
Backward pass finds late dates and total slack.

steps.title: Quick check: the critical path
steps.body: Let's confirm you understand why the critical path is special.

quiz.question: What is true about the critical path?
options: It is the path with the most slack, so delays are safe
It has zero slack, so any delay on it delays the whole project
It is always the shortest list of activities
It only exists in projects with no deadlines
quiz.explanation: The critical path has no slack, so a delay in any of its activities pushes back the whole project.

steps.title: Teach it back: the critical path
steps.body: Explain the critical path to a teammate who is new to project management. In two or three simple sentences, say what the critical path is, why it has zero slack, and why the project manager watches it closely. If you can, mention what happens if a task on the critical path is delayed.
keyPoints: Define the critical path in plain words.
Explain zero slack.
Say why a delay there hurts the whole project.

steps.title: Reducing project duration
steps.body:
Sometimes the client wants the project done faster than your estimates allow. Then you must find ways to shorten the duration. Every method has a trade-off in cost or risk, so choose with care.

Crashing means adding resources, like extra people or machines, to finish faster. It usually costs more money. Fast-tracking means doing activities in parallel (at the same time) that you would normally do one after another (in series). This can save time but adds risk, because tasks overlap before earlier work is fully done.

Other options include: outsourcing work (more flexible but costs more and you may lose knowledge); scheduling overtime (costs more); keeping a stable team so skills stay strong; doing a task twice, first fast then correctly (which can cost a lot); critical-chain, which keeps work flowing through bottlenecks; reducing scope (do less); or compromising quality (a lower quality result). Each choice affects cost, risk, and quality, so weigh them before you decide.
steps.example: To finish a wall two days sooner, crashing means hiring a second bricklayer (more cost). Fast-tracking means starting to paint the finished end of the wall while the other end is still being built (more risk).
keyPoints: Crashing adds resources; it usually costs more money.
Fast-tracking runs tasks in parallel; it adds risk.
Other ways: outsourcing, overtime, reduce scope, and more.
Every method has a cost, risk, or quality trade-off.

steps.title: Build a mini schedule
steps.body:
Time to practice. Pick a small real task with 4 to 6 steps (for example, making dinner, or setting up a laptop). Do this:

1. Write each step as a short activity.
2. Put them in sequence, marking the predecessor and successor for each link.
3. For at least one link, choose a relationship type (most will be Finish-to-Start).
4. Add a lag somewhere if it fits (for example, waiting for water to boil).
5. Guess a duration in minutes for each step, then add up the total.
6. Point to the one path where any delay would delay the whole task. That is your critical path.

Write it out as a simple list or a row of sticky-note style boxes with arrows.
keyPoints: List activities and put them in order.
Mark predecessors, successors, and one relationship type.
Estimate durations and find the total.
Identify the critical path.

steps.title: Monitoring and controlling the schedule
steps.body:
Making a schedule does not mean the project will follow it exactly. But a good schedule shows how things should go, and that alone helps. Its greatest benefit is that you can now track what really happens against what you planned.

To do this you need work performance information: what you have actually done versus what you planned. You can often get this by simple observation. For each activity you check two things: effort (the resources used) and time. A simple way to record progress is by percentage complete: 0% not started, 25% just started, 50% about halfway, 75% well on the way, and 100% finished.

With these figures you compare real progress to your baseline and forecast the future. If you are off track, use your change control process to record and approve changes, then update the baseline. Always ask the root cause: Were the estimates wrong? Are resources not working as planned? Did surprises cause delays? Good answers help you fix the problem and build lessons learned for future projects.
steps.example: A task is "about halfway," so you mark it 50% complete. The software then forecasts a new finish date, and you see the project may run two days late unless you act.
keyPoints: Track real progress against the baseline.
Assess both effort (resources) and time.
Use 0, 25, 50, 75, 100% to record progress simply.
Find the root cause and record lessons learned.

title: Costs and Budget
intro: Money is one of the biggest worries on any project. In this module you will learn what project costs really are, how to estimate them, and how to build a budget you can trust. Then you will learn how to watch your spending as the work happens. We will also make earned value simple, so terms like EV, PV, AC, SPI, and CPI stop feeling scary. Everything is grounded in "The Professional Project Manager" by Sean Whitaker, with easy examples like building a house. Take it step by step. You do not need to be good at math. You just need clear ideas and a little practice.

steps.title: What are project costs?
steps.body:
The question sounds easy: what will your project cost? But the answer is not simple. Project cost is the total money needed to finish all the work in your scope. Scope means everything you promised to do.

Some costs are clear. The people who do the work cost money. The materials you use cost money. These are always part of the project cost.

Other costs are less clear. A company has shared costs like rent, electricity, marketing, and office staff. Should your project pay a fair share of these? You can do this in two ways. You can track the real costs your project uses. Or you can add a fair percentage of the total company cost to your project.

There is also profit. If profit is not already inside your price, you may need to add a fair and clear profit amount. Your accountant can help you choose a fair method.

The key idea: decide what counts as a project cost and what does not. Then use the same rule for every project. This makes your numbers fair and easy to compare.
steps.example: A house project pays workers and buys wood and cement. The builder also adds 8% of the price to cover office rent and admin staff, plus a small profit margin.
keyPoints: Project cost = money to finish all the scope of work.
Include clear costs (people, materials) and a fair share of shared costs.
Add a fair, clear profit margin if it is not already included.
Use the same cost rule for every project so numbers stay fair.

steps.title: Good estimates start with good scope
steps.body:
An estimate is your best guess of the cost, made before the work happens. The quality of your estimate depends on your scope statement. The scope statement describes what the project will deliver.

If your scope is unclear or has gaps, your cost estimate will be unclear too. If your scope is fully defined, your estimate will be much more accurate. This is a simple but powerful rule: unclear scope means an unclear cost.

There are two main ways to estimate. Top-down means you look at the whole project at a high level and guess one big number. Bottom-up means you break the project into small parts, estimate each part, and add them up.

Bottom-up is almost always more accurate. When you estimate many small pieces, small errors tend to cancel out. When you guess one big number, one wrong assumption can throw off everything.

So, before you estimate costs, spend time making your scope clear. Break the work into small pieces first. Then estimate each piece. Good scope work now saves you money and stress later.
steps.example: Instead of guessing 'the house will cost about $100,000', the builder estimates each part: excavation, floor, walls, roof, interior. Adding these gives a stronger number.
keyPoints: An estimate is your best cost guess before the work starts.
Clear scope gives clear estimates; unclear scope gives unclear estimates.
Bottom-up (small parts added up) beats top-down (one big guess).
Break work into small pieces before you estimate.

steps.title: Adding up costs (aggregating)
steps.body:
Aggregating just means adding smaller numbers into bigger totals. This is how you build a full project cost from the bottom up.

Start with your WBS. WBS means Work Breakdown Structure. It is a chart that splits the project into work packages and activities. A work package is a small, clear piece of work.

Give each activity a cost estimate. Then group activities under headings and make subtotals. Then add all the subtotals to get the total project cost.

Here is the house example from the book. The Design Process costs $15,000. Inside it: concept plans $5,000, owner approval $0, construction plans $10,000. The Building Process costs $87,500. Inside it: site excavation $15,000, floor $25,000, walls $10,000, roof $7,500, interior $30,000.

Add the two groups: $15,000 plus $87,500 equals a total house project cost of $102,500.

Notice how the total is built from small, real numbers. Each number can be checked and questioned. This is why bottom-up estimating gives you totals you can defend and explain to your client.
steps.example: Design Process ($15,000) + Building Process ($87,500) = $102,500 total. Each group is the sum of its own small activities.
keyPoints: Aggregating = adding small costs into subtotals, then into a total.
WBS splits the project into small work packages and activities.
Estimate each piece, subtotal by group, then sum the groups.
House example totals $102,500 built from small parts.

steps.title: Teach it back: estimating
steps.body: Time to teach. Imagine a friend asks you: 'Why is breaking the project into small parts better than just guessing one big number for the cost?' Answer in your own simple words. Try to mention the scope statement and how small errors behave when you add many pieces. Give one short example, like the house build, to make it real.

steps.title: Cost contingency: money for uncertainty
steps.body:
No estimate is perfect. There is always some uncertainty. A contingency, also called a reserve, is extra money added to cover this uncertainty. It is not random padding. It should be based on real reasons.

There are two types. The contingency reserve covers known uncertainty from your scope and risk work. You, the project manager, usually control it. The management reserve is a separate pool of money held by senior managers. It is used only for truly unexpected events that nobody could plan for.

How big should contingency be? It is best shown as a percentage of your estimated cost. When uncertainty is very high, it can be large, even 50 to 100 percent. When uncertainty is low, it can be small, around 5 to 10 percent.

You can estimate uncertainty for each work package, then add them up for the whole project. If the total contingency looks too high, that is a signal. It usually means your scope is still unclear. Spend more time defining the scope, and the contingency can shrink.

Always try to reduce the reasons for contingency, until you may not need it at all.
steps.example: A builder is unsure about the ground under a new house, so they add a 10% contingency reserve. After a soil test removes the doubt, they lower it to 3%.
keyPoints: Contingency (reserve) is planned extra money for uncertainty, not random padding.
Contingency reserve covers known uncertainty; PM usually controls it.
Management reserve is held by senior managers for truly unforeseen events.
High contingency often means unclear scope; better scope shrinks it.

steps.title: Quiz: which reserve?
steps.body: Check your understanding of the two reserve types.

quiz.question: Senior managers keep a pool of money for truly unexpected events across projects. What is this called?
options: Contingency reserve
Management reserve
Profit margin
Budget at completion
quiz.explanation: The management reserve is held by senior management for genuinely unforeseen events, while the contingency reserve covers known uncertainty.

steps.title: From cost to budget
steps.body:
People often mix up 'cost' and 'budget', but the difference is simple. Project cost is the total money you forecast to finish the project. Project budget is that cost spread over time.

In other words, the budget shows not just how much, but when. It shows when you will need money to pay bills, and when any income will come in.

How do you build a budget? Once you have your project schedule, it is easy. Take the cost of each item in the schedule. Then group these costs by time period: by day, week, or month. Now you can see your planned spending over time.

Accountants love this. It tells them when money must go out and when money should come in. They use all the project budgets to plan the company's cash flow.

If you draw the total spending over time as a line, it often looks like the letter S. This is called an S curve. Spending is slow at the start, faster in the middle, and slow again at the end. That is the normal shape of most projects.
steps.example: The house costs $102,500 in total. The budget shows $10,000 in month 1, $40,000 in month 2, and so on, so the builder knows when to pay each bill.
keyPoints: Cost = total money to finish. Budget = that cost spread over time.
The budget shows when money is needed and when income arrives.
Build it by grouping schedule costs into time periods.
Cumulative spending often forms an S curve: slow, fast, slow.

steps.title: The baseline: BAC and PV
steps.body:
Once your budget is approved, it becomes your baseline. A baseline is your saved plan. You will measure your real performance against it. If you do not save a plan, you have nothing to compare against later.

Two important terms come from this baseline. The first is BAC, the Budget at Completion. This is the total budget you forecast for the whole project. It is your original 'this is what it will cost' number.

The second is PV, the Planned Value. Planned Value is how much value you planned to have earned by a certain date. In simple words, it is how much work you planned to finish by now, measured in money.

Here is a helpful fact. The total Planned Value for the whole project equals the BAC. So you can find PV at any point by asking: how far through the plan should I be right now? Then read that money value from your baseline.

A quick, rougher way: if you are 6 months into a 12-month project, you are 50% through the time, so PV is 50% of BAC. This is less accurate but fast.
steps.example: A project has a BAC of $100,000. At the halfway point in time, the plan says you should have completed half the work, so PV is $50,000.
keyPoints: Baseline = your saved, approved plan to measure against.
BAC (Budget at Completion) = total forecast budget for the project.
PV (Planned Value) = money value of work you planned to finish by now.
Total PV for the whole project equals BAC.

steps.title: Why simple numbers can fool you
steps.body:
Here is a story from the book. A project manager says: 'We are 5 weeks into a 10-week project, and we have only spent 40% of the budget. Everything is great!'

Is it really great? We cannot know yet. They told you the time and the money spent. But they did not tell you how much work they planned to finish, and how much work is actually done.

Maybe they should have finished half the work by now, but they only finished 30% of it. In that case there is no happy underspend at all. They spent 40% of the budget to get only 30% of the work done. That is bad, not good.

This is the whole reason earned value exists. Looking at money spent alone can trick you. You must compare three things at the same time: what you planned to do, what you actually did, and what you actually paid.

Those three things are Planned Value (PV), Earned Value (EV), and Actual Cost (AC). When you compare all three, you see the true story of your project, not a comforting half-truth.
steps.example: 40% of budget spent sounds fine, until you learn only 30% of the work is done. Now you know the project is really behind and over-spending.
keyPoints: Money spent alone can hide the real project status.
Always compare planned work, actual work, and actual cost together.
These three are PV (planned), EV (actual work done), AC (actual cost).
This comparison is the heart of earned value management.

steps.title: The three key numbers: PV, EV, AC
steps.body:
Earned value uses three main numbers. Learn these well, because everything else is built from them.

PV, Planned Value: the money value of the work you planned to finish by now. It comes from your baseline plan.

EV, Earned Value: the value of the work you have actually finished. Important: this is not what you paid. It is the original planned value of the work that is now done. If a task was budgeted at $5,000 and it is finished, you 'earned' $5,000 of value, even if it cost more or less.

AC, Actual Cost: the real money you actually paid for the work done so far. You get this from your accounts system.

One useful tip from the book. When you record Actual Cost, remove the value of materials still sitting in stock, not yet used. Sometimes you buy materials early to avoid price rises. That money shows in your accounts, but the work is not done yet. Counting it as AC makes your project look worse than it really is. So do stock takes and take that value out.
steps.example: A task planned at $5,000 is done. EV = $5,000 (the planned value earned). But you actually paid $6,000, so AC = $6,000 for that task.
keyPoints: PV = value of work planned by now.
EV = value of work actually finished (planned value, not what you paid).
AC = real money actually paid so far.
Remove unused stock from AC so results are not skewed.

steps.title: Teach it back: EV vs AC
steps.body: Explain this to a beginner in your own words: what is the difference between Earned Value (EV) and Actual Cost (AC)? Use one small example, like a $5,000 task that is finished but cost $6,000. Make it clear why EV uses the planned value of finished work, not the money you actually paid.

steps.title: Variances and indexes: CV, CPI, SV, SPI
steps.body:
Now we compare the three numbers to see how we are doing. All these formulas start with EV.

Cost Variance (CV) = EV minus AC. It shows if you are over or under budget in dollars. Positive is good (under budget). Negative is bad (over budget).

Cost Performance Index (CPI) = EV divided by AC. A variance in dollars alone does not tell you if it is big or small for your project. The index fixes that. CPI above 1 is good; below 1 is bad. A CPI of 1.1 means for every $1 you spend, you get $1.10 of value.

Schedule Variance (SV) = EV minus PV. It shows if you are ahead or behind the plan. Positive is good (ahead); negative is bad (behind).

Schedule Performance Index (SPI) = EV divided by PV. SPI above 1 is good (ahead of time); below 1 is bad (behind). An SPI of 0.95 means for each planned day of work, you get 0.95 days done.

Easy memory trick: variances use minus, indexes use divide. Cost formulas end in AC, schedule formulas end in PV.
keyPoints: CV = EV − AC; CPI = EV ÷ AC (cost formulas end in AC).
SV = EV − PV; SPI = EV ÷ PV (schedule formulas end in PV).
Above 1 (or positive) is good; below 1 (or negative) is bad.
Memory trick: variance = minus, index = divide.

steps.title: Quiz: reading CPI
steps.body: A project reports a CPI of 0.8. What does this tell you?

quiz.question: Your project has a Cost Performance Index (CPI) of 0.8. What does this mean?
options: The project is under budget and doing well
The project is over budget; you get only $0.80 of value per $1 spent
The project is ahead of schedule
The project is exactly on budget
quiz.explanation: CPI = EV/AC. A CPI below 1 means over budget; 0.8 means each dollar spent returns only 80 cents of value.

steps.title: Build: calculate the earned value numbers
steps.body:
Practice with real numbers. A project has these values today:

BAC = $100,000
PV (planned value now) = $50,000
EV (work actually done) = $40,000
AC (money actually paid) = $45,000

Your task: calculate these four results and say if each is good or bad.
1. Cost Variance: CV = EV − AC
2. Schedule Variance: SV = EV − PV
3. Cost Performance Index: CPI = EV ÷ AC
4. Schedule Performance Index: SPI = EV ÷ PV

Write each formula, put in the numbers, and give a one-line meaning (over/under budget, ahead/behind schedule). Check your answers: CV = −$5,000 (over budget), SV = −$10,000 (behind schedule), CPI ≈ 0.89 (over budget), SPI = 0.8 (behind schedule). This project is in trouble on both cost and schedule.

steps.title: Forecasting the finish: EAC, ETC, VAC
steps.body:
Earned value also helps you look forward. You use your performance so far to guess the final cost.

EAC, Estimate at Completion: your new guess of the total final cost. The book gives several formulas. The simplest is EAC = BAC ÷ CPI. If your cost performance so far will likely continue, this works well. Another useful one is EAC = AC + ((BAC − EV) ÷ (CPI × SPI)), which uses both cost and schedule performance and is often more accurate later in the project.

ETC, Estimate to Complete: how much more money you still need to finish. The easy formula is ETC = EAC − AC.

VAC, Variance at Completion: the gap between your first budget and your new forecast. VAC = BAC − EAC. Positive is good (cheaper than planned); negative is bad (more than planned).

A practical tip from the author: early in the project, use EAC = BAC ÷ CPI, because early data is rough. After the halfway point, switch to the fuller formula that uses both CPI and SPI. Remember, forecasts are only as good as the data you feed in.
steps.example: BAC = $100,000 and CPI = 0.8. Then EAC = 100,000 ÷ 0.8 = $125,000. VAC = 100,000 − 125,000 = −$25,000, so you now expect to spend $25,000 more than planned.
keyPoints: EAC = new forecast of total cost; simplest form is BAC ÷ CPI.
ETC = EAC − AC = money still needed to finish.
VAC = BAC − EAC = gap between first budget and new forecast.
Use the simple EAC early; use the CPI×SPI formula after halfway.

steps.title: Quiz: cost vs budget
steps.body: Make sure the difference is clear in your mind.

quiz.question: What is the difference between 'project cost' and 'project budget'?
options: They mean exactly the same thing
Cost is the total money to finish; budget is that cost spread over time
Budget is always bigger than cost because it includes profit
Cost is only for materials; budget is only for people
quiz.explanation: Project cost is the total forecast to finish; the budget is that cost spread over time, showing when money goes out and comes in.

steps.title: Monitoring costs and change control
steps.body:
Planning is not the same as controlling. A good plan does not guarantee good results. Its real power is that it gives you a baseline to compare against as the work happens.

To compare planned versus actual spending, you need a solid tracking system. Use your invoice and payment records to capture real spending, either when money goes out or when it is owed. A loose, messy cost system is a common cause of poor cost control.

A simple method is a spreadsheet: put actual spend next to planned spend and see the difference. A more advanced method is earned value, which you just learned.

Nothing matters more here than controlling changes. Many arguments happen at the final invoice, when a client says 'I never approved that cost.' So record every change request as a variation to the original scope, and estimate the cost impact of each one.

Remember, costs are not only about materials. Changes to time, people, quality, risks, or contract type can all change cost. Get every change approved in writing by the client or senior management. Changes can lower costs too, not only raise them.
steps.example: A client asks for marble floors instead of tiles. The PM writes a variation, estimates +$8,000, and gets written approval before ordering. No surprise on the final invoice.
keyPoints: A plan's power is giving a baseline to compare actual spend against.
Use a solid invoice/payment system; messy tracking causes poor control.
Record every change as a variation and estimate its cost impact.
Get all changes approved in writing; changes can lower or raise cost.

steps.title: Quiz: what does EV measure?
steps.body: One last check on the core idea of earned value.

quiz.question: Earned Value (EV) measures which of these?
options: The actual money you paid for the work done
The planned value of the work you have actually completed
The total budget for the whole project
The extra money kept for uncertainty
quiz.explanation: EV is the original planned value of the work actually completed, not the money paid (that is AC) and not the whole budget (that is BAC).

title: Managing Risk
intro: Every project faces uncertainty. Some surprises are bad, and some are good. This module teaches you how to handle both. You will learn what risk really means, how to find risks, how to judge them (quickly and with numbers), how to plan your response, and how to keep watching for new risks. The ideas come from "The Professional Project Manager" by Sean Whitaker. By the end, you will be able to build a simple risk register and a contingency budget with confidence.

steps.title: What is project risk?
steps.body:
The easiest way to understand risk is to swap the word "risk" for the word "uncertainty." A risk is something that might happen, but we are not sure it will. When there is more uncertainty in a project, the chance of failure goes up.

Here is the key idea. Risk can be bad or good. Most people think risk only means bad news. But that is only half the story. A negative risk (also called a threat) is a bad event you want to avoid or reduce. A positive risk (also called an opportunity) is a good event you want to make more likely.

The best way to deal with uncertainty is to plan for it early. This is called project risk management. It is not something you do once and forget. It is on-going. You keep checking known risks, looking for new ones, and crossing off risks that have passed. Done well, risk management really does raise your chance of success.
steps.example: A builder worries that a hurricane could stop work (a threat). At the same time, a supplier might offer a big discount if the builder orders early (an opportunity). Both are risks.
keyPoints: Risk means uncertainty, something that might happen.
Risks can be negative (threats) or positive (opportunities).
Risk management is on-going, not a one-time job.

steps.title: Start with a risk management plan
steps.body:
Before you hunt for risks, plan how you will manage risk. This is called your risk management plan. It is a simple guide that says how much effort you will spend and what steps you will follow.

Your plan should match three things: the size of the project, how complex it is, and how much risk your organization is willing to accept. This last idea is called risk tolerance. A company that hates surprises will want more careful planning. A small, simple project needs less.

Write it down and keep it standard. Your plan might include a checklist of steps, the tools you will use, the results you expect, and who is responsible for each part. When the plan is ready, you move through the stages of risk management: finding risks, analyzing them, planning responses, and monitoring.

If your project is very large and full of uncertainty, you may bring in a risk professional to help. But for most projects, a clear plan that fits the work is enough.
steps.example: For a small office move, the manager writes a one-page plan: brainstorm risks once, score them 1 to 5, and review the list every week. That is enough effort for that size of project.
keyPoints: Plan your risk approach before you start finding risks.
Match the effort to project size, complexity, and risk tolerance.
Write down steps, tools, expected results, and who is responsible.

steps.title: The project risk register
steps.body:
The main tool for managing risk is the risk register. This is a table that lists every risk you found, what could happen if it occurs, and what you plan to do about it.

As you fill in the register, you move from left to right. You start with identifying risks. Then you add qualitative analysis (a quick score). Then quantitative analysis (numbers in dollars or days). Then your planned responses. You do not have to do this in a strict order. Like most project work, it can be repeated as often as needed.

Not every possible risk belongs in the register. Think about a meteor hitting your site. The chance is tiny, so you leave it out, unless your project is a spacewalk outside the space station. Then you would include it. The rule is balance. Add risks that are sensible and useful to track. Bigger and more complex projects deserve more effort in the register.

Remember, the register is a living document. It is only as useful as the care you give it.
steps.example: A risk register row might read: Risk = heavy rain delays concrete; Consequence = 3-day delay; Type = threat; Response = pour concrete earlier in the schedule.
keyPoints: The risk register lists risks, their impacts, and your plans.
It grows from identification, to analysis, to responses.
Include sensible risks, not every wild possibility.

steps.title: Quick check: threat or opportunity?
steps.body: Let's check the big idea from the last steps.

quiz.question: A supplier might finish early and let your team start the next task sooner. What kind of risk is this?
options: A threat, because any surprise is bad
A positive risk (opportunity) you want to make more likely
Not a risk at all, because it helps you
A negative risk you must avoid
quiz.explanation: An uncertain event that would help the project is a positive risk, or opportunity, and you try to increase its chance of happening.

steps.title: Identifying risks: the risk breakdown structure
steps.body:
The first real step is to find all the risks that could affect your project. There are many ways to do this. One strong tool is the risk breakdown structure, often shortened to RBS.

An RBS helps you sort risks into groups. Start at the top with the whole project. Then break it into big categories of risk. Ask yourself: are there risks around finance, technology, weather, people, design, or buying materials? Next, break each big category into smaller sub-categories. Then think of specific risk events inside each one.

Working in groups makes this stronger. Ask your team, the client, contractors, suppliers, and industry experts to add ideas and check your list. Different people see different risks.

The goal is to be proactive, which means thinking ahead instead of reacting later. Once you have your categories, you can look inside each one and list the real risks, both good and bad.
steps.example: Under the category "Weather," a construction team lists sub-risks: hurricane, heavy rain, and extreme heat. Under "People," they list: key worker gets sick, or a subcontractor quits.
keyPoints: The RBS sorts risks into categories and sub-categories.
Start broad, then break down into specific risk events.
Involve your team and experts to catch more risks.

steps.title: More ways to find risks
steps.body:
The RBS is not the only tool. Here are four more good ways to find risks.

Lessons learned and historical information. This is often the best source. If someone documented risks from a past project, you can reuse their risk register and notes. Did they miss any risks? Did their responses work? A good lessons-learned report helps you move fast.

Risk professionals. On a large, complex project, an expert can help. They know the industry, can spot risks you might miss, and can help put numbers to them.

Brainstorming. Get the team and a few knowledgeable people together. Bring coffee, appoint a neutral facilitator, and let ideas flow freely. Some ideas will be silly, but you sort those out later. Record everything and group the ideas.

Ask the experts. You can simply ask experts for their views, by email or in person. In a group, watch out for peer pressure and groupthink, where people just agree with each other. The Delphi technique avoids this by asking experts for their opinions anonymously.
steps.example: A software team runs a one-hour brainstorm and lists 30 possible risks. They also read the lessons-learned file from last year's project and find two risks they had forgotten.
keyPoints: Lessons learned from past projects is often the best source.
Brainstorming works best with a neutral facilitator.
The Delphi technique polls experts anonymously to avoid groupthink.

steps.title: Teach it back: finding risks
steps.body: Imagine a friend is starting their first project and asks, "How do I even begin finding risks?" In your own simple words, explain two or three ways they could do it. Try to mention the risk breakdown structure and at least one group method like brainstorming or the Delphi technique. Say why involving other people helps.

steps.title: Describe consequences and label each risk
steps.body:
Finding a risk is not enough. For each risk event, you also need to describe what could happen if it occurs. This is the consequence, or impact. One risk can have more than one consequence.

You also label each risk as positive or negative. A positive risk is an opportunity. A negative risk is a threat. Writing this label in your register keeps your thinking clear, because good risks and bad risks need different responses.

Some risks are also urgent. An urgent risk could happen soon, so you must act quickly. A less urgent risk is further off in time, so you have more room to plan. Noting urgency helps you decide what to work on first.

So for every risk, capture the event, its consequences, whether it is a threat or opportunity, and how urgent it is. This turns a plain list of worries into a useful register you can act on.
