steps.example: Risk = hurricane. Consequences = site flooded and 2-week delay. Type = negative (threat). Urgency = very urgent, because it is hurricane season right now.
keyPoints: For each risk, write its possible consequences.
Label every risk as a threat or an opportunity.
Note how urgent the risk is to help set priorities.

steps.title: Quick check: finding risks
steps.body: Let's test the tools for finding risks.

quiz.question: Your experts might just agree with the loudest person in the room. Which method helps avoid this groupthink?
options: A face-to-face group meeting
The Delphi technique (anonymous polling of experts)
The risk breakdown structure
Reading last year's budget
quiz.explanation: The Delphi technique asks experts for their opinions anonymously, so peer pressure and groupthink do not shape the answers.

steps.title: Qualitative analysis: scoring quickly
steps.body:
After you find your risks, you analyze them. The first kind is qualitative analysis. This is a quick, subjective way to score each risk. Subjective means it is based on people's judgment, not on hard research.

You rate two things for each risk: probability (how likely it is) and impact (how much it would hurt or help). You use a simple scale, for example 1 to 5. For probability, 1 means very unlikely and 5 means almost certain. For impact, 1 means almost no effect and 5 means a major effect.

Then you multiply the two scores together. A risk scored 4 for probability and 5 for impact gets 20. Now sort all your risks from highest score to lowest. The top risks are the ones most likely to happen and to hurt the most.

Because this process is subjective, it helps to write short descriptions of what each number means. This makes scoring more consistent across your team. Qualitative analysis is fast, so it is a great way to decide which risks deserve deeper study.
steps.example: A team scores a hurricane: probability 5 out of 10 and impact 10 out of 10. Multiplied together, that is 50 out of a possible 100, a very high score.
keyPoints: Qualitative analysis is a quick, judgment-based score.
Rate probability and impact, then multiply them.
Sort by score to find your top-priority risks.

steps.title: Quick check: qualitative scoring
steps.body: Let's check how the quick score works.

quiz.question: In qualitative analysis, how do you get a single score for a risk?
options: Add the probability and impact scores
Multiply the probability score by the impact score
Use only the impact score
Divide impact by probability
quiz.explanation: You multiply the probability score by the impact score, then sort risks from highest to lowest to find your priorities.

steps.title: Quantitative analysis: putting numbers on risk
steps.body:
Quantitative analysis is the deeper study. Instead of a subjective guess, you do real research to find the actual probability and the actual impact of a risk, measured in dollars or days.

This takes time and money, so you usually do it only on the top risks from your qualitative ranking. The method is simple: multiply the real probability by the real impact. For example, a 10% chance of a $10,000 loss means you should set aside $1,000. That $1,000 goes into your contingency budget, which is money kept in reserve for risks.

When you add up the reserve for every risk, you get a contingency budget that is transparent and defensible. That means you can clearly show how you got the number, which builds trust.

As the project runs, risks either happen or pass. If a risk does not happen, you release the money you set aside for it. Because this work needs special skills, many teams ask a risk professional or an actuary (an expert in measuring risk with numbers) to get a reliable result.
steps.example: A weather bureau says there is a 43% chance of a hurricane this season. The accounts team estimates the impact at $10,000. So the team sets aside 0.43 x $10,000 = $4,300 for that risk.
keyPoints: Quantitative analysis uses researched numbers, not guesses.
Multiply real probability by real impact (dollars or days).
Adding these up builds a defensible contingency budget.

steps.title: Build a mini contingency budget
steps.body:
Practice the quantitative method with three risks. For each one, multiply the probability by the dollar impact to find the reserve, then add them up for a total contingency budget.

Risk A: 20% chance, $5,000 impact.
Risk B: 50% chance, $2,000 impact.
Risk C: 10% chance, $30,000 impact.

Write out each calculation (probability x impact), then add the three reserves together for the total. Finally, in one sentence, explain what should happen to Risk B's reserve if the project finishes and that risk never occurs.

steps.title: Planning responses to negative risks
steps.body:
Once you know your key risks, you plan how to respond. A response is your plan of action for a risk. You can have more than one response for a single risk. For negative risks (threats), there are four choices.

Avoid. Do not do the activity that creates the risk. For example, avoid heavy traffic by taking the back roads instead of the motorway.

Transfer. Make the risk someone else's problem. The classic example is buying insurance. You still face the event, but someone else pays the cost.

Mitigate. Reduce either the chance or the impact of the risk. For example, build a house to a strong standard so a hurricane does less damage. You cannot stop the hurricane, but you can lessen the harm.

Accept. Sometimes the cost of responding is greater than the cost of the risk itself. In that case, the smart choice is to accept the risk and do nothing special, but stay aware of it.

Good responses act as a checklist. They tell you what to prepare before the risk and what to do while it is happening.
steps.example: For the hurricane threat, the team cannot avoid or transfer it fully, so they mitigate: they build to a stronger specification. This lowers the impact from 10 down to 4.
keyPoints: Four responses for threats: avoid, transfer, mitigate, accept.
Transfer means shifting the cost, such as buying insurance.
Accept a risk when responding would cost more than the risk.

steps.title: Planning responses to positive risks
steps.body:
Positive risks are opportunities, good events that would help your project. You do not want to avoid these. You want to make them more likely. There are four choices for positive risks.

Exploit. Remove the uncertainty and make sure the good event happens. If a project could be more profitable, find the reasons why and keep or increase them.

Share. Work with another party to improve the chance of the good event. For example, partner with a helpful vendor to win a bid together.

Enhance. Increase the chance or the impact of the good event. The simple example: buy more lottery tickets to increase your chance of winning.

Accept. If the cost of chasing the opportunity is greater than the benefit, just accept it and let it happen on its own if it does.

One warning. Some risks are unforeseen, meaning you could not reasonably have predicted them. When one hits with no plan ready, create a quick workaround and gather everyone into a "war room" to decide your response as fast as possible.
steps.example: A vendor realizes that teaming up with a partner company would improve their chance of winning a big contract. Working together is the "share" response to a positive risk.
keyPoints: Four responses for opportunities: exploit, share, enhance, accept.
The goal is to make good events more likely, not to avoid them.
For a surprise risk with no plan, use a workaround and a war room.

steps.title: Teach it back: choosing a response
steps.body: A teammate says, "There's a risk that a key machine breaks down and stops production. What are our options?" In your own words, walk through the four responses for a negative risk (avoid, transfer, mitigate, accept) and give one realistic example of each for the broken-machine situation. Then explain when accepting the risk would be the right choice.

steps.title: Triggers, residual risk, and ownership
steps.body:
A few more ideas make your responses stronger. First, a trigger condition. This is an early warning sign that sets your response in motion. Not every risk has one, but when it does, the trigger lets you act early instead of reacting late.

Second, residual risk. After you put your responses in place, some risk usually remains. Residual risk analysis looks at the probability and impact once your responses are working. For the hurricane, the team cannot change the chance it hits, but their stronger building lowers the impact from 10 to 4. That lower number is the residual risk.

Sometimes a steering committee, the senior group guiding the project, will insist that your responses bring the residual risk below a set point. That point reflects the organization's risk tolerance.

Third, ownership. Your register should show who is responsible for each response. When one person owns a risk, someone is always making sure the actions happen and the risk is watched. Clear owners stop risks from being forgotten.
steps.example: Trigger = the weather bureau issues a storm warning. When that trigger fires, the assigned owner, the site manager, starts the flood-protection checklist right away.
keyPoints: A trigger is an early warning that starts your response.
Residual risk is the risk left after your responses are in place.
Assign an owner so every risk is watched and acted on.

steps.title: Monitoring and control
steps.body:
Risk management does not end when the register is written. The register is a living document. It is only as good as the information in it and the attention you give it.

To control risk, keep checking. Have you done the proactive actions you planned? Are the assumptions you made when scoring still true? And most important, are new risks appearing on the horizon? Risks change as the project moves, so your register must change too.

On a larger team, you might appoint someone to keep the register up to date. But you, as project manager, and the whole team still need to take part. When the team is actively involved, everyone starts to see risk management as useful and takes it seriously.

Culture matters too. Organizational culture shapes how people handle risk and problems. Build a culture of honesty and support, where people feel safe to report a risk or a mistake. That openness lets the whole team face risk at the right level, and it is the real secret to managing risk well.
steps.example: Each week, the project manager reviews the register: she ticks off completed actions, removes risks that have passed, and adds two new risks the team spotted during the sprint.
keyPoints: The risk register is living; review it often.
Check completed actions, old assumptions, and new risks.
A culture of honesty helps the team manage risk well.

title: Managing Quality
intro: Welcome to Managing Quality. In this module you will learn what "quality" really means on a project, and why it matters to you and your customer. We will look at how to plan for quality, the difference between quality assurance and quality control, the cost of quality, and some simple tools you can use to check your work. This module is based on Chapter 9 of "The Professional Project Manager" by Sean Whitaker. Take your time, and enjoy the learning.

steps.title: What quality means
steps.body:
Let's start with a simple question: what is quality? On a project, quality means the degree to which a set of features meets the requirements. In plain words, quality is about giving the customer what they asked for.

This means you need a clear list of requirements first. A requirement is something the customer needs or expects. Once you know the requirements, you can measure your work against them. You can then manage the project to reach those requirements.

Quality is not only about the final product. It is also about many other things. For example, the quality of your talks with stakeholders, the quality of your reports, and the quality of your scope descriptions all matter. Quality runs through the whole project, not just the thing you deliver at the end.

So remember: quality is not a vague good feeling. It is a clear match between what you deliver and what was asked for. Without a list of requirements, you cannot really measure quality at all.
steps.example: A customer asks for a blue chair that holds 100 kilograms. The requirements are the color (blue) and the strength (100 kg). Quality means checking the chair against these two clear requirements.
keyPoints: Quality is how well features meet the requirements.
You need a clear list of requirements first.
Quality covers reports, communication and scope, not just the product.

steps.title: Why quality matters
steps.body:
Quality matters a lot to both you and your customer. A good reputation for quality can win you many customers. But poor quality can lose them just as fast. People talk, and word spreads quickly.

Here is a useful thought: if you are not worried about quality on your project, your competitor surely is. A competitor is another company that wants the same customers. If they deliver better quality, customers may choose them instead of you.

Try to put yourself in the customer's shoes. Would you be willing to pay a little more money to get better quality? Many customers would. They often see quality as worth the extra cost.

This is why quality is not a side task. It is a core part of good project management. When you care about quality, you protect your reputation, keep your customers happy, and stay ahead of your competitors. Quality is an investment in trust, and trust brings future work.
steps.example: Two bakeries sell bread at a similar price. One always makes fresh, tasty loaves. The other sometimes sells stale bread. Customers slowly move to the first bakery because of its better quality.
keyPoints: Good quality wins customers; poor quality loses them.
If you ignore quality, your competitor will not.
Many customers will pay a little more for better quality.

steps.title: Three quality ideas: TQM, Six Sigma, Kaizen
steps.body:
There are three quality ideas you should know. They come up often in project work.

The first is Total Quality Management, or TQM. This approach tries to build awareness of quality into every process in the organization and project. It asks everyone involved to be responsible for quality and to help improve it. At its heart is a promise to keep getting better, step by step.

The second is Six Sigma. This approach improves the quality of process outputs by finding and removing the causes of defects. A defect is a fault or error. Six Sigma also removes variability, which means unwanted differences between items. Six Sigma organizations aim for almost no errors, no more than four defects per million events. The name comes from statistics, where six sigma covers 99.999 percent of a population.

The third is Kaizen. This is a Japanese word used in English to mean a promise to keep improving all the time in your search for quality.

Notice that all three ideas share one theme: continuous improvement. You never stop looking for small ways to make things better.
steps.example: A car factory uses Six Sigma to cut door faults from many per thousand cars down to almost zero, by finding the exact cause of each fault and fixing it.
keyPoints: TQM builds quality into every process and asks everyone to help.
Six Sigma removes defects and aims for almost no errors.
Kaizen means a promise of continuous improvement.

steps.title: Quick check: quality basics
steps.body: Let's check what you remember about the basic ideas of quality.

quiz.question: What do TQM, Six Sigma, and Kaizen all share as a central theme?
options: Continuous improvement
Lower prices for customers
Faster delivery times
Bigger project teams
quiz.explanation: All three quality ideas are built around a commitment to continuous improvement over time.

steps.title: Planning for quality
steps.body:
Every good project plan should include a quality management plan. This is a document that sets out how you will set, measure, and improve quality on your project. It is your roadmap for quality.

The quality management plan should not sit unused in a drawer. It needs continuous improvement too. Keep it up to date and check it regularly. Make sure it still fits the type of projects you are working on. Projects change, and so should your plan.

Why write this plan? Because quality does not happen by accident. If you decide in advance how you will check quality, who will do it, and what standards you expect, you are far more likely to reach those standards. A vague hope for good quality is not enough.

So, when you start a project, take time to think about quality early. Write down your approach. Decide how you will measure success. Then review the plan as the project moves along. A living, updated plan is a strong tool for delivering quality work.
steps.example: Before building a house, the project manager writes a quality plan. It says walls will be checked with a level tool, and an inspector will review the work each week. This plan is updated if new problems appear.
keyPoints: A quality management plan sets out how you set, measure and improve quality.
Keep the plan up to date and review it regularly.
Plan quality early; it does not happen by accident.

steps.title: Prevention over inspection
steps.body:
One key idea to include in your quality plan is prevention over inspection. This means it is better to stop errors before they happen than to catch them afterwards.

The book uses a lovely picture for this. It says: have a fence at the top of the cliff instead of an ambulance at the bottom. A fence stops people from falling. An ambulance only helps after they have already fallen and been hurt. The fence is prevention. The ambulance is inspection and repair.

Why does this matter so much? Because it usually costs more to fix an error than to prevent one. If you catch a fault early, or stop it from ever happening, you save time, money, and stress. If you find it late, the fix is often bigger and more expensive.

So, when you plan quality, focus on prevention. Build good processes that reduce mistakes from the start. Do not rely only on checking the finished product and repairing problems later. Prevention is the smarter, cheaper path.
steps.example: A software team writes clear coding rules and reviews each other's work as they go. This prevents bugs early, instead of finding many expensive bugs only after the product ships to customers.
keyPoints: Prevention means stopping errors before they happen.
A fence at the top of the cliff beats an ambulance at the bottom.
Fixing an error usually costs more than preventing it.

steps.title: Teach it back: prevention
steps.body: Now it is your turn to teach. Imagine a teammate has never heard of 'prevention over inspection'. In your own simple words, explain what it means and why it saves money. Try to use the fence and ambulance picture, or make up your own everyday example. Speak as if you are helping a friend understand for the first time.

steps.title: The cost of quality
steps.body:
Quality can cost money. This is called the cost of quality. There is also a cost of poor quality, which is what you pay when things go wrong.

Pursuing quality makes sense when the money you spend on quality processes is less than the return that good quality brings. In other words, quality should pay for itself. But there is a limit. Sometimes reaching a very high level of quality makes the product too expensive, or the effort is simply not worth it.

The book gives a striking example. Getting that extra one percent of quality might add one hundred percent to the cost. If you make lifesaving equipment, that extra cost is probably worth it, because lives are at risk. If you make video games, it may not be worth it, because the risk is far lower.

So you must judge how much quality is right for your project. Assess your tolerance for both quality and poor quality. Then choose an appropriate level of quality for your processes and deliverables. 'Appropriate' is the key word. More quality is not always better; the right amount is what you aim for.
steps.example: A toy maker could spend double the budget to make toys almost perfect. Instead, they choose a good, safe, appropriate level of quality, because customers will not pay double for a tiny extra improvement.
keyPoints: Quality is worth pursuing when its return is bigger than its cost.
Extra 1% quality can sometimes add 100% to the cost.
Choose an appropriate level of quality for your project.

steps.title: Quick check: cost of quality
steps.body: Let's see if the cost of quality idea is clear.

quiz.question: According to the chapter, when does pursuing higher quality make good sense?
options: Always, because more quality is always better
When the money spent on quality is less than the return good quality brings
Only when the customer complains
Never, because quality is too expensive
quiz.explanation: Quality is worth pursuing when the cost of quality processes is less than the return that good quality brings.

steps.title: Quality assurance: checking the process
steps.body:
Quality assurance is about checking that you are actually doing what you planned and said you would do. It looks at your processes, not the product.

Here are some questions quality assurance asks. If you said you would document all changes in a certain way, are you doing that? If you said every project must have a signed project charter, do they all have one? If you said you would follow a plan for cost and time estimating, are you following it?

Notice the pattern. Quality assurance does not check the quality of the product. It checks the quality of the processes you promised to follow. There is no point having processes if you do not follow them and improve them.

It is natural to think quality is only about the product. But checking your processes is just as important. Keep asking yourself: What processes do I have, and what is missing? Am I following them properly? Do I need to update them to make them more suitable? These continuous questions keep your processes healthy and honest.
steps.example: A project rule says every change must be written down and approved. Quality assurance checks whether the team really does this on every change, not whether the final product looks good.
keyPoints: Quality assurance checks that you follow your planned processes.
It is about the process, not the product.
Keep asking: what is missing, am I following it, should I update it?

steps.title: Audits and ISO standards
steps.body:
The easiest tool for quality assurance is a simple audit of the process. An audit means getting someone, usually an independent person, to review your processes and confirm that people really are following them. Independent means they are not part of the team, so they can be fair.

If the audit finds any area of non-compliance, that means a place where the rules are not being followed. These areas should be fixed. Random audits usually work best. Surprise people and ask them to show that they are following the rules. A surprise check gives a truer picture than a planned one.

Also, do not be afraid to change the rules if they are not suitable. You might make them more strict or less strict. The key word again is appropriate.

Quality assurance is the main focus of the ISO 9000 and 9001 standards. These require an organization to define quality processes and stick to them. But there is a weakness: a company can follow a process perfectly and still make a low quality product. This shows the clear difference between quality of the process and quality of the product.
steps.example: An independent auditor arrives without warning and asks a team member to show how they log project changes. This surprise audit reveals whether the process is truly followed every day.
keyPoints: An audit is an independent review that checks people follow the processes.
Random, surprise audits are usually more effective.
ISO 9000/9001 focus on defined processes, but good process can still make a poor product.

steps.title: Quick check: assurance vs control
steps.body: This is an important difference, so let's test it.

quiz.question: What does quality assurance mainly check?
options: The quality of the final product
The price of the product
Whether you are following your planned processes
The number of people on the team
quiz.explanation: Quality assurance checks the quality of your processes, confirming you follow what you planned to do.

steps.title: Quality control: checking the product
steps.body:
Quality control is the process most people think about first, because it checks the quality of the product. It asks a simple question: does the product meet its specifications?

This job is not hard if you have done your homework. You need a list of outputs and specifications that you expect to see in the product. A specification is an exact standard the product must meet, like a size or weight.

The book gives a fun example with fluffy blue widgets. Suppose you decide every widget must be 12 centimeters across, 7 centimeters deep, weigh 3 kilograms, and bounce 30 centimeters when dropped from 1 meter. Now quality is easy to check. You gather some widgets, measure them, and record the results. If everything is okay, you keep going. If not, you fix the process until the product comes out right and within specification.

The main tool for quality control is inspection. Inspection means looking at and measuring the product to see if it meets the standard. Clear specifications make inspection simple and fair.
steps.example: A factory sets exact rules for its widgets: 12 cm wide, 3 kg heavy, and bouncing 30 cm. Workers measure a sample, compare it to these numbers, and fix the process if any widget is out of specification.
keyPoints: Quality control checks the quality of the product against specifications.
You need a clear list of outputs and specifications first.
The main tool for quality control is inspection.

steps.title: Teach it back: QA vs QC
steps.body: Time to teach again. A new team member keeps mixing up quality assurance and quality control. In your own simple words, explain the difference between the two. Give one short everyday example of each. Remember: one is about the process you follow, and the other is about the product you deliver. Keep it friendly and clear.

steps.title: Tools: cause-and-effect and control charts
steps.body:
Quality control uses several simple tools to check and record results. Let's meet the first two.

A cause-and-effect diagram, also called an Ishikawa diagram, helps you map out all the possible causes of a quality issue. Often a defect has more than one cause. The diagram lays these causes out clearly so you can see them together. You can then dig deeper with 5-Whys analysis. This means you keep asking 'why' about a cause until you reach the real root of the problem. Asking why five times often gets you there.

A control chart records measurements taken over time against control limits. These limits are set at three standard deviations either side of an expected mean, or average. The control limits usually sit inside the specification limits set by the customer. If one measurement falls outside the specification limit, the customer will not want the product, and you must investigate the process at once. If a measurement falls between a control limit and a specification limit, the process may be going out of control, so more inspection is needed. Also, if seven points in a row fall above or below the mean, something may be about to go wrong.
steps.example: A drinks factory fills bottles and plots each fill level on a control chart. When seven bottles in a row are slightly above the average line, the team investigates before any bottle overflows.
keyPoints: An Ishikawa (cause-and-effect) diagram maps all the possible causes of an issue.
5-Whys analysis keeps asking 'why' to find the root cause.
A control chart tracks measurements against control limits over time.

steps.title: Tools: run, Pareto, scatter, sampling
steps.body:
Now let's meet more quality control tools. Each one helps you see your data in a useful way.

A run chart plots a single measurement over time. It lets you see trends and changes quickly and easily. A trend is a pattern moving in one direction.

A Pareto chart lists all the possible quality problems and ranks them by how often they happen. This is powerful. Often just a few problems cause most of the issues. In fact, the chapter shows that three problems can cause 80 percent of the quality issues. By fixing those few big problems first, you get the best return for your time. Direct your energy where it counts most.

A scatter diagram plots two independent variables against each other. It helps you see if they are related, for example weight against height.

Statistical sampling means testing only a small part of the total production, not every item. This is useful when testing everything would take too long, cost too much, or destroy the product. Testing a smart sample gives you a good picture without checking every single item.
steps.example: A phone maker uses a Pareto chart and finds that three faults cause most complaints. They fix those three first, and customer complaints drop sharply.
keyPoints: A run chart shows one measurement over time to reveal trends.
A Pareto chart ranks problems by frequency; a few often cause most issues.
Statistical sampling tests a small sample when testing everything is not feasible.

steps.title: Build a mini quality plan
steps.body:
Let's put your learning into action. Pick a simple project you can imagine, for example baking cakes to sell, or building a small website.

Now write a mini quality plan with four short parts:

1. Requirements and specifications: List two or three clear, measurable standards the product must meet (for example, size, weight, or load time).

2. Prevention: Write one thing you will do to prevent errors before they happen.

3. Quality assurance: Name one process you will follow, and say how you will check that you are following it (for example, a quick audit).

4. Quality control: Choose one tool from this module (inspection, Pareto chart, control chart, or sampling) and say how you will use it to check the product.

Keep it short and practical. This simple exercise mirrors what a real project manager does at the start of a project.

steps.title: Final check: quality tools
steps.body: One last question to finish the module.

quiz.question: Which tool ranks quality problems by how often they happen, so you can fix the few that cause most issues?
options: Scatter diagram
Pareto chart
Run chart
Ishikawa diagram
quiz.explanation: A Pareto chart ranks problems by frequency, showing that a few problems often cause most of the quality issues.

title: Managing Contracts
intro: Almost every project needs help from outside your team. You may buy goods or services from another company, or you may be the one selling them. This module teaches you the basics of contracts and procurement. You will learn how to decide whether to make something yourself or buy it, how to choose a good seller, the main types of contracts and who carries the risk in each, and how to watch a contract closely after you sign it. The goal is simple: help you make clear, fair, and professional deals that protect your project.

steps.title: What is procurement?
steps.body:
Most projects need help from outside your own team. You may need to buy goods (things like materials or equipment) or services (work done by other people). This buying and selling is called procurement.

In a project, you can play two roles. You may be the buyer, who pays another person or company for goods or services. Or you may be the seller, who provides goods or services as part of a bigger project. Both roles are common, so it helps to understand both sides.

Before you go to the market to buy, you need a plan. You must answer a few key questions. How will you find sellers? How will you choose the best one? What kind of contract will you use? A contract is a formal written agreement that says what each side must do.

The best approach is to have a documented and repeatable process. This means you write down your steps and follow the same steps each time. You can make the steps bigger or smaller to fit the size of the project. Procurement is one of the easier parts of project management to turn into a simple checklist that you follow every time.
steps.example: You are building a new office. You do not have a team to install the electrical wiring, so you plan to buy that service from an outside company. You are the buyer, and the electrician is the seller.
keyPoints: Procurement means buying or selling goods and services for a project.
You can be the buyer or the seller.
Plan how you will find and choose sellers before you start.
A documented, repeatable process works best.

steps.title: The make-or-buy decision
steps.body:
One of the first choices in procurement is simple to name but important to get right. Should you make the goods or services yourself, using your own team? Or should you buy them from an outside company? This choice is called the make-or-buy decision.

To make a good decision, you weigh the pros and cons of each option. The chapter names several things you should think about:

Expertise: Do you already have the skills to do this well, or does the outside company know it better?

Risk: Which choice is safer for your project? What could go wrong with each option?

Intellectual property: This means ideas, designs, or knowledge that belong to you. Do you want to keep this inside your company, or is it fine to share it?

Commitment: How focused and reliable will each option be for your project?

When you look at all of these factors together, you can make a strong, well-reasoned decision. Only after you decide to buy from outside do you move on to the next steps: how to advertise your need, how to choose the winner, and what contract to use.
steps.example: A software company needs a new logo. Their team has no design skills (low expertise) and design is not their core work, so they decide to buy the logo from an outside designer instead of making it themselves.
keyPoints: Make-or-buy decides if you use your own team or an outside company.
Consider expertise, risk, intellectual property, and commitment.
Look at all factors together to make a robust decision.
Only after choosing 'buy' do you start finding a seller.

steps.title: Teach it back: make-or-buy
steps.body:
Now it is your turn to explain. Imagine a coworker asks you: "How do I decide whether to do this work with my own team or hire an outside company?"

In your own simple words, explain the make-or-buy decision. Try to name at least three of the four factors from the chapter (expertise, risk, intellectual property, commitment), and say what each one means. Then give one short example of when you would choose to buy instead of make.

Explaining an idea in your own words is one of the best ways to remember it. Do not worry about perfect grammar. Focus on making the idea clear.
steps.example: You might start like this: 'Make-or-buy means choosing between using my own people or hiring outside. I check if my team has the skills, which choice is riskier, whether I want to keep my ideas private, and how committed each option is...'

steps.title: Buyer or seller: advertising your need
steps.body:
In the selection process, think of yourself in one of two ways. If you are advertising for goods or services, you are the buyer. If you are responding to someone else's advertisement, you are the seller. This module mostly looks at the buyer's view.

An important early step is deciding how you will advertise your need. You have two main choices. You can advertise publicly, in media, trade magazines, and websites, so anyone can respond. Or you can go to a smaller group of pre-qualified, approved providers that you already trust.

If you often need outside help, there is real value in having a pre-selection process. This means you check companies in advance and keep a list of approved ones. Before they join your list, you ask them for information about their organization, their ability to deliver the right quality on time, and other records you care about. These records might include their health and safety history, their environmental record, past experience, and their financial track record (how stable their money situation is).

Having this information ready saves time later and lowers your risk.
steps.example: A construction firm keeps a pre-approved list of ten suppliers who have already proven their quality and safety records. When a new project starts, the firm invites only these trusted suppliers instead of advertising to the whole market.
keyPoints: The buyer advertises; the seller responds.
You can advertise publicly or use a pre-qualified group.
A pre-selection process saves time if you buy often.
Ask providers about quality, timing, safety, environment, and finances.

steps.title: Three ways to reach sellers: EOI, RFP, RFT
steps.body:
There are three common documents you can send to reach possible sellers. Each one fits a different level of certainty about what you want.

An Expression of Interest (EOI) is used when you know there is a problem to solve, but you are not sure how to solve it. You invite interested companies to share information about themselves, their experience, and any early ideas. This helps you learn who is out there and what the market can offer. An EOI can even help with your make-or-buy decision.

A Request for Proposal (RFP) is used when you have a clear idea of what you want and can describe it, but you are still open to suggestions from sellers. It is used more often for services than for goods.

A Request for Tender (RFT) is very specific. It is usually based on finished design drawings or a fully developed specification (a detailed description of exactly what is needed). You give this to the market and ask sellers to reply with a price to supply exactly what you described.

So the order goes from least certain (EOI) to most certain (RFT). The clearer your need, the more specific your document can be.
steps.example: A city wants a new bridge. First it might send an EOI to see who is interested and what ideas exist. Later, with finished drawings ready, it sends an RFT asking companies for a firm price to build that exact bridge.
keyPoints: EOI: you know the problem but not the solution.
RFP: you know what you want but are open to ideas (often services).
RFT: very specific, based on finished designs; asks for a price.
Move from EOI to RFT as your certainty grows.

steps.title: Quick check: which document?
steps.body: Test your memory of the three documents used to reach sellers.

quiz.question: You have finished design drawings and want sellers to reply with a price to build exactly what you designed. Which document should you use?
options: An Expression of Interest (EOI)
A Request for Proposal (RFP)
A Request for Tender (RFT)
quiz.explanation: An RFT is very specific, based on finished designs, and asks sellers to respond with a price.

steps.title: Being fair while sellers respond
steps.body:
After you send out your EOI, RFP, or RFT, there is a period of time before sellers must hand in their responses. During this time, one rule matters most: act fairly toward every seller.

Sellers will often have questions about your request. A good way to handle this is to hold meetings where they can ask questions. But you must keep a level playing field, which means no seller gets a secret advantage. To do this, ask all questions in public, and share every answer with all the sellers, not just the one who asked.

Why is this so important? If one seller gets extra information that others do not have, the process becomes unfair. That seller could win for the wrong reason. Unfair processes can also damage your reputation and even cause legal trouble.

Fairness also protects you. When every seller has the same information, you can trust that the winning bid is truly the best one. This makes your final choice easier to defend if anyone questions it later.
steps.example: During a tender, one company emails you asking how many parking spaces the new building needs. Instead of replying only to them, you send the question and your answer to all companies bidding on the project.
keyPoints: Treat all sellers fairly during the response period.
Ask questions in public and share answers with everyone.
Keep a level playing field so no seller gets a secret advantage.
Fairness protects both the sellers and you.

steps.title: Choosing a seller: the weighted attribute method
steps.body:
Once sellers send their responses, you need a fair way to choose the best one. The chapter recommends a weighted attribute process. Here 'attribute' means a quality or feature you care about. You list the attributes that matter, and you give each one a weighting (a number that shows how important it is). Then you score each seller and add up the results.

It may feel tempting to simply pick the lowest price. But the chapter warns that this is risky. Some sellers may bid so low that the job could put them out of business. Others may bid low because they are poor at estimating cost or time. Either way, this can badly hurt your project if the seller fails or runs out of money.

So, besides price, you should consider other attributes: previous experience, the people they will use, their ability to finish on time, culture fit (how well their way of working matches yours), health and safety record, environmental record, and financial stability.

Because of this fuller view, the lowest bidder does not always win. In the chapter's example, Seller A has the lowest price, but Seller C is the best overall choice once all attributes are weighed.
steps.example: Three companies bid. Seller A is cheapest but has weak safety records. After scoring price, experience, safety, and timing together, Seller C wins because it scores best overall, even though it is not the cheapest.
keyPoints: Use a weighted attribute process: list what matters and weight each item.
Lowest price is risky and not always best.
Very low bids can mean poor estimating or a seller going bankrupt.
Consider experience, people, timing, safety, environment, and finances too.

steps.title: Quick check: lowest price
steps.body: Think about the risks of simply choosing the cheapest seller.

quiz.question: According to the chapter, what is one real risk of choosing a seller only because they gave the lowest price?
options: The seller will always deliver faster than others
The seller may have bid so low that the job puts them out of business
Low prices guarantee the highest quality work
The buyer must always pay extra fees later
quiz.explanation: A very low bid can mean the seller will lose money and go out of business, or that they estimate cost and time poorly, hurting your project.

steps.title: Writing clear and careful contracts
steps.body:
Now you have chosen a seller. Next comes the contract, and the chapter gives a strong warning here. Be very careful with contracts that are full of good intentions but worded in an unclear way. Unclear wording is called ambiguous, which means it can be understood in more than one way.

Why does this matter so much? Because misunderstandings and broken promises can lead to serious legal problems, sometimes ending in court. To avoid this, you may want to bring in a legal specialist (a lawyer) to help draft, negotiate, and monitor your contracts. 'Draft' means write, and 'negotiate' means discuss and agree on the terms.

There is another key habit. A contract is a formal written document. So any communication about the contract, including any changes (called variations), must also be formal and in writing. Do not rely on a phone call or a quick chat.

You may never need these written records. But on the day a dispute happens, you will be very glad you wrote everything down. Good records are your best protection when two sides disagree.
steps.example: A buyer and seller agree by phone to change the paint color on a job. Later they argue about the cost. Because nothing was written down, neither side can prove what was agreed. A short written note would have prevented the whole dispute.
keyPoints: Avoid ambiguous (unclear) wording in contracts.
Consider a legal specialist to draft and check contracts.
Put all contract communication and changes in writing.
Written records protect you if a dispute happens.

steps.title: Contract type 1: fixed price
steps.body:
The type of contract you choose decides how risk is shared between the buyer and the seller. Risk here means the danger of extra costs if the work is harder or longer than expected. There are three main types, and this step covers the first one.

A fixed price contract puts the risk on the seller. The buyer agrees to pay one agreed, fixed price for clearly defined goods or services. That price does not change, even if the work turns out to be harder than the seller thought.

So what happens if the seller made a mistake and underestimated the time or effort needed? The seller must 'wear the extra costs.' This means the seller pays the extra amount out of their own pocket, not the buyer. The buyer still pays only the agreed price.

This type works best when the goods or services are clearly defined, so both sides know exactly what is included. Fixed price gives the buyer safety and a predictable cost. The seller carries the danger, so a smart seller will estimate very carefully before agreeing to a fixed price.
steps.example: A seller agrees to build a website for a fixed price of $5,000. The work takes twice as long as planned. The buyer still pays only $5,000, and the seller must cover the extra hours themselves.
keyPoints: Contract type reflects how risk is shared.
Fixed price puts the risk on the seller.
The buyer pays one agreed price that does not change.
If costs rise, the seller pays the extra amount.

steps.title: Contract type 2: cost reimbursable
steps.body:
The second main type is the cost reimbursable contract. Here the financial risk is shared between the buyer and the seller. The exact split depends on the exact terms you agree.

In this type, both sides agree that the buyer will reimburse the seller for the costs they spend on the work. 'Reimburse' means pay back. So if the seller spends money on materials and labor, the buyer pays those costs back. There is usually also a margin added on top for overhead and profit. 'Overhead' means the seller's running costs, and 'profit' is the money the seller earns for doing the work.

This type also allows for incentives. An incentive is a reward that encourages good behavior. For example, the contract might say that if the seller finishes under an agreed maximum amount, they earn a bonus. This gives the seller a reason to keep costs down, which helps the buyer too.

Because the risk is shared, this type sits in the middle. The buyer takes on more risk than in a fixed price deal, but the incentives help keep both sides working toward a good result.
steps.example: A buyer agrees to pay back all of a contractor's costs plus a 10% profit margin. The contract also promises a bonus if the contractor finishes below $50,000, giving them a reason to spend carefully.
keyPoints: Cost reimbursable shares the risk between buyer and seller.
The buyer pays back (reimburses) the seller's costs.
A margin is usually added for overhead and profit.
Incentives can reward the seller for coming in under budget.

steps.title: Contract type 3: time and materials
steps.body:
The third main type is the time and materials contract. This one puts a lot of the risk on the buyer.

In this type, the buyer agrees to pay the seller for whatever time and materials are needed to finish the work. The important point is that there is no ceiling, which means there is no limit on the final cost. The seller can keep charging fees until the job is done.

Because of this, the seller carries almost no risk. They will be paid for all their time and all their materials, no matter how long the work takes. The danger sits mostly with the buyer, who does not know the final price in advance.

So when should you use this type? The chapter says it works best in three situations: small projects, emergency projects, and projects where the scope cannot easily be defined. 'Scope' means the full list of work that must be done. Sometimes you simply cannot describe all the work in advance, for example in an emergency repair. In those cases, time and materials is a practical choice, even though the buyer carries more risk.
steps.example: A pipe bursts and floods a basement at night. There is no time to plan, so the owner hires a plumber on a time and materials basis, agreeing to pay for every hour worked and every part used until the leak is fixed.
keyPoints: Time and materials puts most of the risk on the buyer.
There is no ceiling, so no limit on the final cost.
The seller has little risk and charges until the job is done.
Best for small, emergency, or hard-to-define projects.

steps.title: Quick check: who carries the risk?
steps.body: You have learned the three main contract types. Match the type to the risk.

quiz.question: In which contract type does the SELLER carry the most risk?
options: Fixed price contract
Time and materials contract
A pre-qualified provider list
quiz.explanation: In a fixed price contract the seller must deliver for one agreed price, so if costs rise the seller pays the extra amount.

steps.title: Teach it back: the three contract types
steps.body:
Time to explain again. Imagine a new team member asks you: "What are the three main contract types, and who takes the risk in each one?"

In your own simple words, describe fixed price, cost reimbursable, and time and materials. For each one, say clearly who carries most of the risk: the buyer or the seller. Then give one short situation where you would choose each type.

This teach-back helps lock the idea into your memory. A good trick is to picture the risk moving along a line: from the seller (fixed price), to shared (cost reimbursable), to the buyer (time and materials).
steps.example: You might say: 'Fixed price means one set price, and the seller carries the risk. Cost reimbursable means the buyer pays back costs plus profit, so risk is shared. Time and materials has no cost limit, so the buyer carries the risk, and it fits emergencies...'

steps.title: Cooperative contracts: working in good faith
steps.body:
Sadly, most contracts try to put one side in a position of power over the other. This creates distrust and waste. It can even twist the bidding process. A seller might bid low on purpose to win the work, then make up the money later through variations (changes) once the job has started. These one-sided contracts are common, but they create an adversarial environment. 'Adversarial' means the two sides act like enemies, focused on money instead of a good result.

There is a better way. Some contract forms are built to be cooperative. Two examples the chapter names are NEC (New Engineering Contract) and Alliancing. At their heart, these forms want both sides to act in good faith and work together toward a common goal. Some alliance agreements even remove the option of going to court against each other.

The key to a cooperative contract is people. Everyone must understand the intended outcomes and the expected way of working together. Everyone must agree to it and see the benefits. This takes extra time to set up and extra support from senior managers to keep going. That is why these forms suit large, complex projects best. But even on smaller jobs, you can borrow this cooperative spirit and apply it to normal contracts.
steps.example: Two companies building a large hospital sign an alliance agreement. Instead of blaming each other when problems appear, they share risks and rewards and solve issues together, which keeps the project moving.
keyPoints: Many contracts are one-sided and create distrust.
One-sided deals can lead to low bids and later variations.
Cooperative forms like NEC and Alliancing build good faith and teamwork.
They need extra time and manager support, so they suit large projects.

steps.title: Monitoring the contract and handling variations
steps.body:
Signing a contract is not the end of the work. Every party to a contract is responsible for knowing its contents and actively monitoring its terms. This step is often forgotten. Think about the last time you signed up for a credit card, insurance, or software. Did you read the whole contract? Most people do not. But contracts can have serious consequences, so it is wise to read what you sign and know the clauses inside it.

Treat contracts as live documents. This means they stay active and need attention even after signing. After you agree the terms and sign, you must keep checking that the work is really going as agreed.

Another reason to monitor closely is that, sooner or later, someone may ask for a variation. A variation is a change to the terms or to the defined scope of work. When this happens, make sure every request is fully documented. Because the contract is a formal written document, all proposed and approved changes must also be formal and in writing. Never rely on a spoken agreement for a change. Careful monitoring and written records keep everyone honest and protect your project.
steps.example: Halfway through a build, the client asks for an extra room. The project manager does not just say yes on site. Instead, they write a formal variation describing the new work and the new cost, and both sides sign it before work begins.
keyPoints: Everyone must know and actively monitor the contract.
Treat contracts as live documents, even after signing.
Keep checking that work matches what was agreed.
Document every variation formally and in writing.

steps.title: Build it: a dispute resolution plan
steps.body:
Even with good contracts, disagreements can happen. The chapter lists a common order of dispute resolution techniques, from gentlest to strongest: Negotiation (the parties try to solve it themselves), Mediation (an independent third party helps them agree), Arbitration (an independent arbitrator makes a decision that may or may not be binding), and Litigation (the parties go to a court of law).

Your task: imagine you are the project manager on a project where a buyer and seller disagree about whether some work was included in the price.

Write a short, simple plan (4 or 5 sentences) that shows how you would move through these four steps in order. For each step, write one line saying what you would try and who would be involved. Start with the cheapest, friendliest option and only move to the next step if the one before it fails. This mirrors real practice: you try to keep the relationship and save money before ever going to court.
steps.example: Step 1, Negotiation: 'First, I would sit down with both sides to try to agree directly.' Step 2, Mediation: 'If that fails, I would bring in a neutral mediator to help us find a solution...' (continue for Arbitration and Litigation).
keyPoints: Dispute steps go from gentle to strong.
Order: Negotiation, Mediation, Arbitration, Litigation.
Try the cheapest, friendliest option first.
Move to court (litigation) only as a last resort.

title: Project Communications
intro: Communication is the biggest part of a project manager's job. This module shows you why. You will learn how to plan communication, choose good methods, write clear status reports, and run meetings that do not waste time. Everything here comes from Chapter 11 of "The Professional Project Manager" by Sean Whitaker. The lessons are simple and practical, so you can use them on your very next project.

steps.title: Communication is most of the job
steps.body:
Many people think a project manager mostly works with numbers, charts, and schedules. That is only part of the truth. The biggest part of the job is talking and listening. In fact, the PMBOK Guide says a project manager should spend about 90% of their time communicating. Around half of that time is spent with the project team.

Why does this matter so much? Because a project can succeed or fail based on communication alone. The book says it clearly: you must share the right things, with the right people, at the right time, in the right way. Get this right, and you greatly raise your chance of success. Get it wrong, and you face an uphill battle.

When communication is missing, bad things grow. Rumor, guessing, and gossip spread quickly. People also feel that you do not care about them. And if you do not control the message, someone else will. Then you spend your days "fighting fires" and fixing wrong stories. In fact, poor communication is named as the biggest cause of project failure.
keyPoints: A PM should spend about 90% of their time communicating.
About half of that time is with the project team.
Poor communication is the biggest cause of project failure.
If you don't control the message, someone else will.
steps.example: A PM sends no updates for two weeks. Soon the team hears a rumor that the project will be cancelled. People get worried and slow down. A short weekly update would have stopped the rumor before it started.

steps.title: Communication is more than emails
steps.body:
It is easy to think communication just means the emails you send or the talks you have. But in project management it means much more. It is the act of sharing any information about the project with any stakeholder. A stakeholder is any person or group who cares about, or is affected by, the project.

This sharing can happen in many forms. It can be a verbal talk, an email, a phone call, a memo, a meeting, or even advertising. The book says the most important of all may be your weekly and monthly progress reports. These reach the most people on a regular basis.

Here is a key idea that surprises many people. Good communication is not only about what you choose to send. It is also about what you choose to hold back. Sometimes you share less on purpose, because too much detail can confuse people or worry them. So a skilled PM thinks carefully about both sides: what to say, and what to keep quiet for now.
keyPoints: Communication = sharing any project information with any stakeholder.
It includes talks, emails, calls, memos, meetings, and reports.
Regular progress reports may be the most important of all.
Good communication also means choosing what to withhold.
steps.example: A PM knows a small risk might become a problem next month. She does not send a scary alert to all stakeholders yet. She quietly watches it and tells only the two people who can act. That is a smart choice about what to withhold.

steps.title: Quiz: How big is communication?
steps.body: Let's check the first ideas.

quiz.question: According to the PMBOK Guide, about how much of a project manager's time should be spent communicating?
options: About 25%
About 50%
About 90%
About 10%
quiz.explanation: The book says a PM should spend about 90% of their time communicating, with about half of that time spent with the project team.

steps.title: A simple model of communication
steps.body:
Have you played the game where you whisper a message and pass it from person to person? By the tenth person, the message has changed. By the twentieth, it is very different. This shows how hard it is to share even one simple idea.

The book gives a simple model to explain why. First, you (the sender) pick a message. You choose what to include and what to leave out, based on your own views. Next, you choose the words and the way to send it. This choice of words and method is called "encoding." The method itself sends a message too. For example, think how it feels to be broken up with by text message.

Then the message travels to the receiver. On the way, it meets "noise." Noise is anything that changes the message. It can be background sound, an accent, a tone of voice, or a graphic report sent to someone who prefers words. Finally, the receiver "decodes" the message using their own views and filters. They may hear something quite different from what you meant.
keyPoints: Encoding = choosing the words and the method to send.
Noise = anything that changes the message on the way.
Decoding = how the receiver reads the message, in their own way.
The chosen method itself carries meaning.
steps.example: You send a detailed spreadsheet to a manager who hates numbers. That mismatch is 'noise.' She skims it, misses the key point, and decodes a different message than you meant.

steps.title: Teach back: sender to receiver
steps.body: Imagine a new team member asks you: "Why do my messages get misunderstood so often?" In your own simple words, explain the three parts of the communication model: encoding, noise, and decoding. Give one everyday example of 'noise' from your own life, and one small thing the sender can do and one small thing the receiver can do to reduce mistakes.
keyPoints: Name and explain encoding, noise, and decoding.
Give a real example of noise.
Show both sides share the work of good communication.
steps.example: You might say: 'Encoding is how I wrap my idea in words. Noise is a bad phone line that cuts my words. Decoding is how you understand them. So I speak clearly, and you repeat it back to check.'

steps.title: Both sides share the work
steps.body:
Some people say good communication is only the sender's job. The book disagrees. Communication has two or more people, so both sides share the work. The sender must do their best to deliver the message well. The receiver must do their best to understand it correctly. Each person should know their own habits and views, and stay active in the process.

How can you make communication better? The book names three skills. The first is active listening. This means you make a clear choice to take part fully. You watch for problems in the message coming in, instead of just sitting quietly.

The second is effective listening. It is like active listening, but you also watch non-verbal signs, such as body language and physical cues. The third is feedback. Feedback means you ask the receiver for signs that show if they understood. Then you use that chance to explain more if needed. A simple 'Can you tell me what you heard?' is powerful feedback.
keyPoints: Both sender and receiver share the duty for good communication.
Active listening: choose to take part and watch for problems.
Effective listening: also read body language and physical cues.
Feedback: ask if the message was understood, then clarify.
steps.example: After giving instructions, a PM asks, 'Just so we're aligned, what will you do first?' The team member repeats the steps. This feedback catches a wrong assumption before any work starts.

steps.title: Face-to-face and non-verbal signals
steps.body:
Perhaps the most important skill is communicating person to person. This needs you to be real and to care about people. The book lists key traits to build: empathy, trust, honesty, self-confidence, and the ability to express yourself clearly in speech and writing. You also need great listening skills. Remember, you have two ears but only one mouth, so listen more than you speak.

Here is a surprising fact. When two people talk in person, most of the communication is not the words. It is non-verbal. We humans are very good at reading small signs, often without thinking. We notice attention, body language, and posture.

We also read "paralingual" clues. These are vocal but not verbal, such as tone of voice and volume. In other words, how the words are said, not just what words are said. Because of this, we can pick up a different message from the spoken one. The book points to the silent film "The Artist." With no dialog, you still clearly feel the emotions of the story.
keyPoints: Interpersonal skill needs empathy, trust, honesty, and clear expression.
Most in-person communication is non-verbal.
Paralingual clues = tone and volume: how words are said.
You have two ears but one mouth: listen more.
steps.example: A team member says 'I'm fine' in a flat, quiet voice while looking away. The words say okay, but the tone and body language say the opposite. A good PM notices and gently checks in.

steps.title: Quiz: Reading the signals
steps.body: Let's check the listening ideas.

quiz.question: What are 'paralingual' clues in communication?
options: The exact words a person chooses
Vocal signals like tone of voice and volume (how words are said)
Written reports and emails
The charts and graphs in a report
quiz.explanation: Paralingual clues are vocal but not verbal. They include tone and volume, meaning how the words are said rather than what words are said.

steps.title: Reporting: your main tool
steps.body:
Reporting is the type of communication that most stakeholders receive on a regular basis. In your reports, you show how the project is going. You cover money, scope, time, quality, risk, and any other topics people need to know about.

Not everyone needs the same report. You will have different levels of reporting for different levels of stakeholders. Team members doing the work may need detailed information often. Higher-level stakeholders may only want a short summary once a month. The client may need different information, in a different format, at a different time, than a sub-contractor.

To make this easier, keep some standard templates. A template is a ready-made form you fill in each time. Your templates can set how much text, how many graphs, how many numbers, and even what colors you use. Remember, small choices matter. The frequency, the format, the amount you include, and the amount you leave out all shape how people see your project. You may even need a few versions of one report, each with a slightly different focus for a different group.
keyPoints: Reports are the most regular communication for most stakeholders.
Match the level of detail to the level of stakeholder.
Use standard templates to keep reporting easy and consistent.
Frequency, format, and what you leave out all shape perception.
steps.example: The build team gets a detailed weekly report with task lists. The company directors get a one-page monthly summary with a simple traffic-light chart. Same project, two reports, two audiences.

steps.title: Ways to report progress
steps.body:
The book lists many ways to report progress. Each one fits a different reader. Dashboards use pictures and color to show summary information fast. Project software often builds them, and sometimes lets stakeholders check certain data any time.

RAG analysis uses Red, Amber, Green to show status quickly. Red means trouble, and you should explain your plan to fix it. Amber means a possible issue that you are managing. Green means all is well and on plan. Graphs show summary data visually, as bars, lines, or pie shapes, often with some text or numbers.

Gantt charts start as a planning tool but are great for showing progress too. Text is the standard way to report, good for both summaries and detailed reports, but it is open to many readings, so you must write well. Numbers, like percentages or indices, help some people, but they can be shaped to hide or show certain facts, so ask for deeper explanations. Photographs suit visual people. As they say, a picture is worth a thousand words.
keyPoints: Dashboards and graphs show summary data fast and visually.
RAG: Red = trouble, Amber = watched issue, Green = on plan.
Gantt charts show progress, not just plans.
Text and numbers can be misread, so explain them carefully.
steps.example: A monthly report shows three bars: Time = Green, Cost = Amber, Quality = Green. Next to the Amber bar, one line explains: 'Costs are 4% over; a review meeting is set for Friday.'

steps.title: Quiz: Traffic lights
steps.body: Let's check the reporting methods.

quiz.question: In RAG analysis, what does 'Amber' mean?
options: Everything is fine and on plan
There is serious trouble that needs a fix plan
There is a possible issue that is being managed
The task is finished and closed
quiz.explanation: Amber means there is a potential issue that is being managed. Red means trouble needing a plan, and Green means no issues and on plan.

steps.title: Good meetings and bad meetings
steps.body:
Meetings can be a valuable tool. They let the team come together, make decisions, and move the project forward. Beyond decisions, they help build relationships and set the team culture. But meetings can also be the biggest waste of time you will ever meet. Your job is to make them useful, not wasteful.

What makes a good meeting? It has a clear purpose. People come prepared and stay focused. It sticks to an agenda, which is the planned list of topics. It creates real value. And it is a place to solve issues, make decisions, and build the team.

What makes a bad meeting? People turn up unprepared. People who do not need to be there still attend. Talks happen that should have been done before or after the meeting. There is no accountability and no real reason to meet. The room is full of bored people who wish they were somewhere else. Here is a sharp question from the book: think about each person's hourly pay, add it up, and ask, are you getting value for money?
keyPoints: Good meetings have a purpose, an agenda, and create real value.
They help make decisions and build the team.
Bad meetings have unprepared people and no accountability.
A meeting costs money: add up everyone's time.
steps.example: Six people sit for 90 minutes with no agenda, discussing things two of them could have settled by email. That is an expensive, low-value meeting.

steps.title: Tips for running a meeting
steps.body:
Before you book a meeting, ask a few honest questions. Can you justify getting these people together? Is a meeting the best way to get the result you want? Do you even know the result you want? A meeting does not have to be your first choice. There are many other ways to share ideas and discuss issues.

If a meeting really has value, the book gives clear tips. Set a date and time. Send an agenda and stick to it. Set ground rules for attendance, preparation, and timing. Leave off-topic items for the end or for outside the meeting.

Start on time. If you do not, people learn it is fine to arrive late, and they always will. If someone is late, note it in the minutes, and they will rarely do it again. Finish on time, or people will stop coming. Only invite people who are needed, and let them leave when their part is done. Do not run longer than an hour, because focus fades. Do not let people talk over each other. Have someone take short, clear minutes and share them after. Then follow up on the action points.
keyPoints: First ask: is a meeting even the best tool for the result you want?
Send an agenda and stick to it.
Start and finish on time; note latecomers in the minutes.
Take clear minutes and follow up on action points.
steps.example: A PM writes: 'Purpose: decide the launch date. 30 minutes. Attendees: only the three team leads.' She starts at 10:00 sharp, ends at 10:30, and emails five short action points that afternoon.

steps.title: Build: plan a 30-minute meeting
steps.body: Practice makes this real. Plan one short, useful project meeting on paper. Write a simple agenda that includes: (1) a clear purpose in one sentence, (2) the exact date, start time, and finish time (keep it 30 minutes or less), (3) a short list of only the people who truly need to be there, (4) two or three agenda items with a time limit for each, and (5) one ground rule (for example, 'start on time' or 'come prepared'). Finish by naming who will take the minutes and how you will follow up on action points afterward.
keyPoints: State one clear purpose and a firm start and finish time.
Invite only the people who are truly needed.
Give each agenda item a time limit.
Decide who takes minutes and how you follow up.
steps.example: Purpose: agree the test plan for Module A. 10:00-10:25. Attendees: Ana, Ben, You. Items: review last actions (5 min), agree test steps (15 min), assign owners (5 min). Ground rule: come prepared. Minutes: Ben. Follow-up: email actions by noon.

steps.title: Working with virtual teams
steps.body:
New technology lets people in different places work on one team. They might be on different floors, in different cities, or in different countries with different time zones and customs. This is called a virtual team.

Virtual teams use resources well, but they bring real problems. The moment you take away face-to-face contact, you raise the barriers to good communication. The book is firm here: face-to-face is always the best way to communicate. It gives the fullest transfer of information, in both words and non-verbal signs. Better communication builds trust, trust builds better teamwork, and better teamwork raises the chance of success.

So how do you help a virtual team? Set clear roles so no one is unsure who does what. Write down the expected behaviors. Include face-to-face time whenever you can. Keep everyone informed about the whole project, using several methods. Do not let people vanish; keep them talking to each other. Set clear rules for handling conflict, and act early. And if the team spans time zones, rotate meeting times, so everyone takes a turn with an early or late slot. This is fair to all.
keyPoints: Virtual teams save resources but raise communication barriers.
Face-to-face is always the best communication method.
Set clear roles, expected behaviors, and conflict rules.
Rotate meeting times so no time zone is always inconvenienced.
steps.example: A team in London and Manila always met at 9am London time, which was 5pm in Manila. The PM rotated the times so each side sometimes took the early or late slot. The Manila team felt more respected and joined in more.

steps.title: Your communications plan and register
steps.body:
Now bring all these ideas together into a communications plan. This plan describes and guides your more formal communication. It usually does not cover casual chats. It focuses on planned, formal messages. It can be a simple template you fill in for each project. To build it, use past project records, ask team members, brainstorm, and interview stakeholders.

Start the plan with some text. Describe the goals of project communication, the strategies to reach them, the expected outcomes, and the key messages. You can have several of each.

Then build your communications register. There are two ways to organize it. A method-based register lists the type of communication (like email, report, or meeting), the message, the audience, the frequency, how you gather feedback, and who is responsible. A stakeholder-based register lists each stakeholder, their interest, their power (say 1 to 5), and their influence or interest (say 1 to 5). You multiply power by influence to rank who matters most. You also note what information they want, when, in what format, and who delivers it.
keyPoints: The plan guides formal communication, not casual chats.
Start with goals, strategies, outcomes, and key messages.
Method-based register: type, message, audience, frequency, feedback, owner.
Stakeholder-based register: interest, power, influence, and ranking.
steps.example: A method-based row reads: Type = monthly report; Message = progress summary; Audience = directors; Frequency = monthly; Feedback = reply email; Responsible = the PM. One clear row, one clear plan.

steps.title: Teach back: plan your communication
steps.body: A friend is starting their first project and asks: "How do I plan my communication so I don't forget anyone?" In your own simple words, explain what a communications plan is and why it helps. Then describe the two ways to build a communications register (method-based and stakeholder-based), and say which fields each one contains. Finish with one sentence on why going 'straight to the source' gives you more accurate information than second-hand rumor.
keyPoints: Explain the purpose of a communications plan.
Compare method-based and stakeholder-based registers.
Note the value of first-hand, accurate information.
steps.example: You might say: 'A communications plan is a simple table of who gets what message, how, and how often. A method-based one is organized by communication type; a stakeholder-based one is organized by person. And I always check facts with the source, so I don't repeat wrong rumors.'

title: Managing Stakeholders
intro: Every project has people around it — a team, a client, suppliers, and even members of the public. These people are your stakeholders. They can help your project succeed or make it fail. In this module you will learn who stakeholders are, how to find them, how to understand their power and interest, and how to keep them engaged and happy. You will also learn the difference between honest influencing and dishonest manipulation. Let's begin.

steps.title: Who is a stakeholder?
steps.body:
Let's start with the most important word in this module: stakeholder.

A stakeholder is anyone who can affect your project, or anyone your project can affect. It even includes people who only *think* they will be affected. A stakeholder can be one person, a group, or a whole organization.

Who are they on a real project? Your project team is a stakeholder group. So is the wider team of people helping with the work. The client is a stakeholder. So are your suppliers and contractors. Members of the public can be stakeholders. Local government can be a stakeholder too. Really, any interested person or group counts.

Why does this matter so much? Because stakeholders can help your project move forward — or they can get in the way. Your job is to know what each one needs. When you know what information they need, you can keep them working toward your goals. Or, at the very least, you can stop them from blocking you.

This is a big part of planning your project communication. You cannot manage people you have not noticed. So the first step is always to see clearly who your stakeholders are.
steps.example: On a project to build a new park, the stakeholders include the construction team, the city council that pays for it, the families living next door, and the local shops that might lose parking spaces.
keyPoints: A stakeholder is anyone who can affect or be affected by your project.
It can be a person, a group, or an organization.
It even includes people who only believe they are affected.
Examples: team, client, suppliers, the public, and government.

steps.title: Why manage expectations at all?
steps.body:
You might think: if I just do a good job, the results will speak for themselves. Sadly, that is not always true.

You can deliver a project that is perfect on cost, time, and quality — and stakeholders can still call it a failure. Why? Because they care about different things than you do. Or because they never felt part of the project. They had no feeling of ownership.

This is why we manage stakeholder expectations. An expectation is what a person hopes or believes will happen. Managing expectations is a two-way process. First, you find out what is important to each stakeholder. Then you help them clearly understand and support the goals of the project.

When you understand their expectations, you can act early — before problems appear. This is called being proactive. Being proactive means you act first, instead of only reacting to problems after they happen. This way, stakeholders support your project instead of blocking it.
