You can even measure how happy your stakeholders are. How? Through your own observation, the number of complaints and compliments, structured meetings, and written feedback or surveys. Stakeholder satisfaction is one good way to judge if a project truly succeeded.
steps.example: A software team finishes a new app on time and on budget. But they never asked the sales team what they needed. The sales team feels ignored and calls the app 'useless' — even though it works perfectly.
keyPoints: Good technical results are not enough on their own.
Stakeholders may care about different things, like feeling involved.
Managing expectations is a two-way process: understand, then align.
Measure satisfaction with feedback, complaints, compliments, and surveys.

steps.title: Quick check: what is a stakeholder?
steps.body: Let's make sure the basic idea is clear before we go on.

quiz.question: Which of these best describes a stakeholder?
options: Only the person who pays for the project
Anyone who can affect or be affected by the project, including those who just think they are affected
Only the members of the project team
Only people who have signed a contract
quiz.explanation: A stakeholder is anyone who can affect or be affected by the project — even someone who only believes they will be affected.

steps.title: The word 'management' is an active word
steps.body:
The key word in 'stakeholder expectation management' is *management*. In project work, management is an active word. It means actively controlling something, not just watching it happen.

So managing expectations is active work too. You are responsible for understanding, influencing, changing, meeting, and even creating the expectations of your stakeholders. And you do all this in a proactive way — acting early, on purpose.

How do you do this in practice? You communicate with stakeholders. You negotiate with them. You influence them. Every good project manager should be a skilled communicator and a skilled influencer.

But be careful. There is a fine line between influencing and manipulation. Do not cross it. Influencing uses good people skills: empathy, honest persuasion, authenticity, and the ability to motivate. Manipulation, on the other hand, involves some dishonesty.

One more thing: this is not a one-time job. Managing expectations is on-going. It needs continuous dialogue — regular, two-way talking — so stakeholders stay engaged and their expectations stay known and met. You never 'finish' this task. You keep doing it through the whole project.
steps.example: Instead of waiting for a worried neighbour to complain, the project manager visits early, listens to their concerns, and explains the plan. That is active, proactive management — not waiting and reacting.
keyPoints: 'Management' means actively controlling, not passively watching.
You understand, influence, change, meet, and even create expectations.
Influencing is honest; manipulation is dishonest — never cross that line.
It is on-going work that needs continuous, two-way dialogue.

steps.title: Influencing is not a bad word
steps.body:
Some people feel uncomfortable with the word 'influencing'. They think it means becoming a master manipulator who changes people's minds against their will. That is wrong.

Influencing is a necessary skill for a project manager. It helps increase the chance of project success. So what is the real difference between influencing and manipulating? It is simply the difference between honesty and dishonesty.

Influencing means being open about what you want. You change people's views and actions through honest, authentic, and respectful ways. Manipulating means using dishonesty and deception. It often hides an ulterior motive — a secret reason that may not help the other person.

Manipulation may work in the short term. But in the long term, it usually comes back to harm you. Maybe not in project results, but certainly in your reputation.

The chapter gives three main ways to influence honestly. First, build relationships, so you and the stakeholder understand each other's point of view. Second, model the behaviour you want to see — your words and actions must match. Third, motivate people with a clear, inspiring message, and reward them with praise when they respond.

Remember: a style that works on one stakeholder may not work on another.
steps.example: A manager wants the team to write clearer reports. Instead of ordering it, she writes clear reports herself (modelling), explains why it helps everyone (motivating), and praises team members who improve. That is honest influencing.
keyPoints: Influencing is honest and open; manipulation is dishonest and hidden.
Manipulation may work briefly but damages your reputation later.
Three ways to influence: build relationships, model behaviour, motivate.
One influencing style will not work on every stakeholder.

steps.title: Teach it back: influencing vs manipulation
steps.body: Imagine a new project manager on your team says: 'Isn't influencing just a polite word for manipulating people?' In your own simple words, explain the difference to them. What makes influencing honest and acceptable? What makes manipulation wrong? Try to include at least one of the three honest ways to influence from the last step.

steps.title: The stakeholder register
steps.body:
Now let's get practical. Where do you keep all the information about your stakeholders? In a document called the stakeholder register.

A stakeholder register is a simple list. For every project, you should build one. It records all your stakeholders and useful details about each one.

What goes inside? The register can list names and contact details. It records each stakeholder's interest in the project and their expectations from it. It can note their requirements and their ability to influence others. It can also hold short notes on how you plan to manage each person.

There is some overlap between the stakeholder register and the communications plan. On smaller projects, you can even combine them into one document.

If your project is large and has many stakeholders, you might build a fuller stakeholder management plan. This plan sets out the key areas for managing stakeholders. It can explain how you will collect information, who is responsible for watching the plan, the level of engagement you want, how stakeholders depend on each other, and the exact communication needs of each one.

One warning: some of this information is sensitive. Your notes about how to handle a difficult person should be kept private and handled with care.
steps.example: A register row might read: 'Name: Maria Lopez. Role: City inspector. Interest: high. Expectation: full safety reports each month. Note: prefers email, dislikes surprises.'
keyPoints: The stakeholder register is a list of all stakeholders and their details.
It records interest, expectations, influence, and management notes.
On small projects, combine it with the communications plan.
Some notes are sensitive — keep them private and handle with care.

steps.title: Identifying your stakeholders
steps.body:
Before you can fill the register, you must find your stakeholders. This step is called identifying stakeholders.

A great way to start is a brainstorming session with your project team. Brainstorming means everyone shares ideas freely to make a long list. Together, ask: who could affect this project, and who could be affected by it? During this session, you can also give each stakeholder a rough ranking by their power and their interest in the project.

Another place to look is the lessons learned from past projects. Lessons learned are notes about what went well and what went badly before. Old projects often had similar stakeholders, so they give good clues.

You can also let stakeholders find you. Sometimes an announcement about the project brings interested people forward to identify themselves.

Remember one key idea: identifying stakeholders is on-going. It never fully stops. New stakeholders can become interested at any time. And existing ones can lose interest and drift away. So you must keep watching and keep updating your register through the whole project.

For each stakeholder, note their contact details, their interest, and any way they can influence other stakeholders. This tells you how much effort each one needs.
steps.example: During a brainstorm, the team lists the obvious stakeholders. Then someone checks last year's project notes and remembers the local newspaper — an easy stakeholder to forget, but an important one.
keyPoints: Find stakeholders by brainstorming with your team.
Check lessons learned from past, similar projects.
Announcements can make stakeholders identify themselves.
Identifying is on-going — new ones appear and old ones fade.

steps.title: Quick check: finding stakeholders
steps.body: Let's test the ideas about identifying stakeholders.

quiz.question: Which statement about identifying stakeholders is correct?
options: You do it once at the start and never again
It is on-going, because new stakeholders can appear and old ones can lose interest
Only the client is allowed to name the stakeholders
You should skip brainstorming to save time
quiz.explanation: Identifying stakeholders never fully stops — new people become interested and existing ones can drift away, so you keep updating.

steps.title: Levels of stakeholder engagement
steps.body:
If a stakeholder is not engaged with your project, their expectations are much harder to manage. Engagement means how connected and involved a stakeholder feels.

The chapter gives a standard way to describe engagement using five levels:

1. Unaware — the stakeholder does not know about the project or its goals.
2. Resistant — they know about it, but they are against the project's goals.
3. Neutral — they are neither for nor against; they just watch.
4. Supportive — they like the project's goals and back them.
5. Leading — they are fully committed and actively work to make the project succeed.

These five levels give you a simple, shared language. Instead of saying 'that person is difficult', you can say 'that person is currently resistant, and I want them to become neutral'.

Notice that engagement can move up — and down. A supportive stakeholder can slip back to neutral if you ignore them. So you keep checking where each person is. In the next step, we will see how to use these levels to focus your effort where it matters most.
steps.example: A department head has never heard of your new system — she is 'Unaware'. After a friendly demo, she becomes 'Supportive' and starts recommending it to her staff.
keyPoints: Engagement is how involved and connected a stakeholder feels.
The five levels: unaware, resistant, neutral, supportive, leading.
The levels give you a clear, shared language for your team.
Engagement can rise or fall, so you keep checking it.

steps.title: The engagement matrix: closing the gap
steps.body:
Now let's turn the five levels into a simple tool. It is called the stakeholder engagement matrix.

Here is the idea. For each stakeholder, you mark two things: where they are *now*, and where you *want* them to be. The space between these two points is the gap.

Why is the gap so useful? Because it shows you where to spend your energy. You focus your effort on the stakeholders with the biggest gap between their current level and your desired level. A stakeholder who is already 'Leading' needs little work. But a powerful stakeholder who is 'Resistant', when you need them 'Supportive', needs your attention now.

How do you move someone up? Raising engagement is a tailored exercise. Tailored means made to fit one specific person, not a copy-paste for everyone. It starts with communication, and each stakeholder has different communication needs.

The chapter lists the key elements for good communication and engagement: consistency, appropriateness, openness, respect, empathy, and listening. Notice that listening is on the list. Engagement is not only about sending messages — it is about hearing people too.

A good communicator knows what stakeholders expect. A great communicator goes further and shapes expectations to help the project.
steps.example: You mark the finance director as 'Neutral' now, but you need her 'Supportive' to approve the budget. That two-level gap tells you she is a top priority this month.
keyPoints: Mark each stakeholder's current and desired engagement level.
Focus effort on the biggest gaps between now and desired.
Raising engagement is tailored to each individual person.
Key elements: consistency, openness, respect, empathy, and listening.

steps.title: Teach it back: the engagement gap
steps.body: A colleague asks you: 'I have twenty stakeholders and not much time. How do I decide who to focus on using the engagement matrix?' Explain, in your own words, how the idea of the 'gap' between current and desired engagement helps you decide where to spend your effort. Use a short example if it helps.

steps.title: The power/interest grid
steps.body:
Here is one of the most useful tools in the whole chapter: the power and interest grid, also called the stakeholder management ranking.

The idea is simple. You judge each stakeholder on two things. Power is how much they can affect the project. Interest is how much they care about it. Then you place each person on a grid with four boxes.

Each box has its own strategy:

- Low power, low interest: just *monitor* them. Give them little time, but watch for changes.
- High power, low interest: *keep them satisfied*. They can affect you, but they do not care much yet — do not annoy them.
- Low power, high interest: *keep them informed*. They care a lot, so share news, even though they cannot change much.
- High power, high interest: *manage them closely*. These are your most important stakeholders. Give them the most attention.

By plotting everyone this way, you get a prioritized list. You spend most of your time on the powerful, interested people. You spend little time on those who only need monitoring — mostly to catch any change in their power or interest.

The chapter notes you can also chart power and influence, or influence and impact, or urgency. Power and interest is just the most common pair.
steps.example: The mayor has high power but low interest in your small road repair — keep her satisfied. A daily commuter has low power but high interest — keep him informed with updates.
keyPoints: Rate each stakeholder on power (effect) and interest (care).
Low/low: monitor. High power/low interest: keep satisfied.
Low power/high interest: keep informed. High/high: manage closely.
Plotting everyone gives you a prioritized list for your time.

steps.title: Quick check: power and interest
steps.body: Let's practise the four strategies from the power/interest grid.

quiz.question: A stakeholder has HIGH power but LOW interest in your project. What is the recommended strategy?
options: Keep them closely managed at all times
Keep them satisfied
Just monitor them and give little effort
Keep them fully informed with every small detail
quiz.explanation: High power but low interest means 'keep satisfied' — they can affect the project, so do not annoy them, but they do not need heavy involvement yet.

steps.title: Quick check: who to manage closely?
steps.body: One more check on the grid, because it is so important.

quiz.question: Which stakeholders should you manage most closely?
options: Those with low power and low interest
Those with low power and high interest
Those with high power and high interest
Those with high power and low interest
quiz.explanation: Stakeholders with both high power and high interest matter most, so they get your closest attention and effort.

steps.title: The salience model
steps.body:
There is another way to sort stakeholders, called the salience model. Salience means how much a stakeholder stands out and demands your attention.

This model uses three qualities instead of two:

- Power — can they get what they want, even against others?
- Legitimacy — is their involvement proper and appropriate? Do they have a real, rightful claim?
- Urgency — do they need attention right now? Is their issue time-sensitive?

By mixing these three qualities in different ways, the model sorts stakeholders into seven types with unusual names: dormant, dominant, discretionary, definitive, dangerous, dependent, and demanding.

You do not need to memorize all seven names for daily work. The useful idea is this: a stakeholder with all three qualities — power, legitimacy, and urgency — is a 'definitive' stakeholder. They stand out the most and need your strongest attention. A stakeholder with only one quality stands out much less.

Think of the salience model as a more detailed version of the power/interest grid. The grid uses two factors; the salience model uses three. Both help you answer the same question: who deserves the most of my limited time and effort?
steps.example: A government safety regulator has power (they can stop work), legitimacy (a lawful right to inspect), and urgency (a deadline next week). All three make them a 'definitive' stakeholder you cannot ignore.
keyPoints: Salience means how much a stakeholder stands out and needs attention.
It uses three qualities: power, legitimacy, and urgency.
It sorts stakeholders into seven types, like dormant or definitive.
A 'definitive' stakeholder has all three and needs the most attention.

steps.title: Build your own stakeholder map
steps.body:
Time to put it all together with a small task. Pick a real or imagined project — for example, redecorating an office, launching a small website, or organizing a community event.

1. List at least five stakeholders for your project (remember: team, client, suppliers, public, government — think widely).

2. For each one, write two short columns: their expectation (what they hope will happen) and one note on how you will communicate with them.

3. Now rate each stakeholder on power (high or low) and interest (high or low). Place each into one of the four boxes: monitor, keep satisfied, keep informed, or manage closely.

4. Finally, pick the ONE stakeholder you think is most important, and write two sentences on how you will keep them engaged and support their expectations.

This simple map is the heart of stakeholder management. Keep it — a real project manager would update it every week.

steps.title: Keep watching: management never stops
steps.body:
You have learned a lot: who stakeholders are, how to find them, how to keep a register, how to measure engagement, and how to rank people by power and interest. Let's finish with the idea that ties it all together.

Stakeholder management is never a one-time job. Like most things in project management, it is continuous. Once you have identified your stakeholders and built good strategies to influence them, you must keep watching. Monitor your activities and check that what is really happening matches what you expected to happen.

And stay alert for change. New stakeholders can appear at any moment. Existing stakeholders can move between categories — a quiet, low-interest person can suddenly become powerful and very interested. When that happens, you adjust your plans.

So think of stakeholder management as a loop, not a straight line. You identify, you analyse power and interest, you plan engagement, you communicate and influence honestly — and then you check the results and start again. Round and round, through the whole project.

Do this well, and you do more than deliver on cost, time, and quality. You deliver a project that people feel part of, support, and call a real success. That is professional project management.
steps.example: Halfway through, a junior clerk you barely noticed is promoted to department head. Suddenly she has high power. You update your grid and start managing her much more closely.
keyPoints: Stakeholder management is continuous, not a one-time task.
Keep checking that reality matches what you expected.
Watch for new stakeholders and changes in existing ones.
It is a loop: identify, analyse, plan, influence, review, repeat.

title: Leading People
intro: This module is about leading people on a project. Projects are done by people, not robots. So your success depends on how well you get, keep, and grow the right team. You will learn the five steps of people management (the "Five R's"), what really motivates people, how teams grow through stages, how to handle conflict, and the difference between managing and leading. The tone is simple and practical, based on Sean Whitaker's "The Professional Project Manager."

steps.title: Why leading people matters
steps.body:
Projects are not planned and finished by robots. They are done by people. So managing people well is one of the biggest keys to project success.

The skills for leading people are often called "soft skills." Soft skills are about people, feelings, and communication. For many people, especially those from a technical background like engineering or IT, these are the hardest skills to learn. But they are also the most important if you want to lead and work with the people on your project.

Good people management means being proactive, not reactive. Proactive means you plan and act before problems happen. Reactive means you only respond after things go wrong. A proactive manager thinks ahead: who do I need, what will they do, and how will I keep them happy?

Just like technical skills, management and leadership skills can be learned. You study them, you practice them, and you learn from your successes and mistakes. The more you do this, the better leader you become.
steps.example: A new project manager who was once an engineer keeps fixing code himself instead of guiding his team. He must learn to let go of the technical work and focus on leading people.
keyPoints: People, not robots, complete projects.
Soft skills (people skills) are hard but very important.
Be proactive: plan ahead instead of reacting to problems.
Leadership can be learned through study and practice.

steps.title: The Five R's of managing people
steps.body:
To manage people on a project well, you need a human resources plan. "Human resources" simply means the people who do the work. This plan is your checklist and guide. It can be short for a small project or detailed for a large one. Even just thinking about who you need and when already makes you a better manager.

A good people plan should cover the "Five R's." These are five steps that follow the life of a team member on your project:

1. Recognize the skills, experience, and qualities you want.
2. Recruit the right people at the right time.
3. Reward them in a way that fits them.
4. Retain them by helping them grow.
5. Release them when their work on the project is done.

Because projects have a clear start and end, people join and later leave. The Five R's help you handle this whole journey with care. In the next steps, we will look at each R more closely.
steps.example: Before a software project starts, the manager writes a simple plan: 'I need two testers in month three, and I will let them go in month six when testing ends.' That plan already covers Recognize, Recruit, and Release.
keyPoints: A human resources plan guides how you manage people.
The Five R's: Recognize, Recruit, Reward, Retain, Release.
The plan can be big or small, but always think ahead.
Projects have an end, so people will join and leave.

steps.title: Recognize and recruit the right people
steps.body:
Managing team members begins before they even start work. It starts with clear decisions about who you need.

Recognizing who you want is an active process. You decide, on purpose and fairly, what skills, experience, and attitude you want in a person. The clearer your project scope is, the better you will know the skills you need. "Scope" means the full list of work the project must do. Sometimes you cannot know all the skills you need until the scope is fully defined, so this step can repeat.

Getting the right people often takes a long time, so start early. A poor, rushed choice about who you want can cause big problems later.

Recruiting means figuring out how to get those people. Will you find them inside your own organization, advertise online, or search for a specific expert (called "headhunting")? You also decide the interview process, whether they will be an employee or a contractor, how much you pay, and how you will review their work. Once you write all this down, you have a clear plan of action to get your people.
steps.example: A manager needs a data expert. She decides the exact skills first, then chooses to headhunt one specialist rather than advertise, because good data experts are rare and take time to find.
keyPoints: Recognize means decide on purpose what skills and attitude you want.
A clear project scope helps you know the skills you need.
Start early, because good people take time to find.
Recruiting means planning exactly how you will get them.

steps.title: Reward: money is not everything
steps.body:
Getting the right people is only half the job. The next step is keeping them, so they work for you and not for a competitor. This means rewarding them in the right way.

Rewarding people is not only about money. You would be surprised how many people work for other reasons. People work for pride, friendship, excitement, learning, self-satisfaction, and to make a positive difference. Just think about all the people who volunteer for free in their spare time. For highly skilled workers, money is often not the main reason they choose a job. In fact, once income passes a certain level, money becomes a weaker motivator.

So, besides money, you should find out what else drives each person. Maybe they want more freedom to decide how they work (called "autonomy"), flexible hours, a clear career path, a mentor, or a nicer place to work. When you learn what people truly care about, you can reward them in a way that means something to them.

There are famous theories about motivation, such as Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs and Herzberg's Motivation-Hygiene Theory. The author also likes Dan Pink's work on what really drives us.
steps.example: One team member values learning most, so instead of only a pay rise, her manager sends her to a training course. She feels valued and stays happy in the job.
keyPoints: Keeping good people is as important as finding them.
Money matters, but it is not the only reward.
People also work for pride, learning, freedom, and friendship.
Find each person's real driver and reward that too.

steps.title: Quick check: rewards
steps.body: Let's check your understanding of rewarding people.

quiz.question: According to the chapter, what is the best way to reward highly skilled people?
options: Always give more money, because money is the only real motivator
Find out what each person truly cares about and reward that, in addition to money
Give everyone exactly the same reward to be fair
Wait until they ask for a reward before giving one
quiz.explanation: The chapter says money matters but is not the only driver, so you should learn what each person values and reward that too.

steps.title: Retain: keep your people engaged
steps.body:
Retaining means keeping your team members on the project and keeping them interested in the work. This is very important for long-term success.

Here is a simple truth: if you think someone is a great hire, other companies probably think so too. Also, losing a person and finding a replacement costs more money and time than keeping the person you already have. So retention saves you trouble.

Retention is bigger than rewards alone. It means thinking about what matters to each person as an individual. Remember, one person's reason to stay may be another person's reason to leave. So you cannot treat everyone the same.

To keep people engaged, think about the work environment, the culture of the organization, chances to learn and grow, and how much freedom people have. Project people are usually skilled and smart, and they respond well when you clearly care about these things. The result is lower staff turnover, which means fewer people leaving.

A good time to talk about these things is during regular performance reviews. A performance review is a meeting to discuss someone's work and give feedback. Many people forget to do reviews on projects, but they are a great chance to hear people's hopes and plans.
steps.example: During a review, a manager learns that a developer feels bored. Together they plan a harder, more exciting task. The developer feels heard and decides to stay on the project.
keyPoints: Retaining people saves money and time versus replacing them.
One person's reason to stay may be another's reason to leave.
Focus on environment, culture, growth, and freedom.
Use regular performance reviews to give feedback and listen.

steps.title: Teach it back: why keep people?
steps.body: In your own simple words, explain to a friend why retaining (keeping) team members is better than losing them and hiring new ones. Try to include at least two ideas: the cost of replacing someone, and one thing besides money that keeps people engaged. Imagine your friend has never managed a team before, so keep it clear and short.

steps.title: Release people with care
steps.body:
Every project has a defined end. Each task also has an end. So sooner or later, each person will have no more work to do. Even your own role as project manager will end one day.

Releasing people means letting them leave the project. You must plan for this and handle it proactively, so the team stays stable. If you never admit that a role will end, and you do not help people move to their next role, two bad things can happen. Some people may "jump ship" (leave early) before their work is done. Others may hang around after their job is finished, and keep charging you money for no real work.

For some people, leaving is stressful. For others, it is an exciting new chance. Your remaining team members will judge you by how kindly and thoughtfully you release people. There may also be contract and money issues to settle.

You can help by looking for other work for the person, maybe in your organization or on your next project. You can tell other project managers when they will be free and what skills they have. When someone does leave, say goodbye properly. If you just let people disappear with no thanks, the team members who stay will feel worried and less valued.
steps.example: When a tester finishes her part, the manager thanks her in front of the team and recommends her to another project manager. The rest of the team sees this and trusts their manager more.
keyPoints: Projects end, so plan for releasing people in advance.
Poor planning causes people to leave early or stay too long.
Help people find their next role where you can.
Say goodbye properly, or remaining team members lose trust.

steps.title: Quick check: releasing people
steps.body: Let's check your understanding of releasing team members.

quiz.question: Why should a project manager say goodbye to a leaving team member in a thoughtful way?
options: Because it is a legal rule in every country
Because the way you release people sends a strong message to the team members who stay
Because it makes the project finish faster
Because leaving people always cause conflict
quiz.explanation: The chapter says remaining team members judge you by how you release people, so a poor goodbye harms trust and morale.

steps.title: What makes a performing team
steps.body:
So far we looked at managing single people. But once you have more than one person, you have a team. Managing a team needs similar skills, but with a different focus: how people work together as one group.

Your goal is to get a group of individuals to work together for the good of the project. A well performing team brings huge rewards. A poorly performing team greatly lowers your chance of success.

A high performing team will:
- Share a sense of common purpose (everyone wants the same goal).
- Make good use of each person's talents and skills.
- Have balanced and shared roles.
- Have a culture that focuses on solving problems.
- Accept different opinions and let people speak freely.

All of this shows up in the team culture. Culture means the shared habits, values, and way of working. You must actively build the culture you want. If you do not, a culture will still appear on its own, and it may not be the one you want.

Some things help teams perform: ten or fewer members, people who join by choice, long service, full-time work, and sitting close together. Teams within speaking distance build trust faster and solve conflict more easily.
steps.example: A manager seats her whole team, including contractors, in one shared room. They talk easily, help each other, and solve small problems on the spot instead of waiting for emails.
keyPoints: A team's focus is how people work together, not just alone.
High performing teams share a purpose and value problem solving.
Actively build the culture you want, or a random one appears.
Small teams sitting close together perform better.

steps.title: Quick check: team culture
steps.body: Let's check your understanding of team culture.

quiz.question: What happens if a project manager does not actively build the team culture they want?
options: The team will have no culture at all
A culture will still form on its own, and it may not be the one you want
The team will automatically become high performing
Team culture only matters for very large teams
quiz.explanation: The chapter warns that if you do not shape the culture, one will still arise in the vacuum, and it may not be the culture you want.

steps.title: How teams grow: the Tuckman stages
steps.body:
Bruce Tuckman created a simple, famous model of how a team grows. It has five stages: forming, storming, norming, performing, and adjourning.

Forming happens when a group meets for the first time or a new person joins. Everyone tries to figure out who the others are, what they share, and what their role will be.

Storming comes soon after. The team must decide its direction, which ideas win, and which ideas are dropped. This stage often has conflict and argument. People may compete for position and power. Storming is normal and even needed, but the main issues must be solved before the team can move on.

Norming is when the team agrees on how it will behave and work together. These shared rules are called "norms." Norming should be the result of a healthy storming stage. If storming issues were not solved, norming is hard.

Performing is when the team works well together with real synergy. "Synergy" means the group achieves more together than the members could alone. This is not permanent; it needs constant care.

Adjourning is the final stage, when the team finishes and breaks up. Plan for it, and say goodbye well.

Remember: these stages are not always neat and in order. A team can slip back into storming at any time.
steps.example: A new team argues in week two about which tool to use (storming). Once they agree on rules for choosing tools (norming), they start working smoothly together (performing).
keyPoints: Five stages: forming, storming, norming, performing, adjourning.
Storming has conflict but is a normal, needed stage.
Solve storming issues, or norming will be difficult.
Teams can slip backward, so keep giving attention.

steps.title: Build it: spot the stages
steps.body: Practice using the Tuckman model. Write down a short team situation you have seen (at work, school, a sports team, or a group project). Then, in one or two simple sentences each, describe what the forming and storming stages looked like for that team. Finally, write one thing a good leader could do to help the team reach the performing stage. Keep it short and use your own words.

steps.title: Managing conflict
steps.body:
Conflict is normal in teams, especially during storming. But harmful conflict must not be ignored.

If you see damaging conflict between team members, it is very important to find the root cause in a proactive way. The "root cause" is the real reason behind the problem, not just the surface argument. Do not look the other way. Do not "sweep it under the carpet," which means hide it and pretend it is not there. Conflict that is hidden does not go away. It grows and "festers," like a wound that gets worse, and it stops your team from doing its best.

So deal with conflict openly and in a professional, calm way. Talk about it directly. If you cannot reach a solution and the problem is getting bigger (this is called "escalating"), think about bringing in outside help, such as a neutral person to guide the talk.

Removing the team member who is causing the conflict should be your very last option. Only do this if the problem truly cannot be solved any other way. Losing a person is costly, so try every fair solution first.
steps.example: Two designers keep clashing. The manager meets them together, discovers the real cause is unclear roles, and fixes it by writing clear responsibilities. The conflict ends without anyone leaving.
keyPoints: Do not hide conflict; it grows worse when ignored.
Find the root cause, the real reason behind the argument.
Deal with it openly, calmly, and professionally.
Removing a person is the last resort, not the first.

steps.title: Management versus leadership
steps.body:
A good project manager needs both management skills and leadership skills. They are different, but they overlap.

Management is about the plan. It means executing the plans, checking progress, and making sure all the required work gets done by the right people. Much of this book focuses on managing: having a plan and following it.

Leadership is about vision. A "vision" is a clear picture of the future you want to reach. Leadership means motivating and inspiring people to move toward that vision. It is the purposeful influencing of followers. "Purposeful" means you act with a clear aim and direction.

The most important part of leadership is the followers. Without followers, there are no leaders. Followers choose to be led. They can set the terms under which they will follow, and if they dislike the leader, they can "revolt," which means refuse to follow. A good leader understands this relationship and learns what followers expect. Being a good follower also means telling the leader what you need from them.

Leadership is also situational. This means the right skills change with the situation. A good leader uses their natural strengths, but also works on their weaknesses over time.
steps.example: A manager makes sure the schedule is followed and tasks are done (management). She also paints an exciting picture of the finished product to inspire her team (leadership).
keyPoints: Management executes the plan and checks the work.
Leadership sets a vision and inspires people toward it.
Without followers there are no leaders.
Leadership is situational: adapt your skills to the situation.

steps.title: Teach it back: manage or lead?
steps.body: Explain the difference between management and leadership to someone new to the idea. Use one simple example for each. For example, describe one thing a project manager does that is 'management' (about the plan and the work) and one thing that is 'leadership' (about vision and inspiring people). Try to also say why a good project manager needs both.

steps.title: Quick check: leadership
steps.body: Let's check your understanding of leadership.

quiz.question: According to the chapter, why are followers so important to leadership?
options: Because followers do all the planning for the leader
Because without followers there are no leaders, and followers choose to be led
Because leaders should always do whatever followers want
Because followers cannot leave once they join
quiz.explanation: The chapter says the most important part of leadership is the followers; without them there are no leaders, and they choose to be led.

steps.title: Integrity, influence, and power
steps.body:
Great leaders are authentic. "Authentic" means real and true, not fake. You cannot pretend to have leadership qualities for long. As a leader, you must be "true timber, not a thin veneer," which means solid all the way through, not just a nice surface hiding something weak.

Why does this matter? Because you will face hard moments with no manual to follow. In those moments, you draw on your core values. Values are the things you hold most important in life. Integrity means having a clear set of values and sticking to them, no matter what. This is how you influence people without simply ordering them around. People follow leaders they trust.

Having integrity can cost you. You may lose a contract by being honest about problems and prices. You may lose friends because you do not share their values. But over time, the long-term benefits are greater than the short-term losses. You build a reputation as someone who can be trusted.

Finally, a word on power. As a leader, you will be given power over others. Use it wisely, for the good of your people. Power has a dark side. Watch closely for small misuses, because people who fall from power almost always start with small abuses.
steps.example: A project manager tells a client the honest, higher price and the real risks. He loses that contract, but the client returns a year later because they trust him, and refers two more clients.
keyPoints: Be authentic: real all the way through, not a fake surface.
Integrity means sticking to your values, no matter what.
Trust and integrity let you influence people without force.
Use power wisely; misuse of power starts with small abuses.

title: Build Your PM Toolkit
intro: This is the final module. It brings the whole course together. You will learn how to take everything you know and build your own practical project management (PM) toolkit — your own methodology, with templates and checklists that fit your projects. You will also learn how to keep growing as a project manager. The ideas come from Chapter 14 of "The Professional Project Manager" by Sean Whitaker. Simple, practical, and yours to keep.

steps.title: Your own toolkit beats a boxed one
steps.body:
Welcome to the last part of the course. Now we put everything together into one thing: your own project management toolkit. In the book, this toolkit is called a "methodology." A methodology is just your set of steps, tools, and templates for running projects.

Many companies buy a ready-made methodology. They pay a fee, send people to training, and put posters on the wall. Then they are surprised when no one uses it. Why? Because it was not built for them. It does not use their words. No one on the team helped make it, so no one feels it is theirs.

The book suggests a better path. Take the time and money you would spend on a boxed product, and build your own instead. Your version fits your company, your industry, and your projects. It lasts longer because your own people made it and believe in it.

This does not mean ready-made products are bad. Many are very good. But your own tailored toolkit will fit you better.
steps.example: A small building firm buys an expensive PM system. Six months later the posters are still up, but every project manager still works their own way. The money was spent, but nothing changed.
keyPoints: A methodology is your toolkit: steps, tools, and templates for projects.
Ready-made systems often fail because the team did not build them.
Your own toolkit fits your company and lasts longer.

steps.title: What exactly is a methodology?
steps.body:
Let us make the word "methodology" clear. The book gives a simple meaning: it is a collection of processes, tools, techniques, and templates for managing projects. A "process" is a repeatable set of steps. A "template" is a blank, ready-made form you fill in. The methodology is simply what you use to deliver your projects.

A good methodology has a few features. It should be professional and repeatable, so it works the same way each time. It should be "discoverable" and documented, which means it is written down and easy to find. And it should be based on good practice, like the PMBOK Guide (a well-known book of PM good practice) or a standard such as ISO21500.

The methodology should also match the size, complexity, and industry of your projects. "Complexity" means how complicated a project is. And it should never stand still. You keep improving it over time. This is called continuous improvement — always making it a little better.
steps.example: A company keeps its risk template, its status report form, and its closure checklist together in one shared folder that every project manager can open. That shared, written-down set is their methodology.
keyPoints: Methodology = processes, tools, techniques, and templates.
It must be written down, easy to find, and repeatable.
Base it on good practice like the PMBOK Guide, and keep improving it.

steps.title: Quick check: what is a methodology?
steps.body: Let us check the last idea before we move on.

quiz.question: In the book, what best describes a project management methodology?
options: A single expensive software program you must buy
A collection of processes, tools, techniques, and templates for managing projects
A one-time training course for the whole team
A poster that explains the company rules
quiz.explanation: A methodology is your collection of processes, tools, techniques, and templates used to deliver projects.

steps.title: Why a methodology matters
steps.body:
Why bother building a toolkit at all? The book gives a clear picture of life without one. Without a methodology, you do projects "by the seat of your pants." This means you make things up as you go. Every project manager does things their own way.

That leads to many problems. Work is slower and less efficient. Team morale drops. You win less repeat business. You can lose money. And your chance of finishing projects well goes down.

Now think about the opposite. With a good methodology, you get the most out of your effort. Project managers and team members have ready processes, templates, and guides to follow. This helps them plan, do the work, and check progress. Program and portfolio managers (people who look after many projects at once) get standard information for their reports. So overall, a methodology means a better chance of project success.

There is one more benefit. Having a methodology, and always improving it, is a sign of maturity. "Maturity" here means how skilled and organized a company is at running projects.
steps.example: Two teams run similar projects. One team invents its own way each time and often forgets steps. The other follows a shared checklist and finishes on time. The checklist team looks far more mature to their boss.
keyPoints: No methodology means slow work, low morale, and lost money.
A methodology gives ready tools that raise the chance of success.
Having and improving one shows project management maturity.

steps.title: Agile and traditional: more alike than you think
steps.body:
When you build your toolkit, you may wonder which style to pick. There are two famous styles, and it can feel confusing.

A "traditional" methodology moves slowly through clear, well-defined stages. It works best when the project is well understood from the start. An "Agile" methodology moves fast through short cycles, called iterations. An iteration is one short round of work. Agile handles projects where the scope keeps changing and there is less certainty.

Here is the key idea from the book. The main difference is just the speed of the iterations and the amount of certainty. Apart from that, the two styles share a lot. Both start a project, both get formal approval, both estimate time and cost, both control changes, and both close the project properly. Both also care about quality, risk, people, buying goods, and good communication.

So do not get stuck on the label. Do not force your toolkit to be "only Agile" or "only traditional." A truly good toolkit borrows the best parts from anywhere on the line between them.
steps.example: An IT team uses fast Agile cycles but also borrows a formal closure checklist from traditional methods. They mix the best of both, instead of choosing only one label.
keyPoints: Traditional = slow, clear stages, high certainty; Agile = fast cycles, more change.
Both styles share the same core steps: start, approve, estimate, control, close.
Mix the best parts from both instead of picking only one label.

steps.title: Teach it back: Agile vs traditional
steps.body: Imagine a coworker says, "Agile and traditional methods are completely different and have nothing in common." In your own simple words, explain why this is not fully true. Name at least two things both styles share, and say what the real main difference is.

steps.title: Build your own, or buy a boxed one?
steps.body:
When you set up your toolkit, you have two main choices. You can build your own, or you can buy a ready-made one "off the shelf."

Building your own has real benefits. You can use knowledge your company already owns. You can match your own culture, or "the way we do things here." And you get buy-in — this means people support it because you asked for their input. The main downside is time and effort. Building your own is not one single event. It takes time, and several rounds, to get it right. You also need a "champion" — a person who commits to seeing it through to the end. Many good efforts die because no one owned them.

Buying a ready-made one also has benefits. It is available right away, and it is already known and tested. But there are drawbacks. People wrongly assume that because it worked for someone else, it will work for them. A boxed product may not fit your culture or industry. And team members may not feel it is theirs, so support can be weak.
steps.example: A company hires a champion, one experienced project manager, to lead building their toolkit over three months. Because one person clearly owns it, the work does not stall halfway.
keyPoints: Own build: fits your culture and wins buy-in, but takes time and needs a champion.
Boxed product: fast and known, but may not fit and can lack team support.
A champion is a person who commits to finishing the work.

steps.title: Quick check: buy or build
steps.body: Think back to the two choices.

quiz.question: According to the book, what is a common drawback of an off-the-shelf methodology?
options: It is always too expensive for any company to buy
It cannot ever be changed or customized
People assume it will fit them, but it may not match their culture, industry, or win team support
It is illegal to use in most countries
quiz.explanation: A boxed methodology may not reflect your culture or industry, and team members may not feel it is theirs, so support is weak.

steps.title: Tailoring: making it fit your project
steps.body:
Here is one of the most important words in this chapter: tailoring. Tailoring means choosing only the tools and steps that are useful for a project. One size does not fit all. Your toolkit will not fit my projects, and it may not even fit all of your own projects.

The book describes three stages of tailoring. The first stage is the big one you do once. You look at a body of knowledge, like the PMBOK Guide, and pick the processes and tools that fit the kinds of projects you run. This gives you a flexible, scalable toolkit. "Scalable" means you can make it bigger or smaller as needed.

The second stage happens before each project starts. Here the project manager and the PMO (the Project Management Office, the team that owns the methodology) decide which parts to use for this one project. An easy way is to sort projects into small, medium, and large, with a different set of tools for each.

The third stage happens during the project. You keep checking that you are not doing too much or too little. Tailoring is not a one-time act; it repeats through the whole project.
steps.example: For a small one-week job, a team uses only a short charter and a simple checklist. For a large year-long job, they use full plans and detailed reports. Same toolkit, tailored to size.
keyPoints: Tailoring = using only the tools that help this project.
Stage 1: build the toolkit once; Stage 2: pick tools before a project; Stage 3: adjust during it.
Sorting projects into small, medium, and large is an easy way to tailor.

steps.title: Signs of good and bad tailoring
steps.body:
How do you know if your tailoring is working? The book lists warning signs. If your toolkit is not tailored well, you will notice these things: team members are not using it; team members quietly change it on their own; the toolkit has "process for the sake of process," meaning steps that add no real value; and it uses a one-size-fits-all approach for projects of very different sizes.

These are red flags. If people ignore the toolkit or invent their own versions, that tells you it does not fit their real needs.

Now the good news. When tailoring is done well, you get clear benefits. It creates buy-in, so people actually use it. It keeps a focus on the customer. It keeps a "best-for-project" focus, meaning you do what is right for each project, not what is easy. And it uses your resources more efficiently. Resources are your people, time, and money.

Remember: if you do not give people a well-tailored toolkit, they will make their own anyway. So it is worth doing well.
steps.example: A manager notices two teams have secretly created their own versions of the status report. That is a warning sign: the official template does not fit their real work.
keyPoints: Warning signs: people ignore it, change it secretly, or it adds pointless steps.
Good tailoring creates buy-in and focuses on what is best for each project.
If you do not tailor well, people will build their own version anyway.

steps.title: Teach it back: spotting bad tailoring
steps.body: A new project manager asks you: "How can I tell if our methodology is tailored badly?" In your own words, describe two warning signs from the chapter, and explain one benefit the team would gain if the tailoring were fixed.

steps.title: Building your own toolkit: first steps
steps.body:
The book says building your own methodology is "not rocket science." With some knowledge, patience, and the internet, you can put one together. Here are the first steps.

Step one: check your maturity. Look at how good your company is at projects now, and where you want to be. This gives you a starting point, or benchmark, to measure future progress against.

Step two: gather what you already have. Collect all your existing templates, software, processes, and guides. Make a list, or inventory, of everything. This stops you from "reinventing the wheel" — that means making again something you already own. If you find two templates that do the same job, you will later pick the best one, or build a new one from the best parts of each.

Step three: map the process with your team. Get your team members together and draw out how projects should flow, from start to finish. Note the milestones (key points or events), the documents, and the main steps at each stage. Bringing the team in early gets their ideas and their commitment.
steps.example: Before making anything new, a team lists every form they already use and finds three different risk logs. Now they can merge them into one good template instead of building a fourth.
keyPoints: Step 1: check your current maturity to set a starting point.
Step 2: list what you already have so you do not reinvent the wheel.
Step 3: map the project flow together with your team.

steps.title: Building your own toolkit: finishing it
steps.body:
Now let us finish the steps for building your toolkit.

Step four: fill in the blanks. See what is missing, then find free templates, guides, and process descriptions online to complete the gaps. You do not have to build everything from zero.

Step five: build it slowly, round by round. Use your team to write a process flow chart, user guides, and standard templates. Do not try to build everything at once. Focus on the most important parts first. Pick a simple frame to organize it, like the Plan-Do-Check-Act cycle or the PMBOK process groups (initiating, planning, executing, monitoring and controlling, and closing). Store everything where everyone can find it. The book warns that some companies hide their methodology so well that no one knows it exists.

Step six: use it, and watch how it works. Be ready to change it to make it better.

Step seven: run audits. An audit is a check to see if people follow the toolkit as planned. Audits show where you can improve.

One last point: respect the role of time. You will not finish it overnight. Do the most urgent parts first, and keep delivering real projects while you build.
steps.example: A team builds only the "start a project" template first, uses it on three real projects, then adds the "close a project" checklist the next month. Small steps, one at a time.
keyPoints: Fill gaps with free online templates; build slowly and focus on key parts first.
Store the toolkit where everyone can find it, then use it and run audits.
It takes time; do the urgent parts first and keep improving.

steps.title: A ready-made process flow: P1 to P7
steps.body:
The book shares a sample process flow chart as a starting point. It has seven labeled parts. You can borrow these and change them to fit you.

P1 Initiate the Project: pick and start the project. It may include a business case (a document that justifies the project) and ends with a signed project charter (formal approval).

P2 Plan the Project: do all the planning. It ends with a kick-off meeting, held with stakeholders once enough planning is done to begin work.

P3 Execute the Plan: do the actual work, always guided by a planning document, and check that the work matches the plan.

P4 Control Changes: assess and approve any changes through a proper change control process. Changes loop back to update the plans.

P5 Report Progress: report how the project is going. It is shown on its own to stress how important it is.

P6 Close the Project: do closure tasks, guided by a closure checklist.

P7 Post Implementation Review: after the project, check whether the promised benefits were really achieved, and record lessons learned for next time.

The book says: use this as a guide only. Draw your own version, and put it where everyone can see it.
steps.example: A team labels every live project by stage (P1 to P7) on a shared board. At a glance, the boss can see one project is in P3 (doing the work) and another in P6 (closing down).
keyPoints: The sample flow has seven parts, from P1 (initiate) to P7 (post-review).
Key milestones: signed charter (end of P1) and kick-off meeting (end of P2).
Treat it as a starting point and make it visible to everyone.

steps.title: Quick check: the process flow
steps.body: Recall the seven-part sample flow.

quiz.question: In the sample flow, what is the purpose of P7, the Post Implementation Review?
options: To pick which project to start next
To check if the project's intended benefits were achieved and record lessons learned
To hold the kick-off meeting with stakeholders
To assess and approve change requests
quiz.explanation: P7 revisits the project's intended outcomes and benefits to see if they were achieved, and captures lessons learned for future projects.

steps.title: Build a mini toolkit for your projects
steps.body:
Time to make something real and small. Using the chapter's ideas, sketch a simple starter toolkit for a type of project you know.

Do these four things:

1) Write your project flow in 5 to 7 steps, using the P1-to-P7 idea as a guide. Use your own words for each step.

2) From the book's checklist of elements (for example: Charter Approval, Scope Definition, Risk Management, Communications, Reporting, Change Control, Project Closure, Lessons Learned), pick the 5 elements that matter most for your projects. For each, write whether you need a template, a process, or a user guide.

3) Choose how you will tailor: name one thing you would do for a SMALL project and one thing you would do for a LARGE project.

4) Name one champion (a real role or person) who would own this toolkit and keep improving it.

Keep it short — half a page is plenty. The goal is to practice choosing what fits, not to build everything at once.
keyPoints: Write a 5-7 step flow in your own words.
Pick 5 key elements and say if each needs a template, process, or guide.
Show how you would tailor for small vs large projects, and name a champion.

steps.title: Project management is change management
steps.body:
The book shares a strong idea: to be a better project manager, become a better change manager. Why? Because every project delivers something new. New things mean change, and most people naturally resist change. Change can feel scary.

The chapter gives simple steps to manage change well. First, accept that people dislike change and will resist it if it is not handled with care. Use your leadership skills to guide them through the uncertainty.

Second, create a clear reason to change. If people do not see why the project matters, they will not support it. Communicate the reason often. Where there is silence, rumor and gossip take over.

Third, create the ability to change. Get the right people on board, with the right skills and support.

Fourth, carry out the change as planned: first plan, then do.

Fifth, embed the change. Just doing the work does not make the change last. Build a plan to make it stick, create champions, and run reviews afterward. Good project management and good change management go hand in hand.
steps.example: Before rolling out a new toolkit, a manager explains clearly why it helps and repeats the message in every team meeting. Because people understand the reason, they resist far less.
keyPoints: Every project brings change, and people naturally resist change.
Give a clear reason, communicate often, and plan before you do.
Embed the change with champions and reviews so it lasts.

steps.title: Keep growing as a project manager
steps.body:
You have reached the end of the course. Well done for getting here.

The book ends with a gentle reminder. It was never meant to be a deep textbook that covers every topic fully. Instead, it gives an overview of the whole profession. Where a topic matters to you, use the book as a guide to learn more on your own.

The author gives one key message about being professional. It is not about using every tool you know. The trick is to know which tools to use, which ones not to use, and to understand why you chose them. Being professional also means staying flexible through the whole project. You keep checking your choices. You are humble enough to admit when you did too much or too little, and then you adjust to raise the chance of success.

Finally, the book asks you to do three things: commit to being a professional project manager, keep learning, and keep teaching others. Growth never really stops. Your toolkit will keep changing as you and your projects change. That is exactly how it should be.
steps.example: An experienced project manager drops a heavy risk process from a tiny project because it added no value — and can clearly explain why. That choice, not using every tool, is what makes them professional.
keyPoints: Being professional means choosing the right tools and knowing why.
Stay flexible, review your choices, and adjust when you did too much or too little.
Commit to being professional, keep learning, and keep teaching others.
